title: refs v2: intent-based identity (sigil/markdown) for cross-project reference detection
description:
## Why

`bt robot refs --global` v1 (shipped via bt-mhwy.3, 2026-04-21) detects cross-project bead references in prose and dep fields. Even with two filters (cross-project only + prefix-scoping), the broken-flag false-positive rate on the real shared Dolt corpus is ~30% вҖ” residual comes from placeholder text like `bt-xxx`, `cass-xyz` in design docs and slug-like suffixes under known prefixes (`-only`, `-side`, `-show`, `-level`).

The tool is useful today for stale/orphaned_child (target exists, flag is high-signal), but agents need to filter/dedup broken refs against placeholder noise.

## What

Introduce intent-based identity as a v2 schema bump (`ref.v2`):

1. **Sigil requirement for prose refs**: only emit records when the ref appears with a deliberate marker вҖ” e.g. markdown link `[bt-xyz](...)` or `[bt-xyz](bd://bt-xyz)`, inline code ``` `bt-xyz` ```, or a leading keyword like `ref:bt-xyz` / `see bt-xyz` where "see" is a recognized verb.
2. **Markdown-aware parsing**: actually parse fenced code blocks and inline code вҖ” today we treat all prose as a flat string, which means IDs mentioned inside sample JSON or command examples fire as refs.
3. **Keep `external:` dep scanning as-is**: the `external:` prefix is itself an unambiguous sigil.

This is the same class of move bt-gkyn proposes for pairs (intent-based identity instead of suffix-matching).

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bt robot refs --global` with v2 emits вүӨ5% false positives on the broken flag across the shared corpus
- `ref.v2` schema is a strict superset of `ref.v1` (new `confidence` field? explicit sigil type?) or a clean replacement with migration notes
- v1 remains available as `--schema=v1` flag for at least one release (agents can pin)
- Markdown fenced code blocks are excluded from scanning
- Keyword-based detection (`see bt-x`, `ref:bt-x`, `paired with bt-x`) has a documented verb list

## Relates

- bt-mhwy.3 (v1 shipped)
- bt-gkyn (pairs v2, same class of problem)

## Notes

Filed 2026-04-21 as part of bt-mhwy.3 close. Smoke numbers from v1: 408 total refs, 119 broken (~30% FP), 116 stale, 21 orphaned_child.

title: Pairs v2: intent-based identity rule; suffix-only detection produces ~5Г— false positives
description:
## Why

bt-mhwy.2 shipped `bt robot pairs --global` with a structural identity rule: issues pair when they share an ID suffix across distinct ID prefixes. Dogfooding against the real shared Dolt server (29 pairs detected) revealed the rule produces ~5Г— more false positives than true pairs.

**False positives (suffix collisions):**
- `bt-153` "K-shortest critical paths" вҶ” `mkt-153` "Rich description nudge hook" вҖ” unrelated work, same auto-generated suffix
- `cnvs-1hk` вҶ” `cass-1hk` вҖ” completely unrelated issues
- `dotfiles-4r9` вҶ” `tpane-4r9` вҖ” different work that happened to collide

**True positives:**
- `bt-zsy8` + `bd-zsy8` (the memory-documented paired example)
- `bt-byk` + `cass-byk` + `tpane-byk` + `cnvs-byk` (Gastown 4-way)
- `dotfiles-2il` + `mkt-2il` (dogfooding memory example)

24 of 29 pairs fire `title` drift because the dominant case is suffix collision between unrelated work. `title` drift as a flag carries no signal in practice.

## What

Narrow pair identity to intent-based detection, with `title` drift reconsidered:

1. **Identity**: require a metadata marker (`paired_with: <id>` or equivalent) on at least one member, set when the user runs `bd create --id=<prefix>-<suffix>`. Only beads with explicit paired intent surface.
2. **Alternative**: if a marker isn't feasible, require **both** same-suffix AND identical title (or вүҘN-char title overlap) to filter collisions. More brittle; marker is better.
3. **Title drift flag**: remove or demote once identity is intent-based вҖ” with real pairs, title drift becomes the noise case rather than the signal.

## Scope

- In: identity-rule change, drift-flag list review, fixture updates, version bump to `pair.v2` if shape changes.
- In: coordinate with upstream beads on `--id` marker semantics if we need a new field.
- Out: changing the projection shape beyond `drift` list вҖ” still one record per paired set.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bt robot pairs --global` on the real shared server returns only intentional pairs (currently ~5 of 29).
- Zero false positives from suffix collisions between unrelated work.
- Drift flags carry actionable signal (not "title differs" for every pair).
- `pair.v1` вҶ’ `pair.v2` if identity semantics change meaning.

## Relates

- bt-mhwy.2 (shipped вҖ” this is the v2 follow-up)
- bt-mhwy (epic)
- feedback_cross_project_bead_pairing.md (workflow memory)

## Notes

Dogfood data collected 2026-04-20 on real shared Dolt server: 29 detected pairs, ~24 title-drift fires, ~5 intentional pairs. User identified the issue in session; filing as v2 follow-up rather than reopening .2.

close_reason:
Summary: Phase 2 shipped pair.v2 reader with BFS intent-based detection. v2 narrows identity from suffix-match-alone (v1, ~5x FPR on real corpus) to suffix-match + cross-prefix dep edge. Real corpus: v2 emits 22 records (down from v1's 33); all 11 suffix-only collisions correctly dropped.

Change: Added PairRecordV2 + PairRecordSchemaV2 + ComputePairRecordsV2 (BFS over connected components) + buildPairRecordV2 + computeDriftV2 in pkg/view/pair_record.go. Dropped 'title' from v2 drift enum (no-signal). Every record carries intent_source: 'dep'. Wired v2 dispatch in cmd/bt/robot_pairs.go via pairsOutputV2 / emitPairsV2; runPairs switches on schema. Added pairV2FixturePrefix to pkg/view/projections_test.go with new TestPairRecordV2Golden harness; v1 harness excludes pair_v2_* entries.

Files:
- pkg/view/pair_record.go (+212 LOC)
- pkg/view/pair_record_test.go (+14 unit tests)
- pkg/view/projections_test.go (v2 harness + schema existence test)
- cmd/bt/robot_pairs.go (v2 dispatch + pairsOutputV2)
- cmd/bt/robot_pairs_test.go (3 contract tests)
- pkg/view/testdata/fixtures/pair_v2_{empty,single_in_sync,single_drifted,multi_way_connected}.json
- pkg/view/testdata/golden/pair_v2_*.json

Verify: go build + go test ./pkg/view/... ./cmd/bt/... all pass. Real-corpus smoke: bt robot pairs --global --schema=v2 emits 22 records with zero collision suffixes; --schema=v1 unchanged at 33; default still routes to v1; --orphaned emits 11; bt robot schema --command=robot-pairs advertises both pair.v1 and pair.v2 in enum. Invariant v1 == v2 + orphaned holds (33 == 22 + 11).

Risk: Low. v1 code path untouched and test-covered. v2 is opt-in via explicit --schema=v2 or BT_OUTPUT_SCHEMA=v2 вҖ” default remains v1 so existing consumers see no behavior change. Algorithmic risk bounded by the 14 unit tests + 4 golden fixtures + real-corpus math.

Notes: robotSchemaDefault intentionally stays v1 this phase. Flipping it would break bt robot refs --global default invocations (refs subcommand still has the Phase 3 stub for --schema=v2). Phase 3 (bt-vxu9) ships ref.v2 + sigil detector and flips the default for both subcommands together. Commit: 4543db92. Phase 2 closes cleanly; Phase 3 is the next sequential step.

title: Read missing columns in bt's Dolt readers (upstream schema catchup)
description:
## Why

bt's Dolt readers query a fixed column list via `IssuesColumns` plus separate queries for labels/deps/comments. Upstream beads adds columns regularly; bt has drifted at least 2 columns behind (`metadata`, `closed_by_session`). The schema-drift audit (blocker) identifies the full gap. This bead implements the catchup.

Specifically unblocks bt-mhwy.1's aggressive-path session-id surfacing: bt cannot read `metadata.created_by_session` / `metadata.claimed_by_session` if the `metadata` JSON column is not loaded at all. Also unblocks bt-mhwy.6 provenance surfacing for the same reason.

## What

- Scope is set by schema-drift audit output (this bead is blocked by it)
- Update `internal/datasource/columns.go` `IssuesColumns` to include all missing columns identified in the audit
- Update scan in `internal/datasource/dolt.go` `LoadIssuesFiltered`
- Update scan in `internal/datasource/global_dolt.go` `scanIssue`
- Update `pkg/model/types.go` `Issue` struct to include corresponding fields вҖ” at minimum `Metadata map[string]json.RawMessage` and `ClosedBySession string`, plus anything else the audit surfaces
- Backwards compatibility: gracefully handle older Dolt schemas that lack newer columns (conditional SELECT on schema version, or defensive scan with sql.NullString where applicable)

## Scope

- Out: redesigning the reader вҖ” add columns following existing patterns, do not restructure
- Out: exposing the new fields through robot mode (bt-mhwy.1 owns the CompactIssue schema)
- Out: non-issue-family tables (gates, molecules) вҖ” separate audit/bead if needed

## Acceptance Criteria

- All columns identified in audit are readable by both `DoltReader` and `GlobalDoltReader`
- `Issue` struct has corresponding typed fields
- `metadata` JSON column is deserialized to a Go-native map
- Backwards compat: bt against an older Dolt schema does not crash; missing columns handled gracefully
- All existing tests pass; new tests cover metadata parsing + missing-column fallback

## Relates

- bt-mhwy.1 (unblocks aggressive-path session fields in CompactIssue v1)
- bt-mhwy.6 (unblocks metadata access for provenance surfacing)
- Upstream bd-34v (when first-class session columns ship, this bead or a follow-up reads them directly)
close_reason:
## Summary

Landed Tier 1 of the schema-drift audit: bt now reads upstream beads issues.metadata (JSON) and issues.closed_by_session. Deferred Tiers 2 and 3 (22 more columns) вҖ” no bt consumer today; audit report captures the scope for future work.

## Change

- IssuesColumns gains metadata + closed_by_session.
- Both Dolt scans (LoadIssuesFiltered, scanGlobalIssue) extended to match.
- New parseIssueMetadata helper does defensive json.Unmarshal (nil on empty/invalid).
- Issue struct gains Metadata map[string]json.RawMessage + ClosedBySession string. Clone() deep-copies the map.
- Unit tests pin parser contract (null, empty, empty-object, populated, invalid).

## Files

- internal/datasource/columns.go
- internal/datasource/dolt.go
- internal/datasource/global_dolt.go
- internal/datasource/metadata_parse_test.go (new)
- pkg/model/types.go
- docs/plans/2026-04-20-bt-mhwy-1-execution.md (new, execution handoff)

## Verify

- go test ./pkg/model/ ./internal/datasource/ вҖ” pass
- go test ./... вҖ” pass except 3 pre-existing pkg/drift failures (confirmed by stash/retest: failing on base commit too, unrelated to this change)
- go install ./cmd/bt/
- Live smoke test: bt robot list --global returns metadata + closed_by_session on real issues. Confirmed mkt-0c7 has closed_by_session; bt-mhwy.0 has metadata.action / metadata.claimed_by_session.

## Risk

- Older Dolt schemas missing these columns will trigger the existing try-full-then-simple fallback in LoadIssuesFiltered. Fallback surfaces minimal columns (id/title/description/status/priority/issue_type/created_at/updated_at). No crash. If a user is on old upstream beads, they lose everything beyond the minimal set вҖ” previous behavior. No regression beyond the same edge case that existed pre-change.
- Tier 2 deferred columns (created_by, owner, spec_id, defer_until, started_at, no_history, pinned, content_hash) may be added later if a bt feature needs them. Audit report lists them explicitly.

## Notes

- Pre-existing pkg/drift test failures (TestCalculatorStalenessWarningAndCritical, TestCalculatorBlockingCascade, TestCheckActionable_DecreaseWarning) are unrelated. Not filed as a new bead here вҖ” separate issue.
- Claimed_by_session bridge worked; bt-mhwy.0 own metadata shows claimed_by_session populated on some updates but empty on a later one. Evidently shell expansion variance вҖ” operational, not a bt bug.
- Unblocks bt-mhwy.1 per design spec CompactIssue v1 session fields.

title: Compact output mode across robot subcommands
description:
## Why

`bt robot list` returns 184KB for 100 issues вҖ” full descriptions, designs, acceptance criteria, notes, comments in a list command. Agents burn their context window on data they don't need. Every downstream robot subcommand (triage, insights, alerts, portfolio, pairs, refs) has the same problem: bodies where an index would suffice. This is the blocker for everything else in the bt-mhwy epic вҖ” cross-project intelligence is useless if the output burns the agent's context window.

## What

- Add `--compact` flag across all robot subcommands that currently emit issue bodies
- Make compact the default; `--full` restores current body-included behavior
- Compact output includes: id, title, status, priority, type, labels, source_repo, key relationship counts (parent id, blockers, deps, children)
- Excludes: description, design, acceptance, notes, comments
- Apply to: `robot list`, `robot triage`, `robot next`, `robot insights`, `robot plan`, `robot priority`, `robot alerts`, `robot search`, `robot suggest`, `robot diff`, `robot drift`, `robot blocker-chain`, `robot impact-network`, `robot causality`, `robot related`, `robot impact`, `robot orphans`
- Leave alone: `robot bql` (user-controlled SELECT), `robot schema`/`docs`/`help`/`recipes` (metadata, not issue data)
- Measure before/after byte size on a representative query set

## Scope

- Out: changing `bd show <id>` behavior (body view is correct there)
- Out: human-readable text mode (separate concern вҖ” bt-lt2h)
- Out: touching the analysis engine itself (output-layer change only)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bt robot list --global` default output drops вүҘ80% of bytes vs current full output on a 100-issue sample
- `--full` flag restores current full-body output exactly
- Compact output round-trips through `jq` and TOON cleanly (no malformed records)
- All 15+ subcommands listed above support both modes

## Relates

- bt-kkql (shipped вҖ” raw data pipe this builds on)
- bt-lt2h (human-readable CLI mode вҖ” complementary concern, separate bead)
close_reason:
## Summary

Shipped compact output mode for `bt robot list` and `bt robot diff`, plus a persistent `--shape` flag family on the `robot` cobra command inherited by every subcommand. Default `bt robot list --global` output dropped from 383KB to 38KB on a 100-issue sample (90% reduction, exceeds the вүҘ80% acceptance target).

## Change

Four layered commits on main:
- 64748fb8 pkg/view.CompactIssue projection + golden harness
- fcfc9bcc robot list bellwether (flag + envelope + compact default)
- 904fc167 robot diff compact projection + shape-flag coverage for 16 other subcommands
- c4d1ab34 CHANGELOG + ADR-002 + robot docs/schema

Flag resolution: --shape=compact|full > --compact/--full alias > BT_OUTPUT_SHAPE env > compact default. New `schema` field on RobotEnvelope is `omitempty`: absent in --full mode (byte-identical to pre-change output), "compact.v1" in compact mode.

Survey finding: only `list` and `diff` actually carry []model.Issue slots. The other 15 subcommands (triage, next, insights, plan, priority, alerts, search, suggest, drift, blocker-chain, impact-network, causality, related, impact, orphans) already use purpose-built wrapper types (Recommendation, PlanItem, NetworkNode, etc.) that are compact-by-construction. They inherit the persistent flag and accept it without errors вҖ” no per-subcommand projection required.

## Files

New:
- pkg/view/doc.go, compact_issue.go (+ test), projections_test.go
- pkg/view/schemas/compact_issue.v1.json
- pkg/view/testdata/fixtures/*.json (5 fixtures)
- pkg/view/testdata/golden/*.json (5 goldens)
- cmd/bt/robot_compact_flag.go (+ test)
- cmd/bt/robot_list_compact_test.go
- cmd/bt/robot_all_subcommands_test.go

Modified:
- cmd/bt/robot_output.go (Schema field on envelope)
- cmd/bt/robot_ctx.go (projectIssues + compactSchema helpers)
- cmd/bt/robot_list.go (compact payload)
- cmd/bt/cobra_robot.go (persistent flags + PreRun resolution)
- cmd/bt/cli_misc.go (runDiffSince compact branch + helpers)
- cmd/bt/robot_graph.go (generateRobotDocs + schema updates)
- CHANGELOG.md, docs/adr/002-stabilize-and-ship.md

## Verify

```bash
go test ./pkg/view/ ./cmd/bt/                        # green (drift pre-existing, bt-5e99)
go install ./cmd/bt/
bt robot list --limit=1 | jq '.schema'               # "compact.v1"
bt robot list --limit=1 --full | jq '.schema'        # null (omitempty)
bt robot list --limit=1 | jq '.issues[0] | keys'     # no description/design/etc.
bt robot list --limit=100 --full | wc -c             # 383225
bt robot list --limit=100 | wc -c                    # 37587  (90% drop)
```

Contract + acceptance tests:
- pkg/view: 7 unit cases + 5 golden fixtures = 12 cases, all green
- cmd/bt/robot_compact_flag_test: 14 flag-resolution cases
- cmd/bt/robot_list_compact_test: 5 contract suites (forbidden keys, --full bodies, alias/env resolution, semantic counts, full-mode key regression)
- cmd/bt/robot_all_subcommands_test: 64 subtests (16 subcommands Г— 4 flag permutations) + diff compact + diff full contract

## Risk

Low for a pre-alpha project with one active user. The `--full` escape hatch is byte-identical to pre-change output: the new envelope `Schema` field is `omitempty`, so full-mode envelopes stay unchanged; the full-mode code path continues to emit `[]model.Issue` / `*analysis.SnapshotDiff` as before.

Schema-version contract (`compact.v1`) commits us to additive-only evolution on the compact shape. Rename/remove/type-change bumps to v2. Drift-without-bump is a red-flag signal in code review (documented in pkg/view/doc.go).

## Notes

- 15 of the 16 "other" subcommands were no-ops for compact projection вҖ” the bead description and design spec both list 17 subcommands as "touched" but the survey showed most already project wrapper types. The spec's "applies ONLY to []Issue slots" language covered this case explicitly.
- Pre-existing failures in pkg/drift/ (TestCalculatorStalenessWarningAndCritical, TestCalculatorBlockingCascade, TestCheckActionable_DecreaseWarning) confirmed unrelated to this change by `git stash && go test ./pkg/drift/`.
- Unblocks bt-mhwy.2, bt-mhwy.3, bt-mhwy.4, bt-mhwy.5, bt-mhwy.6.

title: Cross-project agent intelligence layer: expose bt analysis engine for agent consumption
description:
## The Seed

bt sits on a gold mine it can't yet spend. The shared Dolt server holds every project's issues. The analysis engine - graph centrality, triage scoring, dependency suggestion, duplicate detection, cycle warnings, ETA forecasting, blocker chains, causal analysis, impact networks, temporal diffs, label health, coverage sets, parallel cuts, k-paths - is the most sophisticated issue analysis system in the ecosystem. But it was built for single-project scope, and the robot output layer was built for a human staring at a TUI.

The missing piece: an agent intelligence layer that takes the global data + the analysis engine and exposes cross-project insights in a shape agents can actually consume. Not "bd list but bigger." Not raw JSON dumps. Purpose-built views that only make sense when you can see across all 15+ projects at once.

This is bt's entire reason to exist. bd is the backend. bt is the brain.

## What Agents Need (and Can't Get Today)

### 1. Cross-Project Paired Bead Resolution

When bt-zsy8 and bd-zsy8 exist in different databases, they're the same logical work viewed from two project perspectives. Today, nothing surfaces this. An agent filing a cross-project bead with `bd create --id` creates a paired ID, but no tool can later answer "what are all the paired beads and are they in sync?"

**The data exists**: global mode loads issues from all databases. Same-suffix detection is string matching. Sync status is comparing status/priority/description fields across the pair.

**What to expose**: `bt robot pairs` (or extend `robot list` with `--show-pairs`) - for each paired set, show the canonical bead, its mirrors, and whether they've drifted (one closed while the other is open, priorities diverged, etc.)

**Related**: bt-6cfg (TUI linking), dotfiles-dth (global mirroring rule)

### 2. Cross-Project Dependency Graph

`external:cass:search-quality` deps span project boundaries. The analysis engine has graph centrality, articulation points, cycle detection, blocker chains, critical path, PageRank, k-core decomposition, HITS, eigenvector centrality - none of which currently cross project boundaries.

**The data exists**: global mode loads all deps. `external:` prefix deps reference other projects. The graph engine accepts any `[]model.Issue` slice - it doesn't know or care about project boundaries.

**What to expose**: `bt robot insights --global` already works but treats cross-project deps as dangling references. The fix is resolving external deps against the global issue set before running analysis. Then the existing `robot graph`, `robot blocker-chain`, `robot impact-network`, `robot causality` all work cross-project for free.

**What's new**: Cross-project articulation points ("if cass-xyz is blocked, it cascades into bt and beads"), cross-project critical path, org-level bottleneck detection.

**Related**: bt-ph1z.5 (cross-project dep graphs), bt-ghbl/bd-la5 (no raw SQL - bt is the sanctioned query layer)

### 3. Portfolio Health Scoreboard

Not individual issues - project-level aggregates. "cass has 12 open P0-P1s and velocity is declining. bt has 5 and velocity is steady. beads has 3 and velocity is accelerating."

**The data exists**: velocity is already computed per-project (7/30-day closure counts, trend direction). Label health, blocker ratios, cycle counts - all computed per analysis run.

**What to expose**: `bt robot portfolio` - one object per project with: open/blocked/closed counts, P0-P1 count, velocity trend (arrow + sparkline data), health score, top blocker, stalest issue. This is the agent's "which project needs attention?" query.

**Related**: bt-ph1z (cross-project management gaps), bt-8f34 (project registry)

### 4. Session Provenance Tracking

Which session filed a bead, from which project, by which agent. The `source_repo` field already exists on every issue. But today it's just the database name. There's no "this bead was filed by a cass session while working on cass-ked" metadata.

**The data exists partially**: `source_repo` comes from the Dolt database name. Beads created with `bd create` in a specific project dir get that project's prefix. But the filing session, the agent identity, the originating workspace - those aren't captured.

**What to expose**: For now, surface what we have - `source_repo` in list output, filter by it. Later, when bd adds session provenance fields (bt-2cvx depends on upstream beads support), bt surfaces the richer data.

**Related**: bt-2cvx (session author provenance), dotfiles-jeq (cross-project authorship requirements)

### 5. Stale Cross-Reference Detection

Beads reference other beads by ID in their descriptions, notes, and dependency fields. When the target is in a different project, no single-project tool can validate the reference. Is bd-la5 still open? Does cass-zzi exist? Did bt-koz8 get closed and its children orphaned?

**The data exists**: global mode has every issue. Cross-references are parseable from description text (regex for `prefix-id` patterns) and from dependency fields.

**What to expose**: `bt robot refs --global` - scan all issues for cross-project ID references, resolve each against the global issue set, flag: broken refs (target doesn't exist), stale refs (referencing a closed issue as if it's active), orphaned children (parent closed but children still open in other projects).

**Related**: bt-4jyd (global cross-project audit)

## Compact Output: The Context Window Problem

All of the above is useless if the output burns the agent's context window. Today `bt robot list` returns full issue bodies - descriptions, designs, acceptance criteria, notes, comments. That's `bd show` level detail in a list command. 184KB for 100 issues.

Every robot subcommand needs a compact mode that returns the index (id, title, status, priority, type, labels, source_repo, key relationships) without the body. The body is for `bd show <id>` when the agent decides to drill in. The list is for scanning and deciding what to drill into.

**Approach**: `--compact` flag (or make compact the default and `--full` for the current behavior). Apply across all robot subcommands, not just `robot list`. The triage output, insights output, alerts output - all of them include full issue bodies that agents rarely need in the overview.

## The Analysis Engine: What's Already Built

Capabilities that exist in `pkg/analysis/` today, all operating on `[]model.Issue`:

| Capability | Function | Current Scope | Cross-Project Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Graph centrality (betweenness, PageRank, eigenvector, HITS) | `Analyzer.Analyze()` | Single project | Org-level bottleneck detection |
| Articulation points | `Analyzer.Analyze()` | Single project | Cross-project single-point-of-failure detection |
| Critical path | `Analyzer.Analyze()` | Single project | Org-level delivery path |
| k-core decomposition | `Analyzer.Analyze()` | Single project | Tightly coupled cross-project clusters |
| Cycle detection + break suggestions | `DetectCycleWarnings()` | Single project | Cross-project circular deps |
| Dependency suggestions | `DetectMissingDependencies()` | Single project | Cross-project dep discovery |
| Duplicate detection | `DetectDuplicates()` | Single project | Cross-project duplicate/overlap detection |
| Triage scoring | `ComputeTriageRecommendations()` | Single project | Global priority ranking |
| ETA forecasting | `ComputeETAs()` | Single project | Cross-project delivery forecasting |
| Blocker chain analysis | `robot blocker-chain` | Single project | Cross-project cascade analysis |
| Causal chain analysis | `robot causality` | Single project | Cross-project cause tracing |
| Impact network | `robot impact-network` | Single project | Cross-project impact radius |
| Label health | `ComputeAllLabelHealth()` | Single project | Label health across projects |
| Coverage set (minimum issues to maximize unblocks) | `generateCoverageSet()` | Single project | Org-level "what to work on" |
| Parallel cut (parallelizable work) | `generateParallelCut()` | Single project | Cross-project parallel work identification |
| Top-K set (highest impact issues) | `generateTopKSet()` | Single project | Org-level highest-impact issues |
| Temporal diff | `ComputeIssueDiff()` | Single project | Cross-project change detection |

**Key insight**: The analysis engine already accepts `[]model.Issue`. Global mode already produces a unified `[]model.Issue` from UNION ALL queries. The engine doesn't know about project boundaries. Most of this works cross-project TODAY - it's just not exposed through the robot layer with the right framing and filters.

## Implementation Arc

1. **Compact output mode** - `--compact` across robot subcommands. Unblocks everything else by making output agent-consumable without burning context.
2. **Cross-project paired bead detection** - new `robot pairs` subcommand. Pure string matching on loaded data, no new infrastructure.
3. **Cross-project reference validation** - new `robot refs` subcommand. Regex + lookup against global issue set.
4. **Portfolio health** - new `robot portfolio` subcommand. Aggregate existing per-project metrics.
5. **External dep resolution** - resolve `external:` deps before analysis. Unlocks cross-project graph analysis for all existing robot subcommands.
6. **Provenance surfacing** - expose `source_repo` in compact output, add `--source` filter.

## Existing Robot Subcommands (33 total)

These already exist and produce JSON/TOON output. Most will benefit from compact mode and cross-project dep resolution without any subcommand-level changes:

triage, next, insights, plan, priority, graph, alerts, search, bql, list, history, suggest, diff, metrics, recipes, schema, docs, help, drift, sprint list, sprint show, labels health, labels flow, labels attention, files beads, files hotspots, files relations, correlation explain/confirm/reject/stats, impact, related, blocker-chain, impact-network, causality, orphans, forecast, burndown, capacity, baseline save/info

## Cross-Project Beads (the constellation)

This work connects to a web of existing beads across projects:

- **bt-ushd** (P1 epic): Cross-project beads operating system - this bead's parent
- **bt-6cfg** (P3): Same-ID cross-prefix bead linking in global view
- **bt-ph1z** (P2 epic): Cross-project management gaps
- **bt-ph1z.5** (P3): Cross-project dependency graphs
- **bt-ghbl** (P2): No raw SQL against shared server
- **bt-k9mp** (P2): Cross-project bead filing
- **bt-2cvx** (P2): Session author provenance
- **bt-8f34** (P3): Project registry
- **bt-4jyd** (P2): Global cross-project bead audit
- **bt-kkql** (P1): bt robot list (shipped - the raw data pipe this builds on)
- **bt-lt2h** (P2): Human-readable CLI output mode
- **cass-zzi** (P3): Cross-project bead awareness for cass
- **cass-ked** (P2): Daily digest needs aggregated bead state
- **bd-la5** (P2): Agents must not use raw SQL - bt is the sanctioned alternative
- **bd-4t0** (P2): bd list --json truncation indicator gap
- **dotfiles-dth** (P2): Global mirroring and dependency linking rules
- **dotfiles-jeq** (P2): Cross-project authorship requirements

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Compact output mode (`--compact`) available on robot list and triage subcommands
- [ ] `bt robot pairs --global` detects and reports paired beads (same suffix, different prefix)
- [ ] `bt robot refs --global` validates cross-project ID references, flags broken/stale/orphaned
- [ ] `bt robot portfolio --global` returns per-project health aggregates
- [ ] External dep resolution: `external:project:capability` deps resolved against global issue set before analysis
- [ ] Existing robot subcommands (insights, graph, blocker-chain, etc.) produce meaningful cross-project output when run with `--global`
- [ ] All new output fits within agent context budgets (compact by default, `--full` for bodies)

## Design Principles

1. **Don't rebuild bd** - bd is the single-project CLI. bt adds the cross-project brain. If bd can already do it, bt shouldn't.
2. **Analysis engine is the differentiator** - the 20+ analysis functions in `pkg/analysis/` are bt's unfair advantage. Expose them, don't reimplement them.
3. **Compact by default** - agents scan lists, then drill. Never dump bodies in list views. Reserve full output for show/detail commands.
4. **Progressive disclosure** - `robot list` (flat list) -> `robot portfolio` (aggregates) -> `robot insights --global` (full graph analysis) -> `robot pairs/refs` (cross-project relationships). Each level adds depth.
5. **Draw from bd, don't copy bd** - bd's codebase is one folder over. Study its filter semantics, output conventions, and user ergonomics. Adapt for agent consumption, don't duplicate for human consumption.

## Origin

This bead was born in the session that shipped bt-kkql (robot list). While testing the raw data pipe, we realized the output was unusable - 184KB of JSON dumped to stdout, full issue bodies in a list command, no cross-project intelligence despite sitting on the global data layer. The audit of bt-kkql against the codebase revealed the analysis engine's full capability set, the brainstorm from 2026-04-12 provided the product vision, and the constellation of 17 related beads across 4 projects mapped the problem space. This bead synthesizes all of it into the implementation roadmap for bt's cross-project agent intelligence layer.
notes:
Origin session: shipped bt-kkql (robot list), discovered the gap between raw data pipe and agent-consumable intelligence. This is the roadmap bead - implementation starts with compact output mode, then paired bead detection, then portfolio health, then external dep resolution. The analysis engine already works cross-project, it just needs the right exposure layer.
2026-04-20: Decomposed into 6 children (bt-mhwy.1 through bt-mhwy.6). bt-mhwy.1 (compact output) is the unblocker; .2-.6 blocked on .1. bt-mhwy.5 (external dep resolution) relates to .2/.3/.4 (graph analysis becomes richer after .5 lands). See individual children for full scope and acceptance criteria.
2026-04-20 brainstorm insight (compact output shape): title-signal convention is a cross-project concern. Bead titles should carry scan-dense signal (scope + action + subcommand/identifier) so agents can triage from compact output without drilling in. Currently not mandated in .beads/conventions/reference.md or equivalents in beads/, cass/, dotfiles/, etc. Candidate for paired beads (bt + beads + marketplace/beads-onboard skill template) вҖ” file when ready to act. Discovered during bt-mhwy.1 brainstorm when reviewing what fields agents actually use.
close_reason:
Summary: Cross-project agent intelligence layer shipped in full. bt is now the agent-facing read layer for the shared Dolt server вҖ” graph-aware, cross-project-aware, projection-versioned. 7 of 7 children closed across 2026-04-20 and 2026-04-21.

Change: Built pkg/view/ projection package (compact.v1, portfolio.v1, pair.v1, ref.v1), four new robot subcommands (portfolio, pairs, refs + existing list/triage/etc. upgraded), cross-project dep resolution primitive (rc.analysisIssues + analysis.ResolveExternalDeps), --source persistent filter. Upstream-schema catchup (metadata, closed_by_session, ephemeral/template/mol_type) done in bt-mhwy.0.

Children shipped (implementation order 0 вҶ’ 1 вҶ’ 5 вҶ’ 4 вҶ’ 2 вҶ’ 3 вҶ’ 6):
- bt-mhwy.0: Dolt reader column catchup (metadata + session provenance + mol fields)
- bt-mhwy.1: Compact output shape default across 17 robot subcommands; pkg/view/ projection package with versioned schemas; --shape/--compact/--full persistent flags; pkg/view/compact_issue.v1.json schema committed
- bt-mhwy.5: External dep resolution (external:<project>:<suffix> вҶ’ canonical IDs) before analysis; rc.analysisIssues() composition rule established; analysis.SplitID exported as shared primitive
- bt-mhwy.4: Portfolio subcommand (portfolio.v1) вҖ” per-project health aggregates with velocity trend + composite health + top blocker + stalest issue
- bt-mhwy.2: Pairs subcommand (pair.v1) вҖ” cross-project paired bead detection with canonical/mirror + drift flags (status/priority/closed_open/title)
- bt-mhwy.3: Refs subcommand (ref.v1) вҖ” cross-project reference validation in prose + deps with broken/stale/orphaned_child/cross_project flags
- bt-mhwy.6: --source <project>[,<project>...] persistent robot flag scoping output by ID prefix or SourceRepo

Files: 4 new projections in pkg/view/, 3 new robot handlers in cmd/bt/, 4 committed JSON schemas in pkg/view/schemas/, 4 design docs in docs/design/2026-04-20-bt-mhwy-{1,2,3,4,5}-*.md, filterBySource helper in cmd/bt/helpers.go, --shape and --source flags in cmd/bt/cobra_robot.go, 80+ test cases across unit + golden + contract + flag-matrix surfaces.

Verify: All children close-verified individually. Full test suite PASS excluding pre-existing unrelated failures (bt-5e99 drift, bt-kt7x blocker-chain, bt-eqro e2e). Smoke tests against real shared Dolt server for all four new subcommands succeeded. Schema consumers can pin on envelope.schema field.

Risk: Low per child at close time. Two v2 follow-ups filed (bt-gkyn pairs v2, bt-vxu9 refs v2) for intent-based identity вҖ” the naive-string-pattern identity in v1 has known false-positive rates (~24/29 pairs unintentional suffix collisions; ~30% broken-flag FPR on refs). v1s ship because the high-signal flags (drift on intentional pairs, stale + orphaned_child on refs) are immediately useful; v2s will add sigil/markdown-aware intent detection.

Notes: The epic established pkg/view/ as the permanent home for graph-derived consumer projections with strict conventions (file-per-projection, schema constant, committed JSON schema, golden harness, version bump on non-additive change). The rc.analysisIssues() composition rule (single preprocessing pipeline for global-mode robot subcommands) is now load-bearing вҖ” future cross-project preprocessing composes inside that function, not alongside it. The pure-helper pattern (pairsOutput/refsOutput returning payload without hitting stdout/os.Exit) is mandatory for any --global subcommand because BT_TEST_MODE=1 blocks Dolt discovery вҖ” documented in memory project_bt_test_mode_global_contract.md. 4-month dev arc from fork takeover to shipped agent intelligence layer, with the last 48 hours delivering 5 of 7 children.

comments.author: sms
comments.text:
## Recommended implementation sequencing for remaining children (2026-04-20)

After bt-mhwy.1 shipped, the recommended order for the remaining five children is:

**5 вҶ’ 4 вҶ’ 2 вҶ’ 3 вҶ’ 6**

Rationale:

- **.5 first** (external dep resolution) вҖ” pure plumbing, highest leverage. Unlocks cross-project graph semantics for ~10 existing robot subcommands with zero per-subcommand changes. When .2/.3/.4 land after, their graph-derived counts (children_count, unblocks_count, is_blocked) naturally respect cross-project edges without retrofit.
- **.4 before .2/.3** (portfolio) вҖ” reads `pkg/analysis` output that .5 just made richer (top_blocker becomes cross-project-aware). Consumes improvements, doesn't add new projection complexity.
- **.2 before .3** (pairs) вҖ” pair detection is pure suffix matching on loaded data, simpler surface than refs regex work. Validates the projection pattern in `pkg/view/` (per `.1`'s `CompactIssue` precedent) for the first time on cross-project data.
- **.3 after .2** (refs) вҖ” regex scanning of description/notes/comments is harder work with more false-positive risk. Benefits from having pair detection working first as a sanity check.
- **.6 last** (provenance polish) вҖ” smallest scope, one field addition to compact output plus a `--source` filter. Closes out the epic with a quick polish rather than leading with it.

Not binding вҖ” if a different child becomes urgent (e.g., an agent workflow needs portfolio sooner), skip ahead. But this is the order that maximizes compounding value and minimizes rework.

title: Audit AGENTS.md: 936 lines loading every turn is excessive
description:
## Problem

bt AGENTS.md is 936 lines. This loads every single turn in every session, consuming significant context window. For comparison: marketplace is 109 lines, beads is 189 lines. bt is ~5-9x larger than it should be.

## Why this is P0

Every turn of every session pays this token cost. At 936 lines, that's potentially thousands of tokens per turn that could be instructions, code, or reasoning instead. This directly degrades agent performance in this project.

## Likely causes

- bd setup injected a large beads integration section with boilerplate
- Manual accumulation of conventions, instructions, and workflow docs over time
- Content that belongs in PRIME.md (loaded once at session start) or skills (loaded on demand) is sitting in AGENTS.md (loaded every turn)

## What to do

1. Read the full AGENTS.md and classify every section as:
   - **every-turn essential**: core rules the agent must see constantly (keep in AGENTS.md)
   - **session-start**: context needed once per session (move to .beads/PRIME.md)
   - **on-demand**: detailed guidance invoked when relevant (move to skill references)
2. Move session-start content to per-project .beads/PRIME.md
3. Move on-demand content to skill references or CLAUDE.md
4. Target: under 200 lines for AGENTS.md
5. Preserve the new Workflow Formulas section (just added 2026-04-13) - it's essential

## Connected

- cass-pze: same problem in cass (931 lines). Fix pattern should be identical.
- mkt-dc7: context load optimization bead in marketplace

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] AGENTS.md under 200 lines
- [ ] No content loss - everything moved to appropriate layer (PRIME.md or skills)
- [ ] Workflow Formulas section preserved
- [ ] Verified: fresh session still has all necessary context
close_reason: AGENTS.md trimmed from 936 to 168 lines. Removed verbose tool docs (UBS, RCH, ast-grep, morph warp, cass), Go code examples, dependency tables, MCP Agent Mail, CLI reference tables, and Codex notes. Preserved core rules, project identity, key dirs, build commands, design constraints, naming, robot mode reference, beads integration block, workflow formulas, and issue tracking conventions. Content already covered by .claude/CLAUDE.md or accessible on-demand was removed.

title: Extend data model with gate and molecule columns
description:
## Why
Gate fields (await_type, await_id, timeout) and molecule fields (ephemeral, template, mol_type) are completely absent from bt's data model. This blocks ALL gate/molecule visibility features (P0 items from bd-0il audit).

## What
Extend the Issue struct and SQL loading to include 6 new columns from the beads issues table.

## Files to change
1. **internal/datasource/columns.go:9-14** - append 6 columns to IssuesColumns constant
2. **internal/datasource/dolt.go:79-154** - add null variables, scan parameters, and validity checks
3. **internal/datasource/global_dolt.go:428-436** - update scanGlobalIssue() to match
4. **pkg/model/types.go:8-97** - add 6 pointer fields to Issue struct + update Clone()

## New fields
- AwaitType *string (gate type: human, timer, gh:run, gh:pr, bead)
- AwaitID *string (gate identifier)  
- Timeout *int (gate timeout in seconds)
- Ephemeral *bool (wisp/molecule flag)
- Template *bool (proto template flag)
- MolType *string (molecule type)

## Complexity
LOW (~50 lines, mechanical pattern-following). No behavioral change - pure plumbing.

## Discovered from
bd-0il feature audit, bt-mol-qvjm research-audit molecule
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] IssuesColumns in columns.go includes await_type, await_id, timeout, ephemeral, template, mol_type
- [ ] DoltReader.LoadIssuesFiltered() scans and maps all 6 new columns
- [ ] GlobalDoltReader.scanGlobalIssue() scans and maps all 6 new columns in same order
- [ ] Issue struct has 6 new pointer fields with json tags
- [ ] Issue.Clone() deep-copies all 6 new pointer fields
- [ ] go build ./cmd/bt/ succeeds
- [ ] go test ./internal/datasource/... passes
- [ ] go test ./pkg/model/... passes
- [ ] bt connects to a Dolt database with gate/molecule data and loads without error
notes:
## Verified Dolt Schema (2026-04-13)

Columns on issues table confirmed via DESCRIBE:
- await_type: varchar(32), default ''
- await_id: varchar(255), default ''
- timeout_ns: bigint (NOT timeout), default 0
- ephemeral: tinyint(1) (boolean), default 0
- is_template: tinyint(1) (NOT template), default 0
- mol_type: varchar(32), default ''

Plan assumed: timeout, template - actual names differ.
Gate data IS on issues table, not separate table.
Also found: waiters (text), wisp_type (varchar), work_type (varchar), pinned (tinyint), no_history (tinyint), defer_until (datetime) - out of scope for this bead.
close_reason: Data model extended with 6 gate/molecule columns (await_type, await_id, timeout_ns, ephemeral, is_template, mol_type). Verified against live Dolt schema - plan assumed 'timeout' and 'template' but actual names are 'timeout_ns' (bigint) and 'is_template' (tinyint). All 5 files updated: columns.go, dolt.go, global_dolt.go, sqlite.go, types.go. Clone() deep-copies new pointer fields. Tests added and passing.

title: Clean up MEMORY.md - bloated project wiki violating memory rules
description:
## Problem

bt's MEMORY.md is ~170 lines and being used as a project wiki/state dump. Most content violates the auto-memory rules - it stores things derivable from code, git, or bd commands:

- Full codebase architecture (theme system, panel system, BQL system, Dolt lifecycle details)
- Open/closed beads lists (that's just bd list)
- Code dependency details with file paths and line numbers (read the code)
- Resolved decisions history (that's git log / ADR docs)
- Current state snapshots ('session 17, 92k lines, 431 files') that are stale immediately

MEMORY.md loads into every conversation turn. Lines after 200 get truncated. bt is burning nearly its entire memory budget on stale snapshots.

## What to keep

- Project identity (name, binary, module path, repo, GitHub account)
- Git setup (remotes, commit author, account switching)
- Process preferences (ADR-driven workflow, changelog convention, session protocol)
- Open decisions not captured elsewhere
- Feedback entries (cross-prefix deps guidance, etc.)
- License info

Target: ~30-40 lines.

## What to remove

- Architecture sections (theme, panel, BQL, Dolt lifecycle) - read the code
- Open/closed beads lists - use bd list
- Code dependencies - grep the codebase
- Resolved decisions - in ADR docs and git history
- Session-numbered state snapshots - immediately stale
- Upstream beads context - read beads repo directly

## Why this matters

Every turn of every bt conversation pays the token cost of this bloat. Stale architecture details are worse than no details - they mislead agents into acting on outdated info instead of reading current code.
notes: This is the same problem as dotfiles-tl9 (audit MEMORY.md across all projects). The fix is in global CLAUDE.md conventions, not per-project. Consider closing this in favor of dotfiles-tl9 as the canonical tracker, or link them.
close_reason: Superseded by dotfiles-tl9 which tracks this as a cross-project initiative. The fix is in global CLAUDE.md memory conventions, not per-project cleanup.

title: Data Integrity: Merge Artifact Files Loaded Instead of Canonical Data
description:
## Problem Statement

bv can load stale/outdated issue data when merge artifact files (`beads.left.jsonl`, `beads.base.jsonl`) are present in the `.beads/` directory. Users see closed issues as open, wrong priorities, or missing issues entirely.

## User Impact

**Severity: Critical (P0)**
- Data integrity issue - users see WRONG information
- Particularly affects users after git merge conflicts
- Can lead to working on already-completed issues
- Reported by @bmjjr in GitHub Issue #5, confirmed by @masonjames

## Root Cause Analysis

**Location:** `pkg/loader/loader.go:36-56`

### Problem 1: Merge Artifacts Not Skipped

The `FindJSONLPath()` function skips files containing:
- `.backup`
- `.orig`  
- `.merge`
- `deletions.jsonl`

But it does NOT skip:
- `beads.left.jsonl` - Git merge artifact (OURS side)
- `beads.base.jsonl` - Git merge artifact (BASE side)

### Problem 2: Wrong Priority Order

```go
preferredNames := []string{"beads.jsonl", "beads.base.jsonl", "issues.jsonl"}
```

This prioritizes `beads.base.jsonl` (position #2) OVER `issues.jsonl` (position #3).

Per beads upstream (steveyegge/beads), `issues.jsonl` is the canonical file:
> Stage all tracked JSONL files (issues.jsonl is canonical, beads.jsonl for backward compat)

So the priority order is wrong AND merge artifacts aren't being filtered.

## Scenario That Triggers Bug

1. User has a beads project with `issues.jsonl` up to date
2. Git merge conflict occurs involving `.beads/` files
3. User resolves conflict but leaves `beads.left.jsonl` and `beads.base.jsonl` behind
4. bv loads `beads.base.jsonl` (old data) instead of `issues.jsonl` (current data)
5. User sees stale issue states

## Proposed Solution

### Fix 1: Skip merge artifact patterns

```go
// Skip backups, merge artifacts, and deletion manifests
if strings.Contains(name, ".backup") ||
    strings.Contains(name, ".orig") ||
    strings.Contains(name, ".merge") ||
    strings.HasPrefix(name, "beads.left") ||  // NEW
    strings.HasPrefix(name, "beads.base") ||  // NEW (when issues.jsonl exists)
    name == "deletions.jsonl" {
    continue
}
```

### Fix 2: Correct priority order

```go
// Priority order per beads upstream:
// 1. issues.jsonl (canonical)
// 2. beads.jsonl (backward compatibility)
// 3. beads.base.jsonl (only if nothing else exists)
preferredNames := []string{"issues.jsonl", "beads.jsonl", "beads.base.jsonl"}
```

### Fix 3: Add detection warning

When merge artifacts are detected, emit a warning to stderr:
```
Warning: Merge artifact files detected in .beads/. Run 'bd clean' to remove them.
Found: beads.left.jsonl, beads.base.jsonl
Loading from: issues.jsonl (canonical)
```

## Test Plan

1. Test: Directory with only issues.jsonl - loads correctly
2. Test: Directory with issues.jsonl AND beads.left.jsonl - skips artifact, loads issues.jsonl
3. Test: Directory with beads.jsonl AND issues.jsonl - loads issues.jsonl (new priority)
4. Test: Warning emitted when artifacts detected
5. Test: Fallback to beads.base.jsonl when NO other files exist

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] beads.left.jsonl always skipped
- [ ] beads.base.jsonl skipped when canonical file exists
- [ ] issues.jsonl prioritized over beads.jsonl
- [ ] Warning emitted for detected merge artifacts
- [ ] Existing tests updated, new tests added
- [ ] No regression for projects without merge artifacts

## Workaround (Current)

Users can run `bd clean` or manually delete merge artifact files.

## References

- GitHub Issue #5: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer/issues/5
- beads pre-commit hook: https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/blob/main/.beads-hooks/pre-commit#L38
- Related: PR #18 (openInEditor hardcoded path) depends on this fix
close_reason: Fixed: 1) Skip beads.left/right merge artifacts, 2) Correct priority order (issues.jsonl canonical per beads upstream), 3) Add FindJSONLPathWithWarnings for merge artifact warnings. Tests added and passing.

title: Focus Isolation: List Selection Changes During Detail View Navigation
description:
## Problem Statement

When the detail view panel has focus (split view mode), pressing j/k or arrow keys to scroll the detail content ALSO changes the list selection in the left panel. The list visually updates to show a different selected item, even though the detail content being displayed doesn't change.

## User Impact

**Severity: Critical (P0)**
- Affects ALL users in split view mode
- Creates confusion about which issue is actually selected
- Breaks the mental model of focus isolation
- Reported by @ZainRizvi in GitHub Issue #12 with video evidence

## Root Cause Analysis

**Location:** `pkg/ui/model.go:1116`

```go
// Update list for filtering input, but NOT for WindowSizeMsg
if _, isWindowSize := msg.(tea.WindowSizeMsg); !isWindowSize {
    m.list, cmd = m.list.Update(msg)  // вҶҗ PROBLEM: Called for ALL messages!
    cmds = append(cmds, cmd)
}
```

The bubbles `list.Model` component internally handles j/k keys for navigation. This code passes ALL keyboard messages to the list, regardless of current focus state. When in `focusDetail` mode:

1. `viewport.Update(msg)` is called (line 986) - correctly scrolls detail content
2. `list.Update(msg)` is ALSO called (line 1116) - incorrectly moves list selection

The second call happens because the guard only excludes WindowSizeMsg, not keyboard messages when focus is elsewhere.

## Why Detail Content Doesn't Change

The detail view shows the issue that was selected when `updateViewportContent()` was last called. Since this function is only triggered by explicit list selection changes (not by internal list.Update navigation), the displayed content stays the same even as the list cursor moves.

## Proposed Solution

Gate list updates by focus state. Only forward keyboard messages to the list when `m.focused == focusList` or when the message is a non-navigation type (like filtering input, WindowSizeMsg).

**Option A (Minimal change):**
```go
// Only update list selection when list has focus
if m.focused == focusList {
    if _, isWindowSize := msg.(tea.WindowSizeMsg); !isWindowSize {
        m.list, cmd = m.list.Update(msg)
        cmds = append(cmds, cmd)
    }
}
```

**Option B (More granular):**
Maintain list.Update for non-navigation messages (filtering, search) while blocking j/k/arrows when unfocused.

## Test Plan

1. Create test: Split view with focusDetail, send j/k keys, verify list index unchanged
2. Create test: Split view with focusList, send j/k keys, verify list index changes
3. Create test: Filtering input works regardless of focus
4. Manual test: Verify scroll in detail pane doesn't affect list selection
5. Manual test: Verify Tab switching focus works correctly

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] j/k in detail view scrolls content, doesn't change list selection
- [ ] j/k in list view still navigates as expected
- [ ] Tab correctly switches focus between panels
- [ ] Filter/search input still works from any focus state
- [ ] New tests pass with 100% coverage of focus scenarios

## References

- GitHub Issue #12: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer/issues/12
- Video demonstration in issue shows exact behavior
- Related code: handleListKeys(), focusDetail case at line 985-988
close_reason: Fixed focus isolation bug - gated list.Update on focusList state to prevent j/k keys from affecting list selection when detail view has focus

title: Topological Work-Plan Generator
description:
Leverage the existing DAG analysis to generate linearized, dependency-respecting work plans. This is the highest-value feature because it directly answers the question every developer and AI agent asks: 'What can I actually work on right now?'

## Background & Motivation
The analysis package already computes TopologicalOrder via topo.Sort(). However, this raw ordering isn't directly actionable because:
1. It includes closed issues
2. It doesn't filter to 'leaf nodes' (tasks with no open blockers)
3. It's not exposed via CLI for programmatic access

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: A 'Smart To-Do' view showing only actionable items eliminates cognitive overhead of scanning blocked tasks.
- For AI Agents: --robot-plan enables autonomous workflows where agents can request a strictly ordered task list, preventing work on blocked items.

## Technical Approach
1. Add GetActionableIssues() to analysis package - returns issues where all blocking dependencies are closed
2. Add GetExecutionPlan() - returns topologically sorted actionable items with parallel tracks identified
3. CLI flag --robot-plan outputs JSON with execution order
4. TUI view showing actionable items grouped by parallel tracks

## Success Criteria
- Agent can request plan and receive valid execution order
- Plan respects all blocking dependencies
- Parallel tracks (independent work streams) are identified
- Zero actionable items shown when all open issues are blocked

title: bt robot blocker-chain panics with nil analyzer
description:
## Summary

`bt robot blocker-chain <id>` panics with nil pointer dereference regardless of whether `--global` is set. The field `robotCtx.analyzer` carries a comment `// lazily created if nil` (cmd/bt/robot_ctx.go) but no call site populates it before `runBlockerChain` dereferences it.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. `cd` into any project with beads
2. `bt robot blocker-chain <any-existing-id>`
3. Observe panic

```
panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference
[signal 0xc0000005 code=0x0 addr=0x20 pc=0x...]
goroutine 1 [running]:
github.com/seanmartinsmith/beadstui/pkg/analysis.(*Analyzer).GetBlockerChain(0x0, ...)
    pkg/analysis/graph.go:2496 +0x5d
main.(*robotCtx).runBlockerChain(...)
    cmd/bt/robot_history.go:764 +0x3e
```

## Root cause

`cmd/bt/robot_history.go:764`:
```go
result := rc.analyzer.GetBlockerChain(issueID)
```

`rc.analyzer` is never assigned. It's the only call site that doesn't construct its own `analysis.NewAnalyzer(...)` вҖ” every other robot subcommand builds the analyzer inline. The `lazily created if nil` comment in `robot_ctx.go` is aspirational.

## Fix direction

Either:
1. Build the analyzer inline like every other robot_* subcommand:
   ```go
   analyzer := analysis.NewAnalyzer(rc.analysisIssues())
   _ = analyzer.Analyze()
   result := analyzer.GetBlockerChain(issueID)
   ```
2. Actually implement the "lazy" doc comment вҖ” a helper on `robotCtx` that builds+caches the analyzer on first use.

(1) is the minimal fix and matches established style. (2) is worth doing separately if the analyzer gets reused across multiple paths.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `bt robot blocker-chain <id>` exits 0 and emits a JSON envelope containing `result.Chain`
- [ ] `bt robot blocker-chain --global <id>` follows chains across project prefixes (also requires bt-mhwy.5 to ship)
- [ ] No panic when the id does not exist вҖ” graceful "issue not found"
- [ ] Misleading `lazily created if nil` comment on `robotCtx.analyzer` either removed or backed by an actual lazy-init helper

## Discovery

Encountered while smoke-testing bt-mhwy.5 acceptance criterion `bt robot blocker-chain --global <id>`. Pre-existing; reproduces on HEAD without mhwy.5 changes.

## Files

- `cmd/bt/robot_history.go:764`
- `cmd/bt/robot_ctx.go` (misleading `lazily created if nil` comment on `analyzer` field)

title: Three pkg/drift tests failing on main (stale severity thresholds)
description:
## Bug

Three drift-calculator tests fail on current `main` with no connection to recent changes. Confirmed pre-existing by stashing current work and running the failing tests against the clean base.

```
--- FAIL: TestCalculatorStalenessWarningAndCritical
    drift_test.go:213: expected 2 warning staleness alerts, got 0
--- FAIL: TestCalculatorBlockingCascade
    drift_test.go:250: expected info severity, got warning
--- FAIL: TestCheckActionable_DecreaseWarning
    drift_test.go:811: 35% decrease should be warning, got info
```

## Repro

```
go test ./pkg/drift/ -count=1 -run "TestCalculatorStalenessWarningAndCritical|TestCalculatorBlockingCascade|TestCheckActionable_DecreaseWarning"
```

Fails regardless of working-tree changes. Failing on commit c4de5ceb and earlier; does not depend on bt-mhwy.0.

## Impact

- CI signal is broken - a real regression in pkg/drift would be invisible behind "3 tests always red".
- Every contributor has to manually remember "ignore these 3".
- Tests contradict each other on thresholds (test expects warning at 35 % decrease; code returns info) - likely tests were updated for one severity-curve revision and the code moved on, or vice versa.

## Expected

Either:
- Code wrong -> three real severity bugs in drift calculator. Fix code, tests stay.
- Tests wrong -> severity thresholds were intentionally tuned. Update tests to match current behaviour.

First task of the fix: decide which side of the mismatch is canonical (code vs test).

## Files

- `pkg/drift/drift_test.go:213` (staleness)
- `pkg/drift/drift_test.go:250` (blocking cascade)
- `pkg/drift/drift_test.go:786-813` (decrease-warning)
- `pkg/drift/` Calculator severity logic

## Relates

- bt-5dvl (test-suite audit, different failures)
- bt-tjq0 (test progress visibility)

title: External dep resolution: rewrite external: deps against global set before analysis
description:
## Why

`external:cass:search-quality` deps span project boundaries. bt's analysis engine (graph centrality, articulation points, critical path, blocker chains, causal chains, impact networks, k-core, PageRank, HITS) all operate on `[]model.Issue`. Global mode produces a unified slice. But `external:` deps are treated as dangling references today вҖ” the graph doesn't cross the boundary even with `--global`.

Fixing this is the highest-leverage change in the epic: it unlocks cross-project graph analysis for ~10+ existing robot subcommands with no per-subcommand changes. `robot insights`, `robot graph`, `robot blocker-chain`, `robot impact-network`, `robot causality`, `robot related`, `robot impact`, `robot orphans`, `robot plan`, `robot priority` all get cross-project semantics for free.

## What

In the analysis engine's issue-loading path (or the layer that feeds it):

- Before running analysis, scan each issue's deps for `external:<project>:<identifier>` patterns
- Resolve each against the global issue set вҖ” match by project prefix + identifier
- If target exists: rewrite the dep as a normal blocking edge pointing at the resolved issue (in-memory graph, not persisted to DB)
- If target doesn't exist: log at debug level and drop the edge (don't panic, don't emit a broken edge to the analysis)
- Gate behind `--global` only; single-project analysis leaves external deps untouched

## Scope

- Out: new subcommands (this is plumbing)
- Out: TUI/UI treatment of cross-project edges (that's bt-ph1z.5)
- Out: creating missing external deps or fixing typos (fixup tooling, separate bead)
- Out: persisting resolved edges back to the DB (resolution is in-memory, per-run)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bt robot insights --global` surfaces cross-project articulation points that were invisible before
- `bt robot blocker-chain --global <id>` follows chains across project boundaries
- `bt robot impact-network --global <id>` includes cross-project impact
- `bt robot graph --global` shows cross-project edges in JSON output
- Unresolved `external:` deps logged at debug, not erroring the run
- Single-project mode (no `--global`) behavior unchanged

## Relates

- bt-ph1z.5 (cross-project dep graphs вҖ” complementary, this is the engine fix)
- bt-mhwy.2, bt-mhwy.3, bt-mhwy.4 (graph analysis within those becomes richer after this lands)
close_reason:
Summary: Cross-project external dep resolver lands; --global routes analysis through rewritten edges.

Change: Added pkg/analysis/external_resolution.go (ResolveExternalDeps), pkg/analysis/doc.go (package scope), robotCtx.analysisIssues() helper, and 10 one-line swaps at every robot analysis call site. Resolver rewrites external:<project>:<id> deps to canonical IDs in memory when --global is active; single-project mode is untouched (byte-identical by construction).

Files: pkg/analysis/external_resolution.go, pkg/analysis/external_resolution_test.go, pkg/analysis/testdata/external/, pkg/analysis/doc.go, cmd/bt/robot_ctx.go, cmd/bt/robot_analysis.go, cmd/bt/robot_alerts.go, cmd/bt/robot_sprint.go, cmd/bt/cli_misc.go, cmd/bt/robot_external_resolution_test.go

Verify: go test ./pkg/analysis/ and go test ./cmd/bt/ both green. Unit tests cover nil/empty, rewrite, drop unresolved, drop malformed, immutability, idempotence, structural-equivalence-without-externals, debug log emission. Integration tests confirm single-project identity (reference equality) and global-mode cross-project blocker chain traversal. Smoke-tested bt robot insights --global, impact-network --global, graph --global end-to-end.

Risk: Low. pkg/analysis/ already worked with external: deps as silent-skip. Resolver's only effect in non-global mode is a no-op pass-through (analysisIssues returns rc.issues by identity when !flagGlobal).

Notes: Design doc's call-site inventory listed cli_misc.go:83 (runFeedback) вҖ” that line is not a robot subcommand and not on *robotCtx, so it was skipped; diff path cli_misc.go:433 was added instead to preserve the "ten mechanical swaps" count, matching the design prose about diff-based subcommands under --global. Flagged with user pre-implementation, confirmed "proceed". Discovered unrelated pre-existing nil panic in bt robot blocker-chain: filed bt-kt7x. Pre-existing drift test failures tracked at bt-5e99. Neither caused by this change.

comments.author: sms
comments.text:
## Handoff for implementation session

Design is approved and on disk вҖ” go build it. No re-deliberation.

**Authoritative spec:** `docs/design/2026-04-20-bt-mhwy-5-external-dep-resolution.md` (committed in `5725781f`). Follow it as written.

**Prior art for the `robotCtx` helper pattern:** `docs/design/2026-04-20-bt-mhwy-1-compact-output.md`. This bead adds `rc.analysisIssues()` mirroring `.1`'s `rc.projectIssues()`.

**Decisions already made in design review (do not re-propose):**

- **ID-prefix only.** Dual-convention lookup (ID-prefix AND SourceRepo both resolving) was considered and cut as accidental complexity. SourceRepo is a convention the capability-label system uses, not the dep system.
- **Three-part form only** (`external:<project>:<identifier>`). Two-part form (`external:<id>`) appears in a single test fixture; not real data. If real data surfaces it, follow-up bead.
- **No stats variant.** `ResolveExternalDepsWithStats` was considered and cut as YAGNI. Debug logging covers the diagnostic need.
- **Single commit, not three-commit bellwether.** Every call site is the same one-line swap; no heterogeneity to stage. `.1`'s bellwether pattern was load-bearing because shape-flag integration varied per output type вҖ” that's not the case here.
- **No opt-out flag.** `--global` is the only gate. `BT_RESOLVE_EXTERNALS=false` was considered and cut вҖ” pre-alpha, byte-identical single-project criterion protects users who don't want cross-project semantics.
- **Dep-type-agnostic.** Resolver runs on every `external:` dep regardless of `DependencyType`. `IsGraphEdge()` filters downstream.

**Composition rule for `rc.analysisIssues()`:** future preprocessing (for mhwy.2/3/4) composes inside this helper, not alongside it. One pipeline, not N `rc.Xissues()` helpers.

**Scope:** single PR, single commit. ~200 lines across `pkg/analysis/external_resolution.go` + tests + fixtures + `cmd/bt/robot_ctx.go` helper + ten one-line swaps across `robot_analysis.go`, `robot_alerts.go`, `robot_sprint.go`, `cli_misc.go`.

Claim the bead before starting. If implementation reveals a gap the design doc didn't anticipate, pause and flag rather than adapting silently.

comments.author: sms
comments.text:
## Implementation addendum (post-handoff)

One item from the architecture review that didn't make it into the design doc's file list:

**Update `pkg/analysis/doc.go`** вҖ” if it exists, add a sentence noting that the package now holds both the graph engine and its input-preparation primitives (the resolver being added here). If it doesn't exist, create it with a one-liner to that effect. This prevents the next author from treating `pkg/analysis/` as engine-only and putting a future input-side transformation somewhere else. Small but compounds.

title: robot portfolio subcommand: per-project health aggregates
description:
## Why

No tool today answers "which project needs attention?" at the org level. bd and bt both operate per-project вҖ” you can see bt's health or cass's health, but not rank them side by side. Velocity, blocker ratios, P0-P1 counts, stalest issues all exist per-project already but aren't aggregated into a portfolio view. Agents working across projects need this to prioritize where to intervene.

## What

New `bt robot portfolio --global` subcommand that emits one record per project:

- **counts**: open, blocked, in_progress, closed_30d
- **priority breakdown**: P0 count, P1 count
- **velocity**: closures_7d, closures_30d, trend (up/down/flat)
- **health score**: composite in [0..1] from open/closed ratio, blocker ratio, staleness
- **top_blocker**: highest-centrality open issue (id, title, priority)
- **stalest**: open issue with oldest `updated_at` (id, title, age_days)

Output is one flat array of project records вҖ” caller sorts/filters as needed.

## Scope

- Out: cross-project rankings within the output (caller sorts)
- Out: dashboards or charts (TUI/web territory)
- Out: historical trend beyond 30d window (v1)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bt robot portfolio --global` returns one object per project discovered in global mode
- Velocity calculation matches the per-project convention already used in `robot insights`
- Health score formula documented in `robot docs`
- Compact by default (bt-mhwy.1 must land first)

## Relates

- bt-ph1z (cross-project management gaps вҖ” parent of this concern)
- bt-8f34 (project registry вҖ” feeds authoritative project list)
close_reason: Shipped. New projection (portfolio.v1), handler, golden fixtures, contract tests. Ranks 15 real projects side-by-side under --global. See docs/design/2026-04-20-bt-mhwy-4-portfolio.md and CHANGELOG.md entry for 2026-04-20.

title: robot refs subcommand: cross-project reference validation
description:
## Why

Beads reference other beads by ID in descriptions, notes, comments, and dependency fields. When the target lives in another project, no single-project tool can validate the reference. Is `bd-la5` still open? Does `cass-zzi` exist? Did a parent close and orphan its cross-project children? Stale cross-refs rot silently вҖ” an agent reading a bead sees a reference to `cass-xyz` and assumes it's current, when it may have been closed months ago.

## What

New `bt robot refs --global` subcommand that:

- Scans all issues' description + notes + comments for ID patterns (regex: `[a-z]+-[a-z0-9]+`)
- Scans dependency fields (including `external:` prefixed ones)
- Resolves each ID against the global issue set
- Flags each reference:
  - **broken**: target doesn't exist in global set
  - **stale**: target is closed (informational вҖ” caller decides severity)
  - **orphaned-child**: target's parent closed but target still open
  - **cross-project**: ref points to a different project prefix (informational)

Output shape per detected ref:
- `source` (bead id where ref was found), `target` (referenced id), `location` (description/notes/comments/deps), `flags` (array)

## Scope

- Out: fixing broken refs вҖ” surface, don't fix
- Out: NLP to distinguish "see bt-xyz" from literal bead ID вҖ” regex with word boundaries is sufficient
- Out: refs inside code blocks in descriptions (treat everything as prose for v1)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bt robot refs --global` emits one record per detected ref
- Regex tolerant to punctuation boundaries (parens, commas, periods, colons)
- False-positive rate low enough to be useful (spot-check on real corpus)
- Compact by default (bt-mhwy.1 must land first)

## Relates

- bt-4jyd (global cross-project bead audit)
close_reason:
Summary: Shipped bt robot refs --global вҖ” cross-project reference validation across prose (description/notes/comments) and unresolved external: deps. Emits ref.v1 records with flags {broken, stale, orphaned_child, cross_project}. Scope tightened beyond AC: cross-project only (same-prefix refs handled by dep graph); additionally prefix-scoped to projects actually loaded (drops slug false positives like round-trip, per-issue, batch-closing).

Change: New pkg/view/ref_record.go projection, new cmd/bt/robot_refs.go handler (mirrors robot_pairs.go with pure refsOutput() helper for in-process testing under BT_TEST_MODE=1). Registered robotRefsCmd alongside pairs in cobra_robot.go. Full unit + golden + contract test coverage. Design doc committed.

Files: pkg/view/ref_record.go (new), pkg/view/ref_record_test.go (new, 18 unit cases), pkg/view/projections_test.go (amended with TestRefRecordGolden + TestRefRecordSchemaFileExists), pkg/view/schemas/ref_record.v1.json (new), pkg/view/testdata/fixtures/ref_{empty,single_broken,mixed,external_deps}.json (new) + matching goldens, cmd/bt/robot_refs.go (new), cmd/bt/robot_refs_test.go (new, 7 contract cases + setupRefsFixture + refsFixtureIssues), cmd/bt/cobra_robot.go (robotRefsCmd declaration + init registration), cmd/bt/robot_all_subcommands_test.go (refs row added to flag matrix), docs/design/2026-04-20-bt-mhwy-3-refs.md (new), CHANGELOG.md (session entry), docs/adr/002-stabilize-and-ship.md (Stream 1 [x] line).

Verify: go test ./pkg/view/... ./cmd/bt/... PASS. bt robot refs (no flag) exits 1 with clean error. bt robot refs --global returns 408 records against real shared server: 119 broken, 116 stale, 21 orphaned_child across 20+ projects. jq '.schema' = ref.v1. jq '.refs | length' = 408. Schema + envelope fields (generated_at, data_hash, version, schema, refs) present.

Risk: Low for stale/orphaned_child flags (target exists, high-signal). Medium for broken flag: residual ~30% false-positive rate on real corpus from placeholder text (bt-xxx, cass-xyz) and slug-like suffixes (-only, -side, -show) under known prefixes. Pre-existing failures (bt-5e99 drift tests, bt-kt7x blocker-chain panic, bt-eqro e2e timeout) unchanged.

Notes: Two design pivots landed this session: (1) cross-project-only scope documented in the doc as AC interpretation; (2) prefix-scoping on top of cross-project filter, added after dogfood showed naive ID regex produced ~85% FPR on the real corpus. Bumped broken FPR down to ~30% with prefix scoping; sigil-based v2 filed as bt-vxu9 (related to pairs v2 identity work in bt-gkyn) to push toward <5% FPR via markdown links / code-block skipping / verb-scoped detection (see/ref:/paired with). pkg/analysis.SplitID promoted by bt-mhwy.2 reused here for ID parsing. rc.analysisIssues() composition rule honored вҖ” unresolved external: deps bypass prefix-scoping because external: itself is an unambiguous ref marker. 6 of 7 bt-mhwy children closed; only bt-mhwy.6 (provenance --source filter) remains.

comments.author: sms
comments.text:
Heads-up from bt-mhwy.2 dogfooding (2026-04-20): pure ID-pattern scanning against the real shared Dolt server produces significant false positives because beads auto-generates suffixes that collide across projects (e.g. `bt-153`, `mkt-153`, `cnvs-1hk`, `cass-1hk` вҖ” unrelated work, same random suffix).

For refs this means: a bead that mentions `bt-153` in prose is almost certainly the bt-153 in the current project, but a bead in `mkt` that mentions `bt-153` may be a real cross-project reference OR noise. Two ideas worth considering before implementation:

1. **Scope the scan by prefix presence**: if prose mentions `<prefix>-<suffix>`, only treat it as cross-project when `<prefix>` differs from the source bead's project. Same-prefix refs are intra-project вҖ” not the "cross-project validation" use case.
2. **Require a sigil or delimiter** (e.g. markdown link, backticks, `ref:` keyword) to reduce false positives from prose coincidence.

Also, bt-mhwy.2 promoted `pkg/analysis.SplitID(id)` to exported вҖ” available for refs parsing. And `rc.analysisIssues()` composition rule holds: resolve external deps before scanning.

Filed follow-up: bt-gkyn (pairs v2 вҖ” same class of false-positive problem, intent-based identity).

title: robot pairs subcommand: cross-project paired bead detection
description:
## Why

When `bt-zsy8` and `bd-zsy8` exist in two different databases, they're the same logical work viewed from two project angles. Today nothing surfaces this pairing. Agents can create paired IDs with `bd create --id=<prefix>-<suffix>`, but can't later ask "which pairs exist, and are they in sync?" The data is all there вҖ” global mode already loads issues across databases вҖ” but no subcommand exposes the relationship.

## What

New `bt robot pairs --global` subcommand that:

- Loads all issues from all databases (via existing global mode)
- Groups by ID suffix (everything after the project prefix, e.g. `zsy8` from `bt-zsy8`)
- Reports groups with size вүҘ 2 as paired sets
- For each paired set, emits: canonical (first created), mirrors (array), drift flags
  - drift detection: status mismatch, priority mismatch, one closed while other open, title significantly diverged

Output shape per paired set (compact):
- `suffix`, `canonical` (id, title, status, priority, source_repo), `mirrors` (array of same), `drift` (array of flags)

## Scope

- Out: enforcing pairing вҖ” that's higher-level policy, not a reporting tool
- Out: auto-syncing pairs вҖ” surface drift, don't fix it
- Out: title similarity heuristics (string equality is fine for v1; fuzzy later)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bt robot pairs --global` emits one record per paired set
- Drift flags cover status divergence, priority divergence, and closed/open mismatch
- Compact by default (bt-mhwy.1 must land first)
- Zero paired sets = empty array, not error

## Relates

- bt-6cfg (TUI linking of same-suffix beads)
- dotfiles-dth (global mirroring and dep linking rules)
close_reason:
**Summary**

Shipped `bt robot pairs --global` вҖ” cross-project paired bead detection with drift flags. Canonical = first-created; mirrors report drift across status, priority, closed_open, title. First cross-project projection in `pkg/view/`, validating the pattern for bt-mhwy.3.

**Change**

- `pkg/view/pair_record.go` вҖ” new `pair.v1` projection. `ComputePairRecords(issues)` buckets by ID suffix, drops same-prefix and malformed-ID cases, sorts by `CreatedAt` ascending (tie-break prefix, then full ID) to pick canonical, compares mirrors against canonical to emit drift flags in fixed order.
- `pkg/analysis.SplitID` promoted from private вҖ” pair detection is the second legitimate consumer.
- `cmd/bt/robot_pairs.go` вҖ” `rc.runPairs()` handler plus pure `pairsOutput()` helper. Mandatory `--global` (errors cleanly when absent); empty result emits `"pairs": []` not `null`.
- Cobra wiring + flag-acceptance matrix row.
- JSON schema, 4 golden fixtures, 10 unit tests, 7 contract tests.

**Files**

- `pkg/view/pair_record.go` (new, 200+ lines)
- `pkg/view/pair_record_test.go` (new, 10 cases)
- `pkg/view/projections_test.go` (amended вҖ” `TestPairRecordGolden` + `TestPairRecordSchemaFileExists`)
- `pkg/view/schemas/pair_record.v1.json` (new)
- `pkg/view/testdata/fixtures|golden/pair_{empty,single_in_sync,single_drifted,multi_way}.json` (8 new)
- `pkg/analysis/external_resolution.go` + `_test.go` (amended вҖ” `splitID` вҶ’ `SplitID`)
- `cmd/bt/robot_pairs.go` (new)
- `cmd/bt/robot_pairs_test.go` (new, 7 cases)
- `cmd/bt/cobra_robot.go` (amended вҖ” `robotPairsCmd`)
- `cmd/bt/robot_all_subcommands_test.go` (amended вҖ” matrix row)
- `docs/design/2026-04-20-bt-mhwy-2-pairs.md` (new)
- `CHANGELOG.md` + `docs/adr/002-stabilize-and-ship.md` (session entry + Stream 1)

**Verify**

- `go test ./pkg/analysis/...` вҶ’ ok
- `go test ./pkg/view/...` вҶ’ ok (unit + golden + schema)
- `go test ./cmd/bt/...` вҶ’ ok (contract tests + flag matrix)
- `go build ./cmd/bt/ && go install ./cmd/bt/` вҶ’ ok
- `bt robot pairs 2>&1` вҶ’ `Error: ... requires --global`, exit 1
- `bt robot pairs --global | jq '.schema'` вҶ’ `"pair.v1"`
- `bt robot pairs --global | jq '.pairs | length'` вҶ’ 29 paired sets (real shared server)
- `bt robot pairs --global | jq '[.pairs[] | select(.drift)] | length'` вҶ’ 24 drifted
- Known pair verified: `bt-zsy8` + `bd-zsy8` surfaces with `title` drift flag

**Risk**

- Low. Pure additive projection in `pkg/view/`. `SplitID` rename is mechanical (1 callsite + 1 test).
- Pre-existing failures unrelated to this change: `pkg/drift/` 3 failures (bt-5e99), `bt robot blocker-chain` nil panic (bt-kt7x).
- AC interpretation: "title significantly diverged" = exact string inequality in v1 ("no fuzzy" per bt-mhwy.5 philosophy). Documented in design doc.

**Notes**

- Mandatory `--global`: without it, pair detection is definitionally empty (single prefix). Erroring cleanly disambiguates from the legitimate "no pairs exist under --global" signal.
- `pairsOutput()` testability seam: binary-level `--global` tests can't run under `BT_TEST_MODE=1` (deliberate Dolt discovery guard). The pure helper keeps contract tests in-process.
- Next in epic sequence (per bt-mhwy comment `5 вҶ’ 4 вҶ’ 2 вҶ’ 3 вҶ’ 6`): bt-mhwy.3 (robot refs).
- 4 of 7 bt-mhwy children closed (.0, .1, .4, .5, .2). Remaining: .3, .6.

title: Alerts system redesign: types, filters, severity, notifications
description:
## Context

The alert system (pkg/drift/) computes 10 active alert types across 3 severity levels, but the TUI modal surfaces only severity icons and message strings. Alert types, timestamps, and project scope are invisible to users. The 169-alert list is unfiltered noise - stale issues bury high-signal items like blocking cascades and dependency loops.

This epic covers the full redesign: exposing alert types, adding filters/sort/pagination, recalibrating severity, implementing missing detectors, and separating alerts from notifications.

## Current State
