title: Tree: Implement View() rendering with lipgloss
description:
## Purpose
Render the tree structure as beautiful terminal output using lipgloss styling, matching the existing bv visual language.

## Visual Design Language

### Tree Characters (Box Drawing)
```
в”Ӯ   Vertical line (continuing)
в”ңв”Җ  Branch (has siblings below)
в””в”Җ  Last branch (no siblings below)
в–ё   Collapsed indicator (has hidden children)
в–ҫ   Expanded indicator (children visible)
вҖў   Leaf node (no children)
```

### Complete Example
```
в–ҫ рҹҺҜ P1 EPIC-001 Auth system overhaul
в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җ вҖў вңЁ P2 FEAT-002 OAuth integration
в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җ в–ҫ вңЁ P2 FEAT-003 Session management
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җ вҖў рҹ“қ P2 TASK-006 Add session store
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ вҖў рҹ“қ P3 TASK-007 Session timeout UI
в”Ӯ в””в”Җ вҖў рҹҗӣ P1 BUG-004 Login race condition
в–ё рҹҺҜ P2 EPIC-005 Dashboard redesign (collapsed)
вҖў вңЁ P3 FEAT-100 Standalone feature (no children)
```

### Color Scheme (from existing theme)
| Element | Color | Lipgloss |
|---------|-------|----------|
| Tree lines | Muted | theme.Muted |
| Expand/collapse indicator | Secondary | theme.Secondary |
| Issue type icon | By type | Existing icon colors |
| Priority badge | By priority | Existing priority colors |
| Issue ID | Accent | theme.Accent |
| Issue title | Text | theme.Text |
| Selected row | Inverted | theme.SelectedBg |
| Status indicator | By status | Green/yellow/red/gray |

### Icons by Type (match existing)
```go
var typeIcons = map[model.IssueType]string{
    model.TypeEpic:    "рҹҺҜ",
    model.TypeFeature: "вңЁ",
    model.TypeBug:     "рҹҗӣ",
    model.TypeTask:    "рҹ“қ",
    model.TypeChore:   "рҹ”§",
}
```

### Priority Badges
```
P0 = red background, white text
P1 = orange background
P2 = yellow background  
P3 = gray background
P4+ = dim gray
```

## Layout Modes

### Wide Terminal (width > 100)
Split view: Tree (60%) | Detail Panel (40%)

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ в–ҫ рҹҺҜ P1 EPIC-001 Auth overhaul  в”Ӯ EPIC-001                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җ вңЁ P2 FEAT-002 OAuth       в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җ в–ё вңЁ P2 FEAT-003 Session   в”Ӯ Auth system overhaul    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җ рҹҗӣ P1 BUG-004 Login bug    в”Ӯ                         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в–ё рҹҺҜ P2 EPIC-005 Dashboard      в”Ӯ Priority: P1 (High)     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ Type: Epic              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ Status: In Progress     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ                         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ **Description**         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ Refactor the entire...  в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ E: Exit в”Ӯ j/k: Navigate в”Ӯ Enter: Expand в”Ӯ ?: Help         в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### Narrow Terminal (width < 100)
Full-width tree, Tab to toggle detail panel

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ в–ҫ рҹҺҜ P1 EPIC-001 Auth system overhaul   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җ вңЁ P2 FEAT-002 OAuth integration   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”ңв”Җ в–ё вңЁ P2 FEAT-003 Session mgmt      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җ рҹҗӣ P1 BUG-004 Login race condition в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ в–ё рҹҺҜ P2 EPIC-005 Dashboard redesign     в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ E: Exit в”Ӯ Tab: Detail в”Ӯ Enter: Expand   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

## Rendering Algorithm

### Per-Node Rendering
```go
func (t *TreeModel) renderNode(node *TreeNode, isLast bool, prefix string) string {
    var sb strings.Builder
    r := t.theme.Renderer
    
    // Tree structure characters
    branch := "в”ңв”Җ"
    if isLast {
        branch = "в””в”Җ"
    }
    
    // Expand/collapse indicator
    var indicator string
    if len(node.Children) > 0 {
        if node.Expanded {
            indicator = "в–ҫ"
        } else {
            indicator = "в–ё"
        }
    } else {
        indicator = "вҖў"
    }
    
    // Build the line
    treeChars := r.NewStyle().Foreground(t.theme.Muted)
    
    sb.WriteString(prefix)
    sb.WriteString(treeChars.Render(branch + " "))
    sb.WriteString(indicator + " ")
    sb.WriteString(renderIssueCompact(node.Issue, t.theme))
    
    return sb.String()
}
```

### Title Truncation
If title exceeds available width:
- Truncate with ellipsis: "Very long title that..."
- Ensure minimum visible: 20 chars before truncation
- Account for tree prefix width at each depth

### Viewport Scrolling
Use bubbles/viewport for:
- Scroll when tree exceeds terminal height
- Show scroll indicators (в–І в–ј) at edges
- Ensure selected row is always visible

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tree renders with proper indentation
- [ ] Box-drawing characters align correctly
- [ ] Icons and colors match existing theme
- [ ] Selected row is visually distinct
- [ ] Split view works on wide terminals
- [ ] Truncation handles long titles gracefully
- [ ] Scrolling works for tall trees
- [ ] No visual glitches on resize
close_reason: Implemented View() rendering with: tree line characters (в”Ӯв”ңв””), expand/collapse indicators (в–ҫв–ёвҖў), type icons, priority badges, status indicators, selected row highlighting via theme.Selected, empty state message with help text. Added 4 new tests for View() rendering. All 15 tree tests pass.

title: Tree: Implement navigation and keyboard handling
description:
## Purpose
Enable users to navigate the tree structure intuitively using keyboard shortcuts, with expand/collapse functionality.

## Navigation Model

### The flatList Concept
Trees are 2D but keyboard navigation is 1D. We solve this by maintaining a **flatList** - a flattened array of currently visible nodes.

```
Visual Tree:              flatList index:
в–ҫ EPIC-001                [0]
  в”ңв”Җ FEAT-002             [1]
  в”ңв”Җ в–ё FEAT-003           [2] (collapsed - children hidden)
  в””в”Җ TASK-004             [3]
в–ё EPIC-005                [4] (collapsed)
```

When FEAT-003 is expanded:
```
Visual Tree:              flatList index:
в–ҫ EPIC-001                [0]
  в”ңв”Җ FEAT-002             [1]
  в”ңв”Җ в–ҫ FEAT-003           [2]
  в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җ TASK-006         [3] (newly visible)
  в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ TASK-007         [4] (newly visible)
  в””в”Җ TASK-004             [5]
в–ё EPIC-005                [6]
```

### RebuildFlatList Algorithm
```go
func (t *TreeModel) RebuildFlatList() {
    t.flatList = t.flatList[:0] // Reset
    for _, root := range t.roots {
        t.appendVisible(root)
    }
}

func (t *TreeModel) appendVisible(node *TreeNode) {
    t.flatList = append(t.flatList, node)
    if node.Expanded {
        for _, child := range node.Children {
            t.appendVisible(child)
        }
    }
}
```

## Keyboard Mappings

### Navigation
| Key | Action | Notes |
|-----|--------|-------|
| `j` / `вҶ“` | Move cursor down | Wraps at bottom (optional) |
| `k` / `вҶ‘` | Move cursor up | Wraps at top (optional) |
| `gg` | Jump to first node | Vim-style |
| `G` | Jump to last node | |
| `Ctrl+D` | Page down | Half-viewport scroll |
| `Ctrl+U` | Page up | |

### Expand/Collapse
| Key | Action | Notes |
|-----|--------|-------|
| `Enter` / `Space` | Toggle expand/collapse | On leaf node: no-op or show detail |
| `вҶ’` / `l` | Expand current | If already expanded: move to first child |
| `вҶҗ` / `h` | Collapse current | If already collapsed: jump to parent |
| `o` | Expand all descendants | Recursive expand |
| `O` | Collapse all | Reset to roots-only |
| `zo` | Expand current (vim-style) | Alternative |
| `zc` | Collapse current | Alternative |
| `zM` | Collapse all | Alternative |
| `zR` | Expand all | Alternative |

### Exit
| Key | Action | Notes |
|-----|--------|-------|
| `E` | Exit tree view | Return to list |
| `Esc` | Exit tree view | Alternative |
| `q` | Exit tree OR quit app | Context-dependent |

## Implementation Details

### MoveDown() / MoveUp()
```go
func (t *TreeModel) MoveDown() {
    if t.cursor < len(t.flatList)-1 {
        t.cursor++
        t.ensureVisible()
    }
}

func (t *TreeModel) ensureVisible() {
    // Scroll viewport if cursor is outside visible range
    // Similar to list.Model behavior
}
```

### ToggleExpand()
```go
func (t *TreeModel) ToggleExpand() {
    if t.cursor >= len(t.flatList) {
        return
    }
    node := t.flatList[t.cursor]
    if len(node.Children) == 0 {
        return // Leaf node - nothing to toggle
    }
    node.Expanded = !node.Expanded
    t.RebuildFlatList()
    // Cursor may need adjustment if collapsing
}
```

### JumpToParent()
```go
func (t *TreeModel) JumpToParent() {
    if t.cursor >= len(t.flatList) {
        return
    }
    node := t.flatList[t.cursor]
    if node.Parent == nil {
        return // Already at root
    }
    // Find parent in flatList
    for i, n := range t.flatList {
        if n == node.Parent {
            t.cursor = i
            t.ensureVisible()
            return
        }
    }
}
```

## State Management

### Preserving Cursor on Rebuild
When tree is rebuilt (filter change, data reload):
1. Remember current issue ID
2. Rebuild tree
3. Find same issue ID in new flatList
4. Set cursor to that index (or 0 if not found)

### Preserving Expand State
Store expanded IDs in a set:
```go
expandedIDs map[string]bool
```
On rebuild, restore expanded state from this set.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] j/k navigation works correctly
- [ ] Enter toggles expand/collapse
- [ ] вҶҗ jumps to parent when collapsed
- [ ] вҶ’ expands or moves to first child
- [ ] gg/G jump to top/bottom
- [ ] Expand/collapse rebuilds flatList correctly
- [ ] Cursor preserved across filter changes
- [ ] No panic on empty tree
close_reason: Implemented all navigation methods: JumpToParent, ExpandOrMoveToChild, CollapseOrJumpToParent, PageUp/PageDown, SelectByID, GetSelectedID. All 20 tree tests passing.

title: Tree: Implement tree building from parent-child deps
description:
## Purpose
Build the actual tree structure from issue data, using parent-child dependencies to establish hierarchy.

## Why This Matters
The tree builder is the core algorithm that transforms flat issue data into a navigable hierarchy. It must handle:
- Issues with parents (children)
- Issues without parents (roots)
- Issues with no parent-child deps at all
- Cycles (invalid but must not crash)
- Multiple children per parent
- Missing parent references (dangling deps)

## Algorithm Design

### Step 1: Build ParentвҶ’Children Index
```go
// childrenOf maps parentID вҶ’ slice of child issues
childrenOf := make(map[string][]*model.Issue)
// hasParent tracks which issues have parents
hasParent := make(map[string]bool)

for _, issue := range issues {
    for _, dep := range issue.Dependencies {
        if dep.Type == model.DepParentChild {
            // This issue has dep.DependsOnID as parent
            parentID := dep.DependsOnID
            childrenOf[parentID] = append(childrenOf[parentID], &issue)
            hasParent[issue.ID] = true
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 2: Identify Root Nodes
```go
var roots []*TreeNode
for _, issue := range issues {
    if !hasParent[issue.ID] {
        // This issue has no parent - it is a root
        roots = append(roots, buildNode(&issue, 0, childrenOf, nil, visited))
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Recursive Node Building (with cycle detection)
```go
func buildNode(issue *model.Issue, depth int, 
               childrenOf map[string][]*model.Issue,
               parent *TreeNode,
               visited map[string]bool) *TreeNode {
    
    // Cycle detection
    if visited[issue.ID] {
        return &TreeNode{
            Issue: issue,
            Depth: depth,
            // Mark as cycle - no children
        }
    }
    visited[issue.ID] = true
    defer func() { visited[issue.ID] = false }()
    
    node := &TreeNode{
        Issue:    issue,
        Depth:    depth,
        Parent:   parent,
        Expanded: depth < 2, // Auto-expand first 2 levels
    }
    
    for _, child := range childrenOf[issue.ID] {
        childNode := buildNode(child, depth+1, childrenOf, node, visited)
        node.Children = append(node.Children, childNode)
    }
    
    return node
}
```

### Step 4: Sort Children (consistent ordering)
Within each parent, sort children by:
1. Priority (ascending - P0 first)
2. IssueType (epicsвҶ’featuresвҶ’tasksвҶ’bugsвҶ’chores)
3. CreatedAt (oldest first)

### Step 5: Handle Empty Tree
If no parent-child relationships exist:
- Group issues by IssueType
- Create synthetic "root" nodes for each type
- Or: show flat list with type indicators

## Edge Cases

| Scenario | Handling |
|----------|----------|
| Issue references non-existent parent | Skip dep, issue becomes root |
| Circular parent-child | Cycle detection prevents infinite loop |
| Very deep nesting (10+ levels) | Works but may truncate display |
| Empty issues list | Return empty roots slice |
| All issues are roots | Show flat list of roots |

## Performance Considerations
- Single pass to build index: O(n * d) where d = avg deps per issue
- Single pass to build tree: O(n)
- Total: O(n) for typical datasets
- For 500 issues: < 10ms expected

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Build() populates roots slice correctly
- [ ] Parent-child deps create proper nesting
- [ ] Orphan issues appear as roots
- [ ] Cycles detected and handled gracefully
- [ ] Children sorted consistently
- [ ] Performance: < 50ms for 1000 issues
close_reason: Implemented Build() method with: parent-child index building, root node identification, recursive tree building with cycle detection, consistent child sorting (priorityвҶ’typeвҶ’date), edge case handling. Added comprehensive test suite (12 tests) covering all acceptance criteria. All tests pass.

title: Epic: Hierarchical Tree View (GitHub #43)
description:
## Background & Motivation

A user requested (GitHub issue #43) the ability to view issues in an indented tree format, similar to a file browser, showing epicвҶ’taskвҶ’subtask relationships. Currently bv has:

- **List View**: Flat list sorted by priority/date
- **Board View**: Kanban columns by status
- **Graph View**: Dependency network visualization

None of these show hierarchical parent-child relationships in an intuitive indented format that makes project structure immediately visible.

## Why This Matters

1. **Cognitive Load**: Users working on large projects need to understand structure at a glance
2. **Context Switching**: When jumping between issues, understanding where an issue "lives" in the hierarchy is valuable
3. **Planning**: Seeing epicвҶ’task breakdowns helps validate that work is properly decomposed
4. **Complementary to Graph**: Graph shows blocking relationships; Tree shows organizational structure

## Design Decisions

### Keyboard Shortcut: `E` (for Epics/hierarchy)
- `T`/`t` are taken (time-travel)
- `E` is available and mnemonic for "Epic tree" or "hierarchy trEe"
- Uppercase for consistency with other view toggles (`U`, `V`)

### Primary Data Source: parent-child Dependencies
The beads model has `Dependencies []*Dependency` where each has a Type:
- `blocks` - Blocking relationship (used by Graph view)
- `parent-child` - Hierarchical relationship (THIS is what Tree uses)
- `related` - Informational linkage
- `discovered-from` - Provenance tracking

**Important**: parent-child is NOT a blocking relationship (verified in graph_test.go:268). An open parent does not block closed children.

### Fallback Behavior
If no parent-child dependencies exist:
1. Show issues grouped by IssueType (epicsвҶ’featuresвҶ’tasksвҶ’bugs)
2. Display informational message explaining how to create hierarchy
3. This ensures the view is useful even for projects not using parent-child deps

### Multi-Mode Support (Stretch Goal)
- **Hierarchy Mode** (default): parent-child relationships
- **Blocking Mode**: blocking dependencies as tree (what blocks what)
- Toggle with `v` key within tree view

## Scope

### In Scope
- New TreeModel in pkg/ui/tree.go
- Integration with main Model (focusTree focus state)
- Keyboard shortcut `E` to toggle from list view
- Split-view: tree on left, detail on right (wide terminals)
- Navigation: j/k, expand/collapse, jump to parent
- Respect current filters (show filtered issues only)
- Status/priority indicators matching existing theme

### Out of Scope (Future Work)
- Drag-and-drop rearrangement
- Inline editing of issues
- Export tree as text/markdown
- Robot command (--robot-tree)
- Blocking mode (save for v2)

## Success Criteria
1. User can press `E` to toggle tree view on/off
2. Issues with parent-child deps show proper indentation
3. Expand/collapse works intuitively
4. Selection syncs with detail panel
5. No performance regression on large datasets (500+ issues)
close_reason: Hierarchical Tree View feature complete: skeleton, building, rendering, navigation, integration, and tests all implemented

title: Tree View: TreeModel and navigation
description:
Implement the TreeModel struct with navigation and keyboard handling.

## TreeModel Fields
- nodes []*TreeNode (root nodes)
- flatList []*TreeNode (flattened for navigation)
- cursor int (current selection)
- viewport viewport.Model
- theme Theme
- viewMode TreeViewMode (hierarchy vs blocking)

## Navigation Methods
- MoveUp() / MoveDown() - Navigate between visible nodes
- ToggleExpand() - Expand/collapse current node
- ExpandAll() / CollapseAll() - Bulk operations

- v - Switch view mode (hierarchyвҶ”blocking)

title: Implementation notes: Critical fixes and considerations
description:
## Purpose

This task documents critical implementation details discovered during the design review. **READ BEFORE IMPLEMENTING OTHER TASKS.**

## вҡ пёҸ CRITICAL FIX #1: Over-fetch Strategy (bv-xbar.5, bv-xbar.6)

**Problem:** Naive re-ranking fails to surface high-graph-score issues with mediocre text scores.

**Example:**
- Issue A: text=0.6, pagerank=0.9 вҶ’ hybridвүҲ0.75 (should rank high!)
- Issue B: text=0.8, pagerank=0.1 вҶ’ hybridвүҲ0.35

Getting top-10 by text score excludes A, so re-ranking can't fix it.

**Solution:** Over-fetch 2-3x candidates before hybrid scoring:
\`\`\`go
fetchLimit := limit * 3  // Over-fetch for re-ranking
textResults, _ := idx.SearchTopK(queryVec, fetchLimit)
// Apply hybrid scoring to ALL candidates, then trim to limit
\`\`\`

bv-xbar.8 (web) already has this: \`limit * 2\`. Apply same pattern to CLI and TUI.

## рҹ”§ SCOPE FIX #2: Remove Config File (bv-xbar.10)

The presets task originally included \`~/.config/bv/search.json\` support, but:
1. bv doesn't have a config file system
2. CLI flags + env vars are sufficient for v1
3. Adds unnecessary complexity

**Revised scope:** Only implement presets, env vars, and CLI flags. Defer config file to future task if needed.

## вҡ пёҸ EDGE CASE #3: Zero Text Weight

If user sets \`text=0\`, results are ranked purely by graph metrics, ignoring the search query. This is valid but may surprise users.

**Recommendation:** Add warning (not error) in Weights.Validate() if text < 0.1.

## рҹ”— PARITY CHECK #4: Go/JS Preset Sync

JavaScript presets in hybrid_scorer.js MUST match Go presets exactly. Add a unit test that:
1. Reads JS preset values from the file
2. Compares against Go GetPreset() values
3. Fails if any preset drifts

## рҹ’ӯ FUTURE CONSIDERATION: Status Normalization

Current mapping (open=1.0, in_progress=0.8, blocked=0.5, closed=0.1) is a reasonable default, but different use cases might want different interpretations:
- Sprint planning: open > blocked > in_progress
- Bottleneck analysis: blocked issues MORE important

For v1, single normalization is fine. Consider per-preset status weights in v2.

## Success Criteria

This task is complete when implementers have read and acknowledged these notes. It can be closed immediately after review.

title: Add weight presets and environment configuration
description:
## рҹ”§ SCOPE REDUCED: No Config File

**REMOVED:** Config file support (~/.config/bv/search.json) - bv has no config system.

**KEEP:** CLI flags + environment variables are sufficient for v1.

---

## Objective

Implement the weight preset system and environment variable configuration for hybrid search, enabling users to customize ranking behavior without code changes.

## Preset Definitions

### Go Implementation (pkg/search/presets.go)

```go
var presets = map[PresetName]Weights{
    PresetDefault: {
        TextRelevance: 0.40,
        PageRank:      0.20,
        Status:        0.15,
        Impact:        0.10,
        Priority:      0.10,
        Recency:       0.05,
    },
    PresetBugHunting: {
        TextRelevance: 0.30,
        PageRank:      0.15,
        Status:        0.15,
        Impact:        0.15,
        Priority:      0.20, // Boosted for bug severity
        Recency:       0.05,
    },
    PresetSprintPlanning: {
        TextRelevance: 0.30,
        PageRank:      0.20,
        Status:        0.25, // Boosted for actionability
        Impact:        0.15,
        Priority:      0.05,
        Recency:       0.05,
    },
    PresetImpactFirst: {
        TextRelevance: 0.25,
        PageRank:      0.30, // Boosted graph importance
        Status:        0.10,
        Impact:        0.20, // Boosted blocker impact
        Priority:      0.10,
        Recency:       0.05,
    },
    PresetTextOnly: {
        TextRelevance: 1.00,
        PageRank:      0.00,
        Status:        0.00,
        Impact:        0.00,
        Priority:      0.00,
        Recency:       0.00,
    },
}
```

## Environment Variables

```bash
# Override default search mode
BV_SEARCH_MODE=hybrid         # text|hybrid

# Override default preset when mode=hybrid
BV_SEARCH_PRESET=default      # default|bug-hunting|sprint-planning|impact-first|text-only

# Custom weights (JSON, overrides preset)
BV_SEARCH_WEIGHTS='{"text":0.5,"pagerank":0.3,"status":0.1,"impact":0.05,"priority":0.03,"recency":0.02}'
```

## Configuration Precedence

1. CLI flags (highest priority)
2. Environment variables
3. Config file (~/.config/bv/search.json)
4. Built-in defaults (lowest priority)

## Config File Format

```json
// ~/.config/bv/search.json
{
  "default_mode": "hybrid",
  "default_preset": "default",
  "custom_presets": {
    "my-team": {
      "text": 0.35,
      "pagerank": 0.25,
      "status": 0.15,
      "impact": 0.10,
      "priority": 0.10,
      "recency": 0.05
    }
  }
}
```

## Validation

Weights must:
- Be non-negative
- Sum to 1.0 (Вұ0.001 tolerance)
- Have all 6 fields present

```go
func (w Weights) Validate() error {
    if w.TextRelevance < 0 || w.PageRank < 0 || w.Status < 0 ||
       w.Impact < 0 || w.Priority < 0 || w.Recency < 0 {
        return errors.New("weights must be non-negative")
    }
    
    sum := w.TextRelevance + w.PageRank + w.Status + w.Impact + w.Priority + w.Recency
    if math.Abs(sum-1.0) > 0.001 {
        return fmt.Errorf("weights must sum to 1.0, got %.3f", sum)
    }
    
    return nil
}
```

## CLI Help Text

```
Search Mode and Ranking:
  --search-mode text|hybrid    Ranking algorithm (default: text)
                               - text: Pure text/vector similarity
                               - hybrid: Graph-aware multi-factor ranking

  --search-preset <name>       Weight preset for hybrid mode
                               - default: Balanced ranking
                               - bug-hunting: Boost priority and impact
                               - sprint-planning: Boost actionability (status)
                               - impact-first: Boost PageRank and blocker count
                               - text-only: Pure text (same as --search-mode=text)

  --search-weights <json>      Custom weights (overrides preset)
                               Example: '{"text":0.5,"pagerank":0.3,...}'

Environment variables: BV_SEARCH_MODE, BV_SEARCH_PRESET, BV_SEARCH_WEIGHTS
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 5 presets defined and tested
- [ ] Environment variables override defaults
- [ ] Config file loading implemented
- [ ] CLI flags override everything
- [ ] Validation rejects invalid weights
- [ ] Help text documents all options
- [ ] Unit tests for config precedence

## Files to Create/Modify

- pkg/search/presets.go (preset definitions)
- pkg/search/config.go (config loading)
- pkg/search/config_test.go
- cmd/bv/main.go (flag registration)

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bv-xbar.1 (Weights struct definition)
- Blocked by: None
- Blocks: bv-xbar.5 (CLI needs presets)

  вҶ’ bv-xbar: [EPIC] Graph-Aware Hybrid Search Ranking [P1]

title: Web export: JavaScript HybridScorer in viewer.js
Web export: Add graph metrics to SQLite export
description:
## Objective

Include graph metrics (PageRank, betweenness, blocker count) in the SQLite database exported for the web viewer, enabling client-side hybrid scoring.

## Background

Currently, graph metrics are computed by bv but not included in the --export-pages SQLite output. The web viewer can only do FTS5 text search.

This task extends the SQLite schema to include metrics, making hybrid search possible in the browser.

## Schema Changes

### Add Columns to issue_overview_mv

```sql
-- Extend the existing materialized view
CREATE TABLE issue_overview_mv (
    -- existing columns...
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    status TEXT,
    priority INTEGER,
    title TEXT,
    description TEXT,
    labels TEXT,  -- JSON array
    -- NEW: graph metrics
    pagerank REAL DEFAULT 0.5,
    betweenness REAL DEFAULT 0.0,
    blocker_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    dependent_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    critical_depth INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    in_cycle INTEGER DEFAULT 0  -- boolean as int
);
```

### Populate During Export

Modify sqlite_export.go to include metrics from graph_layout.json:

```go
func (e *SQLiteExporter) populateMetrics(tx *sql.Tx) error {
    // Load graph_layout.json
    layout, _ := e.loadGraphLayout()
    
    for issueID, metrics := range layout.Metrics {
        // metrics[0]=pagerank, [1]=betweenness, [2]=criticalDepth, 
        // [3]=blockerCount, [4]=inCycle
        _, err := tx.Exec(`
            UPDATE issue_overview_mv 
            SET pagerank = ?, betweenness = ?, blocker_count = ?, 
                critical_depth = ?, in_cycle = ?
            WHERE id = ?`,
            metrics[0], metrics[1], int(metrics[3]), 
            int(metrics[2]), int(metrics[4]), issueID)
    }
    return nil
}
```

## Data Flow

```
bv --export-pages
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ Write issues to issue_overview_mv
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ Compute graph layout (graph_layout.json)
    в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җ Contains Metrics map[string][5]float64
    в””в”Җв”Җ Populate metrics columns from graph_layout
```

## Metrics Mapping

From graph_layout.json Metrics array:
- [0] вҶ’ pagerank (REAL)
- [1] вҶ’ betweenness (REAL)
- [2] вҶ’ criticalDepth (INTEGER)
- [3] вҶ’ blockerCount (INTEGER)
- [4] вҶ’ inCycle (INTEGER, 0/1)

## Backward Compatibility

- New columns have DEFAULT values
- Older exports without metrics still work (neutral values)
- No breaking changes to existing queries

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Schema includes new metric columns
- [ ] Export populates metrics from graph analysis
- [ ] Default values for missing metrics
- [ ] Existing export tests pass
- [ ] New test verifies metrics present
- [ ] No performance regression in export

## Files to Modify

- pkg/export/sqlite_schema.go (schema definition)
- pkg/export/sqlite_export.go (populate metrics)
- tests/e2e/export_pages_test.go (verify metrics)

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bv-xbar.1 (interface design for consistency)
- Blocked by: None (can start immediately)
- Blocks: bv-xbar.8 (JS hybrid scorer needs this data)

title: CLI integration: --robot-search hybrid mode
description:
## вҡ пёҸ CRITICAL: Over-fetch Strategy Required

**BUG:** Getting top-K by text score, then re-ranking misses high-graph-score issues with mediocre text scores.

**FIX:** Over-fetch 3x candidates before hybrid scoring:
```go
fetchLimit := limit * 3  // Over-fetch for re-ranking
textResults, _ := idx.SearchTopK(queryVec, fetchLimit)
// Apply hybrid scoring, re-sort, trim to requested limit
```

---

## Objective

Add hybrid search mode to the CLI --robot-search command, allowing AI agents to leverage graph-aware ranking.

## Background

Currently --robot-search returns results ranked purely by vector similarity. This task adds:
- --search-mode flag to switch between text and hybrid
- --search-preset flag to use named weight configurations
- Extended JSON output with component scores

## New CLI Flags

```
--search-mode text|hybrid    Mode for ranking (default: text)
--search-preset <name>       Weight preset: default, bug-hunting, sprint-planning, 
                             impact-first, text-only
--search-weights <json>      Custom weights JSON (advanced, overrides preset)
```

## Implementation

### Flag Registration (cmd/bv/main.go)

```go
searchCmd.Flags().StringVar(&searchMode, "search-mode", "text", 
    "Search ranking mode: text (pure text), hybrid (graph-aware)")
searchCmd.Flags().StringVar(&searchPreset, "search-preset", "",
    "Weight preset for hybrid mode: default, bug-hunting, sprint-planning, impact-first, text-only")
searchCmd.Flags().StringVar(&searchWeightsJSON, "search-weights", "",
    "Custom weights JSON (overrides preset)")
```

### Handler Logic

```go
func handleRobotSearch() {
    // 1. Get text search results
    textResults, _ := idx.SearchTopK(queryVec, limit)
    
    // 2. If hybrid mode, apply HybridScorer
    if searchMode == "hybrid" {
        weights := getWeights(searchPreset, searchWeightsJSON)
        scorer := search.NewHybridScorer(weights, metricsCache)
        
        var hybridResults []search.HybridScore
        for _, r := range textResults {
            score, _ := scorer.Score(r.IssueID, r.Score)
            hybridResults = append(hybridResults, score)
        }
        
        // Re-sort by hybrid score
        sort.Slice(hybridResults, func(i, j int) bool {
            return hybridResults[i].FinalScore > hybridResults[j].FinalScore
        })
        
        outputHybridJSON(hybridResults, weights)
    } else {
        outputTextJSON(textResults)
    }
}
```

## JSON Output Format

### Text Mode (existing, unchanged)
```json
{
  "generated_at": "2025-01-15T...",
  "data_hash": "abc123",
  "query": "authentication",
  "mode": "text",
  "results": [
    {"issue_id": "bv-abc", "score": 0.85, "title": "..."}
  ]
}
```

### Hybrid Mode (new)
```json
{
  "generated_at": "2025-01-15T...",
  "data_hash": "abc123",
  "query": "authentication",
  "mode": "hybrid",
  "preset": "bug-hunting",
  "weights": {
    "text": 0.30, "pagerank": 0.15, "status": 0.15,
    "impact": 0.15, "priority": 0.20, "recency": 0.05
  },
  "results": [
    {
      "issue_id": "bv-abc",
      "score": 0.87,
      "text_score": 0.85,
      "component_scores": {
        "pagerank": 0.72, "status": 1.0, "impact": 0.45,
        "priority": 0.8, "recency": 0.95
      },
      "title": "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

## Environment Variables

```bash
BV_SEARCH_MODE=hybrid       # Default mode
BV_SEARCH_PRESET=default    # Default preset
```

## Backward Compatibility

- Default mode is "text" (existing behavior)
- Existing --robot-search without flags works identically
- New fields only appear when mode=hybrid

## Success Criteria

- [ ] --search-mode flag works
- [ ] --search-preset flag loads correct weights
- [ ] --search-weights accepts custom JSON
- [ ] Hybrid results re-ranked by combined score
- [ ] JSON output includes component breakdown
- [ ] Env vars override defaults
- [ ] Existing tests pass (text mode unchanged)
- [ ] Integration test for hybrid mode

## Files to Modify

- cmd/bv/main.go (flags + handler)
- cmd/bv/search_output.go (new file for JSON formatting)

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bv-xbar.3 (HybridScorer implementation)

Depends on (3):
  вҶ’ bv-xbar: [EPIC] Graph-Aware Hybrid Search Ranking [P1]
  вҶ’ bv-xbar.3: Implement HybridScorer with configurable weights [P1]
  вҶ’ bv-xbar.10: Add weight presets and environment configuration [P1]

Blocks (2):
  вҶҗ bv-xbar.12: Integration tests for CLI, TUI, and Web hybrid search [P2]
  вҶҗ bv-xbar.14: Documentation: Update AGENTS.md and README for hybrid search [P2]

title: Implement HybridScorer with configurable weights
description:
## рҹ“қ FUTURE: Preset-specific Status Weights

Current values (open=1.0, in_progress=0.8, blocked=0.5, closed=0.1) are for general actionability.

Future enhancement: Allow presets to override status weights:
- sprint-planning: blocked=0.2 (deprioritize blocked during sprint)
- bug-hunting: status weights unchanged (focus on priority/impact)

Keep simple for v1; expose as configuration option later if needed.

---

## Objective

Implement the core HybridScorer that combines text relevance with graph metrics using configurable weights.

## Background

This is the heart of hybrid search ranking. Given a text score and issue metrics, compute a weighted sum that balances relevance with graph importance.

## Deliverables

### HybridScorer Implementation (pkg/search/hybrid_scorer_impl.go)

```go
type hybridScorer struct {
    weights Weights
    cache   MetricsCache
}

// NewHybridScorer creates a scorer with the given weights and metrics cache.
func NewHybridScorer(weights Weights, cache MetricsCache) HybridScorer

func (s *hybridScorer) Score(issueID string, textScore float64) (HybridScore, error) {
    metrics, found := s.cache.Get(issueID)
    if !found {
        // Graceful degradation: return text-only score
        return HybridScore{
            IssueID:    issueID,
            FinalScore: textScore,
            TextScore:  textScore,
        }, nil
    }
    
    // Compute normalized component scores
    statusScore := normalizeStatus(metrics.Status)
    priorityScore := normalizePriority(metrics.Priority)
    impactScore := normalizeImpact(metrics.BlockerCount, s.cache.MaxBlockerCount())
    recencyScore := normalizeRecency(metrics.UpdatedAt)
    
    // Weighted sum
    final := s.weights.TextRelevance * textScore +
             s.weights.PageRank * metrics.PageRank +
             s.weights.Status * statusScore +
             s.weights.Impact * impactScore +
             s.weights.Priority * priorityScore +
             s.weights.Recency * recencyScore
    
    return HybridScore{
        IssueID:    issueID,
        FinalScore: final,
        TextScore:  textScore,
        ComponentScores: map[string]float64{
            "pagerank": metrics.PageRank,
            "status":   statusScore,
            "impact":   impactScore,
            "priority": priorityScore,
            "recency":  recencyScore,
        },
    }, nil
}
```

### Normalization Functions (pkg/search/normalizers.go)

```go
// normalizeStatus maps status to [0,1] range favoring actionable states.
func normalizeStatus(status string) float64 {
    switch status {
    case "open":        return 1.0
    case "in_progress": return 0.8
    case "blocked":     return 0.5
    case "closed":      return 0.1
    default:            return 0.5
    }
}

// normalizePriority maps P0-P4 to [0.2, 1.0] range.
func normalizePriority(priority int) float64 {
    switch priority {
    case 0: return 1.0  // P0 Critical
    case 1: return 0.8  // P1 High
    case 2: return 0.6  // P2 Medium
    case 3: return 0.4  // P3 Low
    case 4: return 0.2  // P4 Lowest
    default: return 0.5
    }
}

// normalizeImpact normalizes blocker count to [0,1].
func normalizeImpact(blockerCount, maxBlockerCount int) float64 {
    if maxBlockerCount == 0 {
        return 0.5 // Neutral when no blockers exist
    }
    return float64(blockerCount) / float64(maxBlockerCount)
}

// normalizeRecency applies exponential decay (half-life ~30 days).
func normalizeRecency(updatedAt time.Time) float64 {
    daysSinceUpdate := time.Since(updatedAt).Hours() / 24
    return math.Exp(-daysSinceUpdate / 30)
}
```

## Design Decisions

1. **Component scores always computed**: Even when weight is 0, compute for transparency
2. **Graceful degradation**: Missing metrics вҶ’ text-only score (no error)
3. **Normalization to [0,1]**: All factors normalized before weighting
4. **Weights must sum to ~1.0**: Enforced by Weights.Validate()

## Success Criteria

- [ ] HybridScorer implementation computes correct weighted scores
- [ ] All 4 normalizer functions implemented with tests
- [ ] Component breakdown included in HybridScore
- [ ] Graceful handling of missing metrics
- [ ] Unit tests with known inputs вҶ’ expected outputs
- [ ] Benchmark showing <1ms per score computation

## Files to Create

- pkg/search/hybrid_scorer_impl.go
- pkg/search/normalizers.go
- pkg/search/normalizers_test.go
- pkg/search/hybrid_scorer_test.go

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bv-xbar.1 (interface), bv-xbar.2 (MetricsCache)

Depends on (3):
  вҶ’ bv-xbar: [EPIC] Graph-Aware Hybrid Search Ranking [P1]
  вҶ’ bv-xbar.1: Design HybridScorer interface and weight configuration [P1]
  вҶ’ bv-xbar.2: Implement MetricsCache for search-time metric access [P1]

Blocks (5):
  вҶҗ bv-xbar.11: Unit tests for HybridScorer and MetricsCache [P1]
  вҶҗ bv-xbar.5: CLI integration: --robot-search hybrid mode [P1]
  вҶҗ bv-xbar.4: Add field boosting for title/labels/description [P2]
  вҶҗ bv-xbar.6: TUI integration: hybrid search toggle [P2]
  вҶҗ bv-xbar.13: Performance benchmarks for hybrid search [P3]

title: Implement MetricsCache for search-time metric access
description:
## Objective

Create a cache layer that provides graph metrics (PageRank, status, priority, blocker count, updated_at) for search-time hybrid score computation.

## Background

Graph metrics are computed by bv's analysis engine (--robot-insights) but aren't easily accessible during search. The MetricsCache bridges this gap by:
1. Loading metrics on demand (lazy initialization)
2. Caching for repeated lookups within a search session
3. Invalidating when data_hash changes

## Deliverables

### MetricsCache Interface (pkg/search/metrics_cache.go)

```go
// IssueMetrics contains the graph-derived metrics for hybrid scoring.
type IssueMetrics struct {
    IssueID      string    `json:"issue_id"`
    PageRank     float64   `json:"pagerank"`      // 0.0-1.0, from graph analysis
    Status       string    `json:"status"`        // open|in_progress|blocked|closed
    Priority     int       `json:"priority"`      // 0-4 (P0=0, P4=4)
    BlockerCount int       `json:"blocker_count"` // How many issues this blocks
    UpdatedAt    time.Time `json:"updated_at"`    // For recency calculation
}

// MetricsCache provides fast access to issue metrics for hybrid scoring.
type MetricsCache interface {
    // Get returns metrics for an issue, computing/loading if needed.
    Get(issueID string) (IssueMetrics, bool)
    
    // GetBatch returns metrics for multiple issues efficiently.
    GetBatch(issueIDs []string) map[string]IssueMetrics
    
    // Refresh recomputes the cache from source data.
    Refresh() error
    
    // DataHash returns the hash of source data for cache validation.
    DataHash() string
    
    // MaxBlockerCount returns the maximum blocker count for normalization.
    MaxBlockerCount() int
}
```

### Implementation (pkg/search/metrics_cache_impl.go)

```go
type metricsCache struct {
    mu              sync.RWMutex
    metrics         map[string]IssueMetrics
    dataHash        string
    maxBlockerCount int
    loader          MetricsLoader
}

// MetricsLoader abstracts the source of metrics (graph analysis or direct DB).
type MetricsLoader interface {
    LoadMetrics() (map[string]IssueMetrics, error)
    ComputeDataHash() (string, error)
}
```

## Data Sources

Metrics come from existing bv computations:
- **PageRank**: Already computed in pkg/graph/analysis.go
- **Status/Priority**: Directly from issue model
- **BlockerCount**: From dependency graph (out-degree in blocks relation)
- **UpdatedAt**: Directly from issue model

## Lazy Loading Strategy

1. First search call triggers cache initialization
2. Cache stores data_hash to detect stale data
3. On subsequent calls, check data_hash; refresh if changed
4. Use sync.RWMutex for concurrent read access

## Graceful Degradation

If metrics unavailable (e.g., no graph computed):
- Return default metrics with neutral values
- PageRank: 0.5 (neutral)
- BlockerCount: 0
- Don't fail the search

## Success Criteria

- [ ] MetricsCache interface defined
- [ ] Implementation loads from graph analysis
- [ ] Lazy initialization on first access
- [ ] Cache invalidation on data_hash change
- [ ] GetBatch() for efficient bulk lookups
- [ ] Thread-safe with RWMutex
- [ ] Unit tests for cache behavior
- [ ] Graceful handling of missing metrics

## Files to Create

- pkg/search/metrics_cache.go (interface)
- pkg/search/metrics_cache_impl.go (implementation)
- pkg/search/metrics_cache_test.go (unit tests)

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bv-xbar.1 (Design HybridScorer interface)
- Blocked by: None

title: Design HybridScorer interface and weight configuration
description:
## вҡ пёҸ EDGE CASE: Zero Text Weight Warning

If text weight < 0.1, add WARNING (not error) in Validate():
```go
if w.TextRelevance < 0.1 {
    log.Printf("WARNING: text weight %.2f is very low; results may not match query", w.TextRelevance)
}
```

---

## Objective

Define the Go interface for HybridScorer and the weight configuration data structures. This is the foundational design that all other tasks depend on.

## Deliverables

### 1. HybridScorer Interface (pkg/search/hybrid_scorer.go)

```go
// HybridScorer computes hybrid search scores combining text relevance with graph metrics.
type HybridScorer interface {
    // Score computes the hybrid score for an issue given its text score and metrics.
    // Returns the final score and component breakdown.
    Score(issueID string, textScore float64) (HybridScore, error)
    
    // Configure sets the weights for hybrid scoring.
    Configure(weights Weights) error
    
    // GetWeights returns the current weight configuration.
    GetWeights() Weights
}

// HybridScore contains the final score and component breakdown for transparency.
type HybridScore struct {
    IssueID         string             `json:"issue_id"`
    FinalScore      float64            `json:"score"`
    TextScore       float64            `json:"text_score"`
    ComponentScores map[string]float64 `json:"component_scores,omitempty"`
}
```

### 2. Weight Configuration (pkg/search/weights.go)

```go
// Weights defines the relative importance of each ranking factor.
// All weights should sum to 1.0 for normalized scoring.
type Weights struct {
    TextRelevance float64 `json:"text"`     // Core search match quality
    PageRank      float64 `json:"pagerank"` // Graph centrality importance
    Status        float64 `json:"status"`   // Actionability (open > closed)
    Impact        float64 `json:"impact"`   // Blocker count normalized
    Priority      float64 `json:"priority"` // User-assigned priority
    Recency       float64 `json:"recency"`  // Temporal decay
}

// Validate checks that weights are valid (non-negative, sum to ~1.0).
func (w Weights) Validate() error

// Normalize scales weights to sum to 1.0.
func (w Weights) Normalize() Weights
```

### 3. Weight Presets (pkg/search/presets.go)

```go
// PresetName identifies a named weight configuration.
type PresetName string

const (
    PresetDefault       PresetName = "default"
    PresetBugHunting    PresetName = "bug-hunting"
    PresetSprintPlanning PresetName = "sprint-planning"
    PresetImpactFirst   PresetName = "impact-first"
    PresetTextOnly      PresetName = "text-only"
)

// GetPreset returns the weights for a named preset.
func GetPreset(name PresetName) (Weights, error)

// ListPresets returns all available preset names.
func ListPresets() []PresetName
```

## Design Considerations

1. **Extensibility**: Interface allows future implementations (e.g., ML-based scorer)
2. **Transparency**: ComponentScores breakdown enables debugging and user understanding
3. **Validation**: Weights validated to prevent invalid configurations
4. **JSON-friendly**: All structs have JSON tags for CLI/API output

## Normalization Functions

Document these in comments:
- StatusWeight: {open: 1.0, in_progress: 0.8, blocked: 0.5, closed: 0.1}
- PriorityWeight: P0вҶ’1.0, P1вҶ’0.8, P2вҶ’0.6, P3вҶ’0.4, P4вҶ’0.2
- RecencyWeight: exp(-days_since_update / 30)
- ImpactWeight: blocker_count / max_blocker_count (or 0.5 if max=0)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Interface defined with clear documentation
- [ ] Weight struct with validation
- [ ] All 5 presets defined with values matching design doc
- [ ] Unit tests for Weights.Validate() and Weights.Normalize()
- [ ] No implementation yet - just interfaces and types

## Files to Create

- pkg/search/hybrid_scorer.go (interface + types)
- pkg/search/weights.go (Weights struct + presets)
- pkg/search/weights_test.go (validation tests)

title: [EPIC] Graph-Aware Hybrid Search Ranking
description:
## Overview

Transform bv's search from pure text matching to graph-aware hybrid ranking that combines text relevance with PageRank, actionability, impact, priority, and recency.

## Problem Statement

bv positions itself as a "graph-aware triage engine" with precomputed metrics (PageRank, betweenness, critical path, cycles, HITS, eigenvector, k-core). However, search functionality completely ignores these metrics:

- **TUI semantic search**: Pure dot product of vector embeddings
- **CLI --robot-search**: Pure dot product of vector embeddings
- **Web FTS5 search**: Pure BM25 text relevance

This is a fundamental misalignment. A user searching "authentication bug" should see HIGH-IMPACT authentication bugs first, not just any text match.

## Solution: Hybrid Ranking Formula

```
hybrid_score = 0.40 Г— text_relevance   # Core search match
             + 0.20 Г— pagerank         # Graph importance
             + 0.15 Г— status_weight    # Actionability (open > closed)
             + 0.10 Г— impact_weight    # Blocker count normalized
             + 0.10 Г— priority_weight  # P0 > P4
             + 0.05 Г— recency_weight   # Temporal decay
```

## Key Design Decisions

1. **Backward Compatible**: Default mode remains text-only; hybrid is opt-in via --search-mode=hybrid
2. **Graceful Degradation**: Missing metrics fall back to text-only, no failures
3. **Configurable Weights**: Presets for common use cases (bug-hunting, sprint-planning, impact-first)
4. **Performance Target**: <2x overhead vs text-only for typical queries

## Weight Presets

- **default**: Balanced across all factors
- **bug-hunting**: Boost priority and impact (0.20 priority, 0.15 impact)
- **sprint-planning**: Boost actionability (0.25 status)
- **impact-first**: Boost graph importance (0.30 pagerank, 0.20 impact)
- **text-only**: Pure text search (1.0 text, 0.0 everything else)

## Success Criteria

1. High-PageRank issues rank higher for same text match
2. Open issues rank higher than closed for same text match
3. P0 bugs rank higher than P4 bugs for same text match
4. Preset switching works without code changes
5. Existing tests pass without modification
6. Performance within 2x of baseline

## Architecture

```
Search Request вҶ’ Text Search Layer вҶ’ MetricsCache вҶ’ HybridScorer вҶ’ Ranked Results
```

Components:
- **MetricsCache**: Lazy-loads PageRank, status, priority, blockerCount from graph analysis
- **HybridScorer**: Computes weighted sum with configurable weights
- **WeightPreset**: Named configurations for common use cases

## Scope

IN SCOPE:
- HybridScorer interface and implementation
- MetricsCache for search-time metric access
- CLI, TUI, and Web integration
- Weight presets and configuration
- Unit and integration tests
- Documentation updates

OUT OF SCOPE (future work):
- True semantic embeddings (sentence-transformers/OpenAI)
- Click-through feedback loop
- Query expansion/synonyms
- Machine-learned ranking

title: Fix startup delay: ready timeout fallback
description:
The TUI shows 'Initializing...' until a tea.WindowSizeMsg is received from the terminal. Some terminals delay this message, causing a random startup hang.

Fix: Add a 100ms timeout that sets ready=true with default dimensions if no WindowSizeMsg arrives.

title: [EPIC] Documentation & Testing Sprint - Q4 2025
description:
# Epic: Documentation & Testing Sprint

## Context & Motivation

We've added significant new functionality to beads_viewer over the past few weeks:

1. **Pure-Go SQLite with FTS5** - Switched from mattn/go-sqlite3 (CGO) to modernc.org/sqlite (pure-Go) for built-in FTS5 support without special build flags
2. **Legacy AGENTS.md Blurb Migration** - Detection and migration of old-format agent blurbs to versioned HTML-marker format
3. **Context-Specific Help System** - The `~` key shows quick reference help tailored to current view
4. **Tutorial System** - Comprehensive interactive tutorial with progress persistence
5. **Three-Pane History Layout** - Responsive history view with git commit correlation
6. **Static Pages Export Enhancements** - Vendored dependencies, closed issues inclusion, FTS5 search

These features are implemented but documentation and test coverage haven't kept pace. Since we don't have users yet, this is the ideal time to polish documentation (write it as if features always existed) and add comprehensive tests.

## Goals

1. **README Excellence**: Update README to document all features comprehensively, maintaining its exemplary quality
2. **In-App Help Completeness**: Ensure help modal (?) and context help (~) accurately cover all features/shortcuts
3. **Tutorial Depth**: Complete tutorial content for all views and advanced features
4. **Test Coverage**: Add unit tests for new functionality and integration tests for user workflows
5. **Self-Documentation**: Ensure the codebase itself (comments, doc.go files) matches implementation

## Success Criteria

- [ ] README documents all major features with examples
- [ ] Help modal keyboard shortcuts are accurate for all views
- [ ] Context help content exists and is accurate for all 15+ contexts
- [ ] Tutorial has complete content for all sections
- [ ] Unit tests cover legacy blurb migration edge cases
- [ ] E2E tests verify export workflows with new options
- [ ] All tests pass (`go test ./...`)

## Scope Boundary

This epic focuses on **documentation and testing**. It does NOT include:
- New feature development
- Bug fixes (unless discovered during testing)
- Performance optimization
- Refactoring

## Technical Notes

- README is ~120KB - changes should maintain existing quality/style
- Tutorial content lives in pkg/ui/tutorial.go as string constants
- Context help lives in pkg/ui/context_help.go
- E2E tests are in tests/e2e/ directory
- Use existing test patterns (table-driven, testdata files)

## Dependencies

This epic blocks nothing and is blocked by nothing - it's a polish/stabilization effort.

Unblocking E2E test tasks (bv-4hds, bv-ib7j, bv-qnlb) and final review (bv-zh3l).

### Implementation

2. Calculate layout: board columns get 60%, detail gets 40%
3. Tab toggles detail panel visibility
4. Reuse existing viewport/renderer for markdown content
5. Update detail content on card selection change

### Key Bindings
- `Tab`: Toggle detail panel
- `j/k`: Navigate cards (detail auto-updates)

### Acceptance Criteria

title: Board: Remove Width Cap, Use Full Screen
description:
## Bug: Arbitrary Width Cap Wastes Screen

### Current Code
```go
minColWidth := 28
maxColWidth := 60  // WHY?!
```

On a 200-column terminal with 4 columns, max used = 240.
**80+ columns are wasted.**

### Fix

Remove the maxColWidth cap:
```go
// Available width after gaps
availableWidth := width - (gaps * 2)

// Distribute evenly, with minimum
colWidth := availableWidth / numCols
if colWidth < minColWidth {
    colWidth = minColWidth
}
// NO MAX CAP - use all available space
```

### Behavior by Terminal Width

| Width | Behavior |
|-------|----------|
| < 80 | Board may be cramped but functional |
| 80-100 | 4 columns, minimal cards (current-like) |
| 100-140 | 4 columns with more card content |
| 140-200 | Wide cards, optional detail panel |
| 200+ | Full detail panel by default |

### Narrow Terminal Handling
On very narrow terminals (< 80), columns will be cramped but should still render. No need for vertical stacking - that's a different UX entirely.

If terminal is too narrow for minColWidth Г— numCols, allow horizontal scrolling OR just let columns overlap/truncate. Users with narrow terminals accept compromises.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Remove maxColWidth = 60 cap
- [ ] Board fills available width
- [ ] minColWidth = 28 preserved
- [ ] Works correctly at 80-300+ columns
- [ ] No horizontal dead space on wide terminals
close_reason: Removed maxColWidth=60 cap, board now uses full screen width

title: [EPIC] Board View Overhaul
description:
# Epic: Board View Overhaul

## Executive Summary
The Kanban board view is currently the **weakest view** in the application. It wastes screen real estate, shows minimal information, has no detail preview capability, and lacks features that every other view has.

## Current Problems

### 1. Width Cap Destroys Usability
```go
maxColWidth := 60
```
On a 200-column terminal with 4 columns, we use max 240 chars. **40% of screen wasted.**

### 2. No Detail Panel
Unlike list view (which has split-pane detail), board view has NO way to see bead content without leaving. Agent workflows require seeing descriptions/body.

### 3. Anemic Card Content
Cards show only:
- Type icon + Priority + ID (truncated)
- Title (truncated to ~20 chars)  
- Metadata chips

**Missing**: Description preview, body markdown, dependency names, blocked-by info, labels as names.

### 4. No Markdown Rendering
Glamour renders markdown everywhere else. Board cards are plain text.

### 5. Fixed 5-Line Card Height
```go
cardHeight := 5
```
Even with 60 lines of terminal height, cards stay tiny.

### 6. No Swimlane Options
Hardcoded to status columns. Can't group by:
- Priority (P0|P1|P2|P3)
- Type (bug|feature|task|epic)
- Label
- Epic parent

### 7. Limited Keyboard Navigation
Only h/l/j/k, G, Ctrl+D/U, Enter.
Missing: column jump (1-4), search, filter, inline detail.

### 8. No Dependency Visualization
Can't see which cards are blocked/blocking without leaving.

### 9. No Inline Actions
Can't change status or priority from board.

### 10. Empty Columns Waste Space
Empty columns take same width as full columns.

## Vision

### Adaptive Layout
- Use ALL horizontal space
- Show detail panel on wide screens (like list view does)
- Variable card height based on content
- Collapsible empty columns

### Rich Cards with Markdown
- Description preview rendered with Glamour
- Full labels shown (not just count)
- Dependency graph snippet
- Blocked-by indicator with bead titles

### Swimlane Flexibility
- Toggle grouping: status/priority/type/label/epic
- Save preference per project

### Detail Panel Integration
- Tab toggles detail panel for selected card
- Full markdown body rendering
- Same quality as list view detail

### Quick Actions
- `m`: Move to next status
- `M`: Move to previous status
- `1-4`: Jump to column
- `s`: Switch swimlane mode
- `d`: Toggle inline detail expansion

## Success Criteria
- Board view as useful as list view for understanding work
- No wasted screen space on wide terminals
- Agent can see full bead content without leaving board
- Swimlane flexibility for different workflows
close_reason: Board View Overhaul complete: Detail Panel/Split View (bv-r6kh), Inline Card Expansion (bv-i3ii), Adaptive Column Width (bv-ggmc - already implemented). Board view now supports expanded card details, responsive column widths, and swimlane navigation. All related tasks closed.

title: Pages export README: add live URL and meaningful insights
close_reason: Fixed: README now includes computed live GitHub Pages URL at the top, Top Priorities with reasons and impact scores, Critical Bottlenecks table, Cycles as structural bugs, Graph Analysis insights with density interpretation, and compact status summary. Replaced useless count tables with actionable intelligence.

title: History: Change Impact Analysis
description:
## Overview
Answer: 'If I modify this file, what open beads might be affected?'

## Why Agents Need This (Critical)
Before making changes, an agent MUST know:
- Are there open beads that touch these files?
- Could my changes conflict with in-progress work?
- What context should I be aware of?

## Implementation

### Impact Query
```go
func (h *HistoryReport) ImpactAnalysis(files []string) ImpactResult

type ImpactResult struct {
    AffectedBeads []AffectedBead
    RiskLevel     string  // low/medium/high
    Warnings      []string
}

type AffectedBead struct {
    BeadID       string
    Status       string
    OverlapFiles []string  // which of the input files this bead touches
    LastActivity time.Time
    Relevance    int       // how relevant (based on recency, file overlap %)
}
```

### Display in History
When agent is about to modify files:
```
вҡЎ Impact Analysis: auth/token.go, auth/session.go

   рҹ”ҙ High Risk - Open beads on these files:
   вҖў bt-123 (in_progress): Token refresh
     в””в”Җ Overlaps: auth/token.go (3 recent commits)
   
   рҹҹЎ Medium Risk - Recently closed:
   вҖў bt-089 (closed 2d ago): OAuth foundation  
     в””в”Җ Overlaps: both files
   
   Recommendation: Check bt-123 before modifying auth/token.go
```

### Robot Command (PRIMARY USE CASE)
`bv robot impact auth/token.go auth/session.go`

Returns JSON that agent can use to decide whether to proceed or check context first.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Impact analysis callable from robot command
- [ ] Risk level computed based on open beads
- [ ] Recency factored into relevance
- [ ] Clear actionable output for agents
close_reason: Closed

title: [EPIC] History View Overhaul
description:
# Epic: History View Overhaul

## Executive Summary
The current History view is functional but underwhelming. It shows bead-to-commit correlations in a basic two-pane layout but fails to leverage the rich data available or provide the flexibility users need to understand project activity.

## Current State Problems

### 1. Single Perspective Only
- **Bead-centric only**: Can only browse beads вҶ’ see their commits
- **No git-centric view**: Cannot browse commits вҶ’ see related beads
- The \`CommitIndex\` exists in the data but isn't exposed in UI

### 2. Wasted Screen Real Estate
- Fixed 40/60 split regardless of terminal width (even on 200+ column displays)
- No adaptive layout for wide screens
- Detail panel truncates content when there's room for more
- No visual timeline despite having rich timestamp data

### 3. Poor Information Density
- Bead list shows: status icon, ID, title, commit count
- **Missing**: priority, labels, creation date, assignee, activity sparkline
- Commit list shows basic info but no visual confidence indicator

### 4. Missing Features
- **Lifecycle events hidden**: BeadHistory.Events has created/claimed/closed but UI ignores them
- **Author filter broken**: 'f' key just shows a hint, doesn't actually filter
- **No search**: Can't find commits by message, SHA, or files
- **No date filtering**: Can't ask "what happened last week?"
- **Statistics hidden**: HistoryReport.Stats exists but isn't displayed

### 5. UX Issues
- Poor keyboard discoverability (no persistent hints)
- No way to expand commits to see full file list
- Can't drill into a file to see all beads that touched it

## Vision

### Dual-Perspective Design
Users can toggle between:
1. **Git-centric**: Browse commits chronologically, see connected beads
2. **Bead-centric**: Browse beads, see their commit history with timeline

### Three-Pane Layout for Wide Screens
\`\`\`
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ HISTORY VIEW                    [v] Git  Bead в”Ӯ Stats: 47 beads вҖў 156 commits в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ LEFT PANE          в”Ӯ MIDDLE PANE        в”Ӯ RIGHT PANE                         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ (Primary list)     в”Ӯ (Relationships)    в”Ӯ (Details)                          в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ j/k:navigate  v:toggle-view  /:search  f:filter  t:timeline  y:copy  ?:help в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
\`\`\`

### Rich Visualizations
- Timeline panel showing activity over time
- Confidence bars (not just percentages)
- Activity sparklines per bead
- Lifecycle event markers

## Success Criteria
- Users can answer "what commits relate to this bead?" (current)
- Users can answer "what beads were affected by this commit?" (NEW)
- Users can answer "what happened this week?" (NEW)
- Users can answer "who has been working on what?" (NEW)
- Wide-screen users see more useful information
- All interactions feel Stripe-level polished

## Technical Approach
- Refactor HistoryModel to support dual view modes
- Add CommitListModel for git-centric navigation
- Create TimelinePanel component
- Implement adaptive layout based on terminal width
- Add proper search/filter infrastructure

## Dependencies
Blocks tutorial content that describes history view.
close_reason: History View Overhaul complete: Git-Centric View Mode (bv-tl3n), Temporal Causality View (bv-j74w), Session Timeline Integration (bv-pr1l), View Mode Toggle Animation (bv-kvlx), Three-Pane Layout E2E tests (bv-4hds), README documentation (bv-gxik). History view now shows bead-to-commit correlations with timeline panel, supports git-first navigation, and has comprehensive test coverage.

title: [EPIC] AGENTS.md Auto-Integration
description:
# Epic: AGENTS.md Auto-Integration

## Overview
Automatically detect AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md files and offer to append beads_viewer usage instructions, enabling AI coding agents to leverage the tool effectively without manual setup.

## Goals
1. **Zero-friction AI integration** - agents immediately know how to use beads
2. **Consistent best practices** - all projects get the same high-quality instructions
3. **Respect user autonomy** - never modify files without consent, remember preferences

## Key Requirements
- Detect AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md in working directory
- Check for existing blurb via marker comment
- Prompt user with Accept/Decline/Don't Ask Again
- Store per-project preferences
- Safely append blurb with proper formatting

## User Flow
1. User runs `bv` in a directory with AGENTS.md (but no blurb)
2. Modal appears: "Add beads_viewer instructions to AGENTS.md?"
3. User chooses: [Yes] [No] [No, don't ask again for this project]
4. If Yes: blurb appended, success message shown
5. If No/Never: preference stored, never asked again for this project

## Technical Approach
- Marker comment: `<!-- bv-agent-instructions-v1 -->`
- Config storage: ~/.config/bv/agent-prompts/<project-hash>.json
- Trigger: on startup, after initial load, before main loop

## Success Criteria
- AI agents using the project immediately understand beads workflow
- Users never feel pestered by the prompt
- File modifications are safe and reversible

## Dependencies
This epic can proceed independently but shares some UI patterns with Tutorial System.
close_reason: All tasks completed: detection, preferences, file ops, modal, integration trigger, and tests

title: [EPIC] Interactive Tutorial System
description:
# Epic: Interactive Tutorial System

## Overview
Create a comprehensive, visually impressive interactive tutorial that walks users through beads_viewer's features, philosophy, and optimal usage patterns. The tutorial should be the gold standard for CLI tool onboarding.

## Goals
1. **Reduce time-to-productivity** for new users
2. **Showcase advanced features** users might not discover organically
3. **Establish best practices** for both human users and AI agents
4. **Demonstrate the tool's polish** - first impressions matter

## Key Requirements
- Triggered by CapsLock key (full tutorial) or double-tap CapsLock (context help)
- Renders markdown content via Glamour with our theme
- Multi-page navigation with progress tracking
- Real-world scenarios and concrete examples
- No gimmicky animations - sophisticated, professional feel

## Success Criteria
- New user can become productive within 5 minutes of tutorial
- All major features are covered with practical examples
- Tutorial itself demonstrates the quality/polish of the tool
- Context help provides immediate, relevant assistance

## Technical Approach
- TutorialModel as new BubbleTea component
- go:embed for markdown content files
- Reuse existing Glamour rendering infrastructure
- Timer-based double-tap detection for CapsLock

## Dependencies
This epic blocks the AGENTS.md integration epic (shares tutorial infrastructure).
close_reason: All tutorial system tasks complete: TutorialModel infrastructure (bv-d6vi), Glamour rendering (bv-lb0h), keyboard navigation (bv-wdsd), progress persistence (bv-1lel), main model integration (bv-8y31), UI chrome (bv-h6rq), help modal entry (bv-0trk), and all content tasks (Introduction, Core Concepts, Views & Navigation, Advanced Features, AI Agent Integration, Real-World Workflows). 24 tutorial tests pass. Tutorial accessible via backtick key or Space from help modal.

title: cmd/bv: use velocity helper in --robot-insights (avoid ComputeTriage)
description:
## Goal
Remove the redundant `analysis.ComputeTriage()` call from the robot-insights path.

## Constraints
- Robot insights JSON shape must not change.
- Velocity values must match existing behavior.
close_reason: Replaced ComputeTriage call with direct ComputeProjectVelocity call in --robot-insights path. This avoids expensive graph analysis when only velocity data is needed.

title: Perf: compute triage unblocks map in O(E) (no per-node traversal)
description:
## Problem
Triage currently builds an вҖңunblocks mapвҖқ by calling `analyzer.computeUnblocks(issueID)` for every open issue.

This can devolve to O(VВ·E) and becomes expensive on large graphs.

## Goal
Compute the same unblocks map in ~O(E):
- Precompute each issueвҖҷs count of open blockers (only existing blocking deps).
- Build blocker -> dependents adjacency lists.
- Dependent is unblocked by closing blocker B iff openBlockerCount[dependent] == 1 and B is that blocker.

## Correctness requirements
- Only blocking deps count (`dep.Type.IsBlocking()`).
- Missing blockers do not block.
- Duplicate deps must not double-count.
- Unblocks lists must be deterministically sorted.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] O(E) implementation matches reference algorithm on fixtures.
- [ ] No triage output changes.
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes.
close_reason: O(E) unblocks map now used by triage (buildUnblocksMap) and covered by perf_invariants_test; semantics match reference (blocking-only, missing/dup deps). go test ./... now passes after restoring Phase1 TopologicalOrder in analysis.

title: Perf baseline: define invariants + measurement protocol
description:
## Goal
Before we change any code, define what вҖңstartup timeвҖқ means and add guardrails ensuring **outputs do not change**.

## Why
Perf refactors are safest when we can quickly prove they are semantics-preserving. This work creates the safety net.

## Scope
- Define benchmark commands/datasets for:
  - TUI startup (Phase 1 ready + Phase 2 completion)
  - `bv --robot-triage`, `bv --robot-plan`, `bv --robot-insights`
- Add *functional* invariance tests (NOT wall-time assertions) for:
  - unblocks map correctness
  - velocity computation
  - (optional) triage recommendation stability on a small fixture

## Principles
- No flaky time assertions.
- Prefer fixed clocks (`now`) + deterministic fixtures.
- Prefer вҖңfast vs slow referenceвҖқ equivalence tests for algorithmic changes.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Benchmark protocol exists (commands + datasets).
- [ ] Invariance tests exist for unblocks + velocity at minimum.
- [ ] Tests are deterministic and fast.
close_reason: Invariance tests done: invariance_test.go (15 unblocks + 7 velocity + 1 triage sanity) and perf_invariants_test.go. Benchmark protocol documentation deferred to bv-runn.3.

title: Perf tests: invariance suite for unblocks + velocity (+ triage sanity)
description:
## Goal
Add deterministic tests guaranteeing the planned perf refactors keep identical semantics.

## Unblocks invariance
Create a deterministic synthetic issue set that includes:
- blocking vs non-blocking deps
- missing dependency IDs
- duplicated deps
- mixed statuses (open/blocked/in_progress/closed)

Test strategy:
- Implement a slow вҖңreferenceвҖқ unblocks computation in *_test.go mirroring current behavior.
- Implement (or later call) the optimized unblocks computation.
- Assert equivalence for every issue ID (including deterministic sorted order of unblocks lists).

## Velocity invariance
- Use a fixed `now` and known `ClosedAt` timestamps.
- Assert the exported helper matches the existing triage velocity logic.

## Optional triage sanity
- For a small fixture, assert recommended IDs/order matches a golden list.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Unblocks tests cover edge cases (missing blockers, duplicated deps, non-blocking deps).
- [ ] Velocity test uses fixed `now` and is stable.
- [ ] `go test ./...` remains fast and deterministic.
close_reason: Added invariance tests (unblocks + velocity) and fixed Phase1 topo order regression; go test ./... passes.

title: Perf: remove dead GetExecutionPlan call in triage
description:
## Problem
`pkg/analysis/triage.go` calls `analyzer.GetExecutionPlan()` and discards the result.

This is pure waste:
- It can be expensive on large graphs.
- It does not affect triage output.

## Proposed change
Delete the unused call.

## Safety
`GetExecutionPlan()` should be pure computation with no side effects.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Unused call removed.
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes.
- [ ] Triage output unchanged on fixtures.
close_reason: Removed unused analyzer.GetExecutionPlan call from ComputeTriageFromAnalyzer (pure dead work). go test ./... passes; triage output unchanged.

title: Perf: remove unused dependents precompute in impact scoring
description:
## Problem
`pkg/analysis/priority.go` builds + sorts a dependents map labeled as an optimization, but it is never used.

This adds avoidable overhead every time impact scores are computed.

## Proposed change
Delete the unused map and sorting.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Unused precompute removed.
- [ ] Output unchanged (scores, ordering).
- [ ] `go test ./...` passes.
close_reason: Already completed in commit c8378cb which removed the unused dependents precompute from ComputeImpactScoresFromStats and simplified ComputeRiskSignals

title: Perf: compute velocity without running full triage
description:
## Problem
`--robot-insights` currently calls `analysis.ComputeTriage()` solely to extract `ProjectHealth.Velocity`.

That forces redundant work (new analyzer + triage scoring) unrelated to insights output.

## Goal
Expose velocity computation as a lightweight helper that does not require graph construction.

## Output invariance
Velocity numbers must match the existing triage velocity calculation for the same `now`.
close_reason: Already accomplished via bv-runn.7 (exported ComputeProjectVelocity) and bv-runn.8 (wired robot-insights to use it). Velocity helper works without full triage.

title: analysis: export velocity computation helper (shared)
description:
## Goal
Make velocity computation reusable without going through ComputeTriage.

## Proposed API (example)
`analysis.ComputeProjectVelocity(issues []model.Issue, now time.Time, weeks int) *analysis.ProjectVelocity`

## Requirements
- Must preserve exact semantics used by triage today.
- Must be testable with a fixed `now`.
close_reason: Exported ComputeProjectVelocity function from triage.go. Added comprehensive docstring. Exact semantics preserved - just capitalized the function name.

title: Perf: speed up startup by removing redundant work (no output changes)
description:
## Goal
Make bv measurably faster and more scalable at startup by removing *waste* (redundant computation, dead work, avoidable O(VВ·E) passes), **without changing any results/output**.

This is specifically about:
- TUI startup responsiveness (Model construction, Phase 1 readiness)
- Robot-mode command latency for analysis-heavy outputs
- CPU + allocation efficiency when graphs get large (hundreds/thousands of issues)

## Context / Why this matters
bv is intentionally a вҖңgraph sidecarвҖқ for Beads. As projects grow, the analysis pipeline becomes the dominant cost, and the *worst* kind of cost is redundant work that produces identical results.

We already observed several clear cases of:
- computing values and discarding them
- computing the same derived data multiple times from the same inputs
- per-node work that can be replaced with a linear pass

These are вҖңfree winsвҖқ: they improve speed and reduce CPU without changing behavior.

## Non-goals
- Do **not** change metric definitions, scoring weights, or recommendation ordering.
- Do **not** skip metrics or change what gets computed (unless explicitly gated behind an opt-in flag AND the default remains identical).
- Do **not** change the shape of robot JSON outputs.
- Do **not** change UI behavior/interaction.

## Out of scope (explicitly)
- Any product/UX changes.
- The export wizard / huh dependency decisions.

## Success criteria (high-level)
- `go test ./...` passes.
- Output invariants hold on fixtures: triage recommendations/unblocks/velocity remain identical.
- Measurable reduction in startup CPU/allocations on a вҖңlarge graphвҖқ fixture.
design:
## Candidate improvements (no output change)
1) Remove dead work in triage:
   - `pkg/analysis/triage.go` calls `analyzer.GetExecutionPlan()` and discards it.

2) Remove unused precompute in impact scoring:
   - `pkg/analysis/priority.go` builds/sorts a dependents map that is never used.

3) Avoid computing full triage just to extract velocity:
   - `cmd/bv/main.go` uses `analysis.ComputeTriage()` inside `--robot-insights` only to read `ProjectHealth.Velocity`.

4) Reduce asymptotic cost of вҖңunblocks mapвҖқ construction:
   - current triage builds unblocks by calling a per-node graph traversal.
   - can compute equivalently in ~O(E) by counting open blockers + grouping dependents.

5) Eliminate duplicated analyzer/stats construction in the TUI:
   - `pkg/ui/model.go` builds analyzer+stats, then calls `analysis.ComputeTriage()` which builds another analyzer+stats.

## Risks / tricky bits
- Must preserve dependency semantics:
  - only blocking deps matter (`dep.Type.IsBlocking()`)
  - missing blockers must not block (graceful degradation)
  - duplicated deps in JSONL must not double-count (graph edges are unique)
- Determinism: stable ordering of IDs in outputs.
- Avoid introducing time-based flaky tests; focus on functional invariants.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] No robot JSON output shape changes (field names/types).
- [ ] Existing unit tests remain green; new invariance tests added where appropriate.
- [ ] Documented benchmark protocol to measure improvements.
- [ ] Demonstrated CPU/allocation reduction on at least one large synthetic fixture.
close_reason: All core P1 performance optimizations complete: O(E) unblocks, TUI reuses analyzer/stats, velocity helper, dead code removed, invariance tests added. Remaining items are P2 docs and P3 optional optimizations.

title: Fix govulncheck findings (Go 1.25.5 + x/net bump)
description:
govulncheck (via: go run golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest ./...) reports:
- GO-2025-4175 + GO-2025-4155 in stdlib crypto/x509 fixed in go1.25.5 (current toolchain: go1.25.4)
- GO-2025-3595 in golang.org/x/net fixed in v0.38.0 (current v0.33.0)

Acceptance:
- Add toolchain directive go1.25.5 and update CI to use Go 1.25.5.
- Bump golang.org/x/net to at least v0.38.0 (and tidy).
- go test ./... passes.
- govulncheck scan no longer flags these vulnerabilities.
close_reason: Added toolchain directive go1.25.5 + bumped CI to Go 1.25.5, upgraded golang.org/x/net to v0.38.0, and verified govulncheck reports no vulnerabilities.

title: Robot commands must not emit parse warnings to stderr
description:
Robot-mode commands (e.g. --robot-triage/--robot-plan) must keep stdout JSON parseable even when .beads/*.jsonl contains malformed lines. Currently loader/sprints parsing can emit warnings to stderr, which breaks toolchains that treat stderr as fatal.

Acceptance:
- In robot mode, malformed JSONL lines do not produce stderr output for issues + sprints parsing.
- Existing interactive behavior can still warn (non-robot).
- Add E2E tests covering malformed issues.jsonl and sprints.jsonl.
close_reason: Robot-mode now sets BV_ROBOT for --robot-* flags; loaders suppress parse warnings when BV_ROBOT=1. Added E2E tests to ensure malformed issues/sprints lines do not emit stderr while keeping stdout JSON parseable.

title: Integrate glamour markdown renderer for rich TUI display
description:
Integrate charmbracelet/glamour for rendering markdown content in the TUI.

## Scope
1. Add glamour dependency to go.mod
2. Create reusable markdown rendering component in pkg/ui
3. Integrate with existing Theme system for consistent styling
4. Apply to:
   - Issue description in detail view
   - Comments rendering
   - Acceptance criteria display
   - Notes/design docs preview

## Benefits
- Rich markdown preview (bold, italic, lists, code blocks, links)
- Consistent with Charm ecosystem (Bubble Tea, Lipgloss)
- Improved readability for issue content

## Implementation Notes
- Glamour supports custom styles - align with bv Theme
- Consider terminal width for word wrapping
- Handle edge cases (empty content, very long content)
close_reason: Implemented theme-aware markdown renderer using glamour with custom styles matching bv Theme system. Created MarkdownRenderer component with Dracula/light mode support, syntax highlighting, and adaptive colors. Integrated into detail view for consistent visual styling.

title: Infrastructure: Test fixture generator for graph topologies
description:
Create a test fixture generator for various graph topologies.

## Purpose
Many tests need graph fixtures with specific properties. Create a generator that produces deterministic test data.

## Topologies to Generate
1. **chain(n)** - Linear A->B->C...
