title: Robot status output shows pending/empty states
description:
Problem:
- `bv --robot-plan` and `bv --robot-priority` emit `.status` with `state: pending` for all metrics and empty states for `KCore/Articulation/Slack`.
- This breaks the robot JSON contract and makes agents think metrics are still running even though bv exits immediately.

Repro:
- `bv --robot-plan | jq .status`
- `bv --robot-priority | jq .status`

Expected:
- Status should be deterministic and reflect what was actually computed for the command (`computed`/`approx`/`timeout`/`skipped`), with no `pending`/empty states.

Acceptance:
- `--robot-plan` and `--robot-priority` status fields never contain `pending`/empty states at process exit.
- If metrics are intentionally not computed for a fast path, they are marked `skipped` with a reason.
notes:
Implemented fix: `--robot-plan` and `--robot-priority` now wait for analysis completion before emitting `.status`, eliminating `pending`/empty states.

- `--robot-plan` skips expensive centrality metrics by default and marks them `skipped` with reason `not computed for --robot-plan`; still computes fast structural signals (k-core/articulation/slack) for a complete status contract.
- Tests updated to enforce no `pending`/empty states in robot outputs.

title: Integrate force-graph library with static viewer
description:
# Integrate force-graph Library ## Context Add the force-graph library to render dependencies as an interactive graph. ## Requirements ### 1. Add Library ```html ``` ### 2. Initialize Graph Container ```html
``` ### 3. Load Data and Render ```javascript const graphData = { nodes: issues.map(i => ({ id: i.id, name: i.title, status: i.status, priority: i.priority, val: calculateNodeSize(i) // PageRank or blocker count })), links: dependencies.map(d => ({ source: d.issue_id, target: d.depends_on_id })) }; const Graph = ForceGraph() (document.getElementById('graph-container')) .graphData(graphData) .nodeId('id') .nodeLabel('name') .nodeColor(node => statusColors[node.status]) .nodeVal('val') .linkDirectionalArrowLength(6) .linkDirectionalArrowRelPos(1) .onNodeClick(handleNodeClick) .onNodeHover(handleNodeHover); ``` ### 4. Node Sizing by Impact ```javascript function calculateNodeSize(issue) { // Use WASM-computed metrics if available if (WASM_READY) { const idx = NODE_MAP.get(issue.id); const pr = GRAPH.pagerank(0.85, 100); return 5 + pr[idx] * 50; // Scale to reasonable size } // Fallback: use blocker count from SQLite return 5 + (issue.blocker_count || 0) * 2; } ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] force-graph loads without errors - [ ] All issues rendered as nodes - [ ] Dependencies shown as directed edges - [ ] Smooth pan/zoom interaction - [ ] Basic node coloring by status - [ ] Performance: 500 nodes at 60fps

title: Interactive Graph Visualization with Force-Graph
description:
# Interactive Graph Visualization with Force-Graph

## Vision
Transform the static viewer from a simple table into an **immersive, interactive graph exploration experience**. Using [force-graph](https://github.com/vasturiano/force-graph) (WebGL-accelerated), users can explore their dependency graph visually, see patterns at a glance, and perform what-if analysis with beautiful animations.

## Why This Matters

### Current State: Tables Are Limiting
- Users scroll through lists, mentally reconstructing relationships
- Hard to see the "big picture" of work
- Bottlenecks and cycles are numbers, not visible patterns
- No way to explore "what if I close this?"

### Future State: Visual Graph Intelligence
- **See the shape** of your work at a glance
- **Spot bottlenecks** as large, central nodes
- **Find cycles** as visual loops
- **Explore what-if** by clicking nodes and watching cascades
- **Understand priority** through size, color, position

## Core Features

### 1. Force-Directed Dependency Graph
- Nodes = issues, sized by impact (PageRank/blocker count)
- Edges = dependencies with directional arrows
- Colors: green (closed), blue (open), orange (blocked), red (critical)
- Smooth WebGL rendering for 1000+ nodes at 60fps
- Click node to focus, hover for tooltip, drag to reorganize

### 2. Visual What-If Analysis
- Click any open issue to "simulate close"
- Watch downstream unblocks animate (ripple effect)
- See parallelization gain visually
- Color transitions: blockedвҶ’unblocked nodes glow green
- Reset button to restore original state

### 3. Smart Clustering
- Automatic grouping by label using force clustering
- Label "galaxies" with distinct colors
- Cross-label edges show integration points
- Expand/collapse clusters
- Mini-map for navigation

### 4. Critical Path Highlighting
- Toggle to highlight longest dependency chain
- Animate path traversal
- Show "time to completion" along path
- Compare multiple paths

### 5. Time Travel Animation
- Scrubber to move through git history
- Watch graph grow/shrink over time
- See issues appear, get worked on, close
- Playback controls (play, pause, speed)

## Technical Architecture

### Library Choice: force-graph
- **Why**: WebGL-accelerated, handles 10k+ nodes, excellent API
- **CDN**: `https://unpkg.com/force-graph`
- **Alternative**: 3d-force-graph for even cooler 3D view
- **Fallback**: vis-network if WebGL unavailable

### Data Flow
```
SQLite вҶ’ JS вҶ’ WASM (metrics) вҶ’ force-graph (render)
                вҶ“
         User interaction
                вҶ“
         WASM (what-if) вҶ’ Animated update
```

### Performance Targets
- Initial render: <500ms for 500 nodes
- What-if calculation + animation: <100ms
- Smooth 60fps during interaction
- Memory: <100MB for 1000-node graph

## Visual Design Principles

1. **Clarity**: Important things are bigger, brighter
2. **Consistency**: Same colors mean same things everywhere
3. **Delight**: Smooth animations, satisfying interactions
4. **Accessibility**: Colorblind-safe palette, keyboard nav

## Success Metrics
- Users can identify top bottleneck in <5 seconds
- What-if takes <2 clicks to understand impact
- 90%+ of users prefer graph view over table for exploration
- Zero WebGL crashes/freezes

## Dependencies
- WASM Graph Engine (bv-5mgw) for metrics
- SQLite viewer infrastructure
- Static export with dependency data

## References
- force-graph: https://github.com/vasturiano/force-graph
- 3d-force-graph: https://github.com/vasturiano/3d-force-graph
- D3 force simulation: https://d3js.org/d3-force
- Graph viz inspiration: https://observablehq.com/@d3/force-directed-graph
close_reason: Static viewer graph view shipped: ForceGraph (#/graph) + WASM metrics + node interactions; export works; added COEP-safe d3 loading

title: Light Mode Terminal: Low Color Contrast Makes Text Unreadable
description:
## Problem Statement

The bv color scheme is very hard to read on light terminal backgrounds. The 'dimmed' text (Subtext, Muted colors) is nearly invisible on white backgrounds, making the application unusable for users with light terminal themes.

## User Impact

**Severity: High (P1)**
- Effectively blocks users with light terminal themes
- Affects readability of secondary information (dimmed text, hints)
- Reported by @mikker in GitHub Issue #17

## Root Cause Analysis

**Location:** `pkg/ui/theme.go:41-64`

The theme uses `lipgloss.AdaptiveColor` which provides Light/Dark values:

```go
Subtext: lipgloss.AdaptiveColor{Light: "#999999", Dark: "#BFBFBF"}
Muted:   lipgloss.AdaptiveColor{Light: "#888888", Dark: "#6272A4"}
```

The Light values are too light for white backgrounds:
- `#999999` on white = contrast ratio ~2.8:1 (WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text)
- `#888888` on white = contrast ratio ~3.5:1 (still fails WCAG AA)

While lipgloss attempts auto-detection of terminal background color, the chosen Light values are simply too faint regardless.

## Proposed Solution

### Fix 1: Improve Light mode color contrast

```go
// Improved light mode colors (WCAG AA compliant)
Subtext: lipgloss.AdaptiveColor{Light: "#666666", Dark: "#BFBFBF"} // ~6:1 contrast
Muted:   lipgloss.AdaptiveColor{Light: "#555555", Dark: "#6272A4"} // ~7:1 contrast
```

All colors need review:
- Primary: #7D56F4 on white = ~4.6:1 (barely passes)
- Secondary: #555555 is good
- Status colors need verification

### Fix 2 (Optional): Add explicit theme flag

```bash
bv --theme=light   # Force light mode colors
bv --theme=dark    # Force dark mode colors
bv --theme=auto    # Default: auto-detect (current behavior)
```

This helps when auto-detection fails or user preference differs.

## Color Audit Required

All AdaptiveColor values need contrast verification:

| Color | Current Light | Contrast | Suggested Light | New Contrast |
|-------|--------------|----------|-----------------|--------------|
| Subtext | #999999 | 2.8:1 вқҢ | #666666 | 6:1 вң“ |
| Muted | #888888 | 3.5:1 вқҢ | #555555 | 7:1 вң“ |
| Secondary | #555555 | 7:1 вң“ | Keep | - |
| Open | #00A800 | 2.7:1 вқҢ | #007700 | 4.6:1 вң“ |
| Closed | #555555 | 7:1 вң“ | Keep | - |
| Border | #DDDDDD | 1.4:1 вқҢ | #AAAAAA | 2.3:1 (border OK) |

## Test Plan

1. Manual test on iTerm2 with Solarized Light theme
2. Manual test on macOS Terminal with default light theme
3. Verify all text elements are readable
4. Verify status colors are distinguishable
5. Consider adding screenshot tests

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All text readable on white terminal background
- [ ] Status colors visually distinguishable on light background
- [ ] Existing dark mode appearance unchanged
- [ ] Optional: --theme flag for explicit control
- [ ] No WCAG AA violations for text colors (4.5:1 minimum)

## References

- GitHub Issue #17: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer/issues/17
- WCAG Color Contrast Guidelines: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html
- lipgloss AdaptiveColor: https://github.com/charmbracelet/lipgloss#adaptive-colors
close_reason: Improved light mode color contrast for WCAG AA compliance - darker Subtext, Muted, Open, InProgress, Feature, Task, Chore, and Border colors

title: Labels Missing from Detail View Panel
description:
## Problem Statement

Labels are NOT displayed in the detail view panel, even though they are a critical organizational feature. Users who rely on labels for categorization (e.g., `repo:web`, `area:backend`) cannot see this information when viewing issue details.

## User Impact

**Severity: High (P1)**
- Labels completely hidden in the most detailed view
- Users must use list view (width > 140px) to see labels at all
- Particularly problematic for multi-repo workflows using labels
- Reported by @neilpoulin in GitHub Issue #14 with screenshot

## Root Cause Analysis

**Location:** `pkg/ui/model.go:2401-2498` (`updateViewportContent` function)

The detail view renders:
- Title with type icon вң“
- Meta table (ID, Status, Priority, Assignee, Created) вң“
- Graph Analysis metrics вң“
- Description, Acceptance Criteria, Notes вң“
- Dependency tree вң“
- Comments вң“
- History (if loaded) вң“

**Missing:** Labels are never rendered\!

Labels ARE rendered in other contexts:
- List view delegate (`pkg/ui/delegate.go:97-99`) - but only when width > 140
- Copy to clipboard (`pkg/ui/model.go:2909-2910`)
- Kanban board (`pkg/ui/board.go:469-474`)

But `updateViewportContent()` simply doesn't include them.

## Proposed Solution

Add labels after the meta table, around line 2433:

```go
// After meta table, before Graph Analysis
if len(item.Labels) > 0 {
    sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("**Labels:** %s\n\n", strings.Join(item.Labels, ", ")))
}
```

Or integrate into meta table as an additional row:

```go
// Extended meta table
sb.WriteString("| ID | Status | Priority | Assignee | Created | Labels |\n")
sb.WriteString("|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n")
labelsStr := strings.Join(item.Labels, ", ")
if labelsStr == "" { labelsStr = "-" }
sb.WriteString(fmt.Sprintf("| **%s** | **%s** | %s | @%s | %s | %s |\n\n",
    item.ID, strings.ToUpper(string(item.Status)), GetPriorityIcon(item.Priority),
    item.Assignee, item.CreatedAt.Format("2006-01-02"), labelsStr))
```

## Design Considerations

1. **Placement**: Should labels be in the meta table or as a separate section?
   - Meta table: Compact, consistent with other metadata
   - Separate section: More prominent, can be styled differently
   - **Recommendation**: Separate line after meta table for visibility

2. **Styling**: How should labels be formatted?
   - Simple comma-separated list (like clipboard function)
   - Styled tags with background colors
   - **Recommendation**: Start simple (comma-separated), enhance later if needed

3. **Empty state**: What if issue has no labels?
   - Don't render anything (current clipboard behavior)
   - Show "Labels: None" explicitly
   - **Recommendation**: Don't render if empty (cleaner)

## Test Plan

1. Test: Issue with multiple labels - all displayed
2. Test: Issue with no labels - section not rendered
3. Test: Issue with very long label list - wraps appropriately
4. Manual test: Labels visible in detail view for user's screenshot scenario

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Labels displayed in detail view panel
- [ ] No display when issue has no labels
- [ ] Markdown renders correctly (no escaping issues)
- [ ] Test coverage for with/without labels scenarios

## References

- GitHub Issue #14: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer/issues/14
- Screenshot in issue shows labels exist but don't display
- Related code: delegate.go:97-99, board.go:469-474 (where labels DO render)
close_reason: Added labels display to detail view after meta table, only renders when issue has labels

title: openInEditor Uses Hardcoded Path Instead of Configured beadsPath
description:
## Problem Statement

The 'O' keybinding to open the beads file in editor fails when the file isn't named exactly `beads.jsonl`. The `openInEditor()` function hardcodes the path instead of using the `beadsPath` field already stored in the Model.

## User Impact

**Severity: High (P1)**
- Fails silently or shows error for projects using `issues.jsonl`
- beads upstream uses `issues.jsonl` as canonical, not `beads.jsonl`
- Reported by @qmx in GitHub PR #18

## Root Cause Analysis

**Location:** `pkg/ui/model.go:2945-2955`

```go
func (m *Model) openInEditor() {
    cwd, err := os.Getwd()
    // ...
    beadsFile := filepath.Join(cwd, ".beads", "beads.jsonl")  // HARDCODED!
    if _, err := os.Stat(beadsFile); os.IsNotExist(err) {
        m.statusMsg = "No .beads/beads.jsonl file found"  // Wrong error message
        // ...
    }
}
```

The Model already has a `beadsPath` field (line 138) that's set during initialization and used for file watching. This function ignores it.

## Why This Matters

The beads project documentation (pre-commit hook) states:
> Stage all tracked JSONL files (issues.jsonl is canonical, beads.jsonl for backward compat)

So the hardcoded `beads.jsonl` path is wrong for the canonical case.

## Proposed Solution

```go
func (m *Model) openInEditor() {
    if m.beadsPath == "" {
        m.statusMsg = "No beads file configured"
        m.statusIsError = true
        return
    }

    if _, err := os.Stat(m.beadsPath); os.IsNotExist(err) {
        m.statusMsg = fmt.Sprintf("Beads file not found: %s", m.beadsPath)
        m.statusIsError = true
        return
    }
    
    // Continue with m.beadsPath instead of hardcoded path...
}
```

## Test Plan

1. Test: openInEditor with beadsPath set to issues.jsonl - should work
2. Test: openInEditor with beadsPath empty - should show "No beads file configured"
3. Test: openInEditor with beadsPath pointing to non-existent file - should show path in error
4. Manual test: 'O' key works for both beads.jsonl and issues.jsonl projects

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Uses m.beadsPath instead of hardcoded path
- [ ] Error messages include actual file path for debugging
- [ ] Works for issues.jsonl, beads.jsonl, and any other valid jsonl file
- [ ] Tests cover all error paths

## Dependencies

**Blocked by:** bv-mvvu (Data Integrity: Merge Artifact Files)
- The beadsPath value depends on correct file selection
- Fix the loader first, then this will use the correct path

## References

- GitHub PR #18: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer/pull/18
- beads pre-commit hook documentation on canonical file names
close_reason: Fixed: openInEditor now uses m.beadsPath instead of hardcoded 'beads.jsonl'. Error messages now include actual file path. Build verified.

title: GitHub Issue Triage - Community Bug Fixes (December 2025)
description:
## Overview

This epic tracks all bugs and feature requests identified from GitHub Issues (#5, #6, #7, #8, #12, #13, #14, #17) and PRs (#15, #16, #18) submitted by the community in late 2025. Each issue has been independently verified through code analysis.

## Background & Context

The bv (Beads Viewer) project received valuable community feedback identifying real bugs affecting user experience. While we don't accept outside code contributions, this feedback is essential for improving the tool.

### Why This Matters

bv is designed as an AI sidecar tool - a fast, reliable companion for issue triage. These bugs undermine that mission:

1. **Data Integrity Issues** - Showing stale/wrong data (#5) breaks user trust
2. **UX Friction** - Focus isolation bug (#12) affects every user session
3. **Feature Gaps** - Missing labels (#14) hides critical info
4. **Accessibility** - Light mode issues (#17) blocks users entirely
5. **Discoverability** - Help overlay limitations (#16) hurt onboarding

### Scope

**In Scope:** All verified bugs from community reports, related improvements, comprehensive test coverage.

**Out of Scope:** Major new features, architecture changes beyond fixes, performance optimizations.

### Success Criteria

1. All P0/P1 bugs resolved with tests
2. No regression in existing functionality
3. Community reporters can verify fixes

### Key Files

- pkg/ui/model.go - Focus handling, detail view, help overlay
- pkg/loader/loader.go - File selection, priority order
- pkg/ui/theme.go - Color definitions
- install.sh - Bash compatibility

### References

GitHub Issues: #5, #6, #7, #8, #12, #14, #17
GitHub PRs: #15, #16, #18 (closed, fixes internal)
close_reason: All 10 GitHub Issue Triage items completed - P0/P1/P2/P3/P4 bugs fixed, BEADS_DIR env var support added, shortcuts sidebar implemented

title: Set up Rust WASM crate with wasm-bindgen
description:
# Set up Rust WASM Crate with wasm-bindgen

## Context
This is the foundational task for the Rust WASM graph engine. We need a properly configured Rust workspace that compiles to WASM and integrates with the bv build system.

## Requirements

### Cargo.toml
```toml
[package]
name = "bv-graph-wasm"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"
authors = ["bv contributors"]
description = "High-performance graph algorithms for bv static viewer"
repository = "https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer"
license = "MIT"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib", "rlib"]

[features]
default = ["console_error_panic_hook"]

[dependencies]
wasm-bindgen = "0.2"
js-sys = "0.3"
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1.0"
console_error_panic_hook = { version = "0.1", optional = true }

[dev-dependencies]
wasm-bindgen-test = "0.3"

[profile.release]
# Optimize for size - critical for WASM bundles
opt-level = "s"
lto = true
codegen-units = 1
panic = "abort"
```

### Directory Structure
```
bv-graph-wasm/
  Cargo.toml
  Makefile                    # Build commands
  src/
    lib.rs                    # WASM entry point, panic hook
    graph.rs                  # DiGraph struct
    algorithms/
      mod.rs                  # Algorithm module exports
      pagerank.rs
      betweenness.rs
      eigenvector.rs
      hits.rs
      topo.rs
      cycles.rs
      critical_path.rs
      kcore.rs
      articulation.rs
      slack.rs
      density.rs
      degree.rs
    advanced/
      mod.rs
      topk_set.rs             # Greedy submodular selection
      coverage.rs             # Vertex cover
      k_paths.rs              # K-shortest paths
      parallel_cut.rs         # Parallelization analysis
      cycle_break.rs          # Cycle break suggestions
    whatif.rs                 # Impact simulation
    subgraph.rs               # Subgraph operations
    reachability.rs           # Reachability queries
  tests/
    web.rs                    # WASM integration tests
    algorithms/               # Algorithm unit tests
```

### lib.rs Skeleton
```rust
use wasm_bindgen::prelude::*;

mod graph;
mod algorithms;
mod advanced;
mod whatif;
mod subgraph;
mod reachability;

pub use graph::DiGraph;

// Initialize panic hook for better error messages in browser console
#[wasm_bindgen(start)]
pub fn init() {
    #[cfg(feature = "console_error_panic_hook")]
    console_error_panic_hook::set_once();
}

// Re-export main types for JavaScript
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn version() -> String {
    env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION").to_string()
}
```

### Makefile
```makefile
.PHONY: build build-release test clean

# Development build (faster, larger)
build:
	wasm-pack build --target web --dev

# Release build (optimized)
build-release:
	wasm-pack build --target web --release
	wasm-opt -Os -o pkg/bv_graph_wasm_bg_opt.wasm pkg/bv_graph_wasm_bg.wasm
	mv pkg/bv_graph_wasm_bg_opt.wasm pkg/bv_graph_wasm_bg.wasm

# Run tests (in browser via wasm-pack)
test:
	wasm-pack test --headless --firefox

# Clean build artifacts
clean:
	cargo clean
	rm -rf pkg/

# Check size
size:
	@echo "WASM size:"
	@wc -c pkg/bv_graph_wasm_bg.wasm
	@echo "Gzipped:"
	@gzip -c pkg/bv_graph_wasm_bg.wasm | wc -c
```

### Integration with bv Build System
Add to main Makefile:
```makefile
wasm:
	cd bv-graph-wasm && make build-release
	cp bv-graph-wasm/pkg/bv_graph_wasm.js pkg/export/viewer_assets/vendor/bv_graph.js
	cp bv-graph-wasm/pkg/bv_graph_wasm_bg.wasm pkg/export/viewer_assets/vendor/bv_graph_bg.wasm
```

### Prerequisites Check
```bash
# Required tools
rustup --version          # Rust toolchain
cargo --version           # Rust package manager
wasm-pack --version       # WASM build tool
wasm-opt --version        # Binaryen optimizer (optional but recommended)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cargo.toml configured with wasm-bindgen
- [ ] Directory structure created
- [ ] lib.rs compiles with `wasm-pack build`
- [ ] Output files generated in pkg/
- [ ] Makefile works for build/test/clean
- [ ] Release build produces <150KB WASM
- [ ] Integration with bv main Makefile
- [ ] README.md documents build process

## Why This Structure?
1. **Separate crate**: Clean boundary, independent testing
2. **wasm-pack**: Standard RustвҶ’WASM tooling
3. **wasm-opt**: Critical for size reduction (often 30-50% smaller)
4. **cdylib**: Required for WASM target
5. **console_error_panic_hook**: Better debugging in browser
6. **Module organization**: Matches Go pkg/analysis/ structure
notes: IMPORTANT: Use serde-wasm-bindgen instead of deprecated JsValue::from_serde. Add to Cargo.toml: serde-wasm-bindgen = "0.6". Use serde_wasm_bindgen::to_value() for JSON serialization to JS.
close_reason: Implemented Rust WASM crate with DiGraph core structure. Tests pass, WASM builds to 109KB (57KB gzipped).

title: Rust WASM Graph Engine for Static Viewer
description:
# Rust WASM Graph Engine for Static Viewer

## Overview
Add a Rust-compiled WASM module to the static viewer that enables **live graph calculations** on filtered data. Since we're already shipping sql.js WASM for database queries, adding a graph WASM module is a natural extension that unlocks powerful interactive features impossible with pre-computed data.

## Motivation: Why WASM for Graph Calculations?

### The Limitation of Pre-Computed Metrics
The current static export computes all graph metrics (PageRank, betweenness, etc.) at export time and stores them in SQLite. This works well for full-dataset views, but falls short when users:

1. **Filter to a subset**: PageRank of issues in the "auth" label вү  pre-computed PageRank
2. **Ask "what-if" questions**: "If I close bt-123, what unblocks?"
3. **Explore dependencies**: "Show me the critical path for just P1 issues"
4. **Analyze subgraphs**: "Are there cycles within just the API work?"

### What Rust WASM Enables
With client-side graph computation, the static viewer can:

- **Live PageRank**: Recompute centrality on any filtered subset
- **What-If Simulation**: Preview cascade effects before closing issues
- **Subgraph Analysis**: Detect cycles, critical paths within filtered views
- **Impact Preview**: Hover to see downstream effects
- **Dynamic Exploration**: "What gets unblocked if I close these 3 issues?"

### Performance Comparison (estimated on 1000-node graph)
| Algorithm | JavaScript | Rust WASM | Speedup |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| PageRank | ~200ms | ~5ms | 40x |
| Betweenness | ~2000ms | ~50ms | 40x |
| Cycle Detection | ~500ms | ~10ms | 50x |
| Critical Path | ~100ms | ~3ms | 33x |
| Topological Sort | ~50ms | ~2ms | 25x |

### Bundle Size Impact
- **bv_graph_bg.wasm**: ~80-120KB (wasm-opt optimized)
- **bv_graph.js**: ~15KB (wasm-bindgen glue)
- **Total**: ~100-135KB (acceptable; sql-wasm.wasm is ~1MB)

## Technical Architecture

### Rust Crate Structure
```
bv-graph-wasm/
  Cargo.toml
  src/
    lib.rs              # WASM bindings via wasm-bindgen
    graph.rs            # Core DiGraph structure with adjacency lists
    algorithms/
      mod.rs
      pagerank.rs       # PageRank with damping factor
      betweenness.rs    # Exact + approximate (Brandes sampling)
      eigenvector.rs    # Power iteration
      hits.rs           # Hub/Authority scores
      topo.rs           # Topological sort, Kahn's algorithm
      cycles.rs         # Tarjan SCC + cycle enumeration
      critical_path.rs  # Heights/depths in DAG
      kcore.rs          # K-core decomposition
      articulation.rs   # Tarjan's cut vertices
      slack.rs          # Critical path slack
    whatif.rs           # What-if simulation API
    subgraph.rs         # Subgraph extraction, reachability
  tests/
    integration_test.rs # Cross-algorithm tests
```

### WASM Interface Design
```rust
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub struct DiGraph {
    nodes: Vec<String>,           // Node ID strings
    node_index: HashMap<String, usize>,
    adj: Vec<Vec<usize>>,         // Forward adjacency
    rev_adj: Vec<Vec<usize>>,     // Reverse adjacency
}

#[wasm_bindgen]
impl DiGraph {
    #[wasm_bindgen(constructor)]
    pub fn new() -> DiGraph;
    
    // Graph construction
    pub fn add_node(&mut self, id: &str) -> usize;
    pub fn add_edge(&mut self, from: usize, to: usize);
    pub fn node_count(&self) -> usize;
    pub fn edge_count(&self) -> usize;
    
    // Algorithms (return JSON for complex types)
    pub fn pagerank(&self, damping: f64, iterations: u32) -> Vec<f64>;
    pub fn betweenness(&self) -> Vec<f64>;
    pub fn betweenness_approx(&self, sample_size: usize) -> Vec<f64>;
    pub fn eigenvector(&self, iterations: u32) -> Vec<f64>;
    pub fn hits(&self, iterations: u32) -> JsValue; // {hubs: [], authorities: []}
    pub fn topological_sort(&self) -> Vec<usize>;
    pub fn detect_cycles(&self) -> JsValue; // [[cycle1], [cycle2], ...]
    pub fn critical_path_heights(&self) -> Vec<f64>;
    pub fn kcore(&self) -> Vec<u32>;
    pub fn articulation_points(&self) -> Vec<usize>;
    pub fn slack(&self) -> Vec<f64>;
    
    // Subgraph operations
    pub fn subgraph(&self, node_indices: &[usize]) -> DiGraph;
    pub fn reachable_from(&self, node: usize) -> Vec<usize>;
    pub fn reachable_to(&self, node: usize) -> Vec<usize>;
    
    // What-if simulation
    pub fn what_if_close(&self, node: usize) -> JsValue; // {direct, transitive, ids}
}
```

### JavaScript Integration
```javascript
import init, { DiGraph } from './vendor/bv_graph.js';

let GRAPH_ENGINE = null;

async function initGraphEngine() {
    await init();
    GRAPH_ENGINE = new DiGraph();
    
    // Load edges from SQLite
    const deps = db.exec('SELECT issue_id, depends_on_id FROM dependencies');
    const nodeMap = new Map();
    
    for (const [from, to] of deps[0].values) {
        if (!nodeMap.has(from)) nodeMap.set(from, GRAPH_ENGINE.add_node(from));
        if (!nodeMap.has(to)) nodeMap.set(to, GRAPH_ENGINE.add_node(to));
        GRAPH_ENGINE.add_edge(nodeMap.get(from), nodeMap.get(to));
    }
    
    return nodeMap;
}

// Live recalculation example
function recalculateForFilter(filteredIds) {
    const indices = filteredIds.map(id => NODE_MAP.get(id)).filter(Boolean);
    const subgraph = GRAPH_ENGINE.subgraph(indices);
    
    return {
        pagerank: subgraph.pagerank(0.85, 100),
        cycles: JSON.parse(subgraph.detect_cycles()),
        criticalPath: subgraph.critical_path_heights(),
    };
}
```

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Core Infrastructure
- Set up Rust workspace with wasm-bindgen
- Implement DiGraph structure with adjacency lists
- Add wasm-pack build pipeline
- Integrate into bv build system

### Phase 2: Algorithm Porting (from pkg/analysis/graph.go)
- Port PageRank (power iteration)
- Port Betweenness (exact + approximate)
- Port Eigenvector centrality
- Port HITS algorithm
- Port Topological sort
- Port Cycle detection (Tarjan SCC + enumeration)
- Port Critical path heights
- Port K-core decomposition
- Port Articulation points
- Port Slack computation

### Phase 3: Advanced Operations
- Subgraph extraction
- Reachability queries (from/to)
- What-if simulation API

### Phase 4: JavaScript Integration
- Initialize from SQLite data
- Live recalculation hooks
- Fallback for WASM failures
- Performance monitoring

### Phase 5: UI Features
- "Recalculate" button for filtered views
- "What-If" panel for impact analysis
- Live metrics updates on filter change
- Subgraph highlighting

### Phase 6: Testing & Optimization
- Port Go tests to Rust
- Cross-validate with Go implementation
- JavaScript integration tests
- wasm-opt size optimization
- Benchmarks

## Fallback Strategy
If WASM fails to load (old browser, security policy, etc.):
1. Use pre-computed values from SQLite
2. Disable live recalculation features
3. Show "Limited Mode" indicator
4. Graceful degradation - everything still works, just no live updates

## Success Criteria
1. WASM module loads reliably across browsers
2. PageRank on 1000 nodes completes in <50ms
3. All algorithms match Go implementation results
4. Bundle size increase <150KB
5. Fallback works when WASM unavailable
6. Live recalculation enabled in filtered views

## References
- bv pkg/analysis/graph.go: Go implementations to port
- bv pkg/analysis/betweenness_approx.go: Approximate betweenness
- bv pkg/analysis/whatif.go: What-if simulation patterns
- wasm-bindgen: https://rustwasm.github.io/wasm-bindgen/
- wasm-pack: https://rustwasm.github.io/wasm-pack/
close_reason: Rust WASM graph engine fully implemented: DiGraph structure, PageRank, betweenness, HITS, cycles, critical path, k-core, topological sort, what-if simulation, subgraph extraction. Integrated with viewer.js.

title: Static Site Export & GitHub Pages Deployment
description:
# Static Site Export & GitHub Pages Deployment

## Overview
Add the ability to generate and deploy a slick static website that presents bv's issue tracker data to GitHub Pages (and optionally Cloudflare Pages). This feature mirrors the successful "shared mailbox" functionality in mcp_agent_mail, using the SAME proven SQLite + sql.js + FTS5 architecture.

## Motivation & Goals
1. **Shareability**: Allow teams to share project status without requiring bv installation
2. **Archival**: Create point-in-time snapshots of project health for stakeholders
3. **CI Integration**: Auto-publish project dashboards from CI pipelines
4. **Accessibility**: View rich analysis data in any browser

## Key Features
- **Dashboard View**: Quick stats (open/blocked/actionable), health indicators, top picks
- **Issues List**: Filterable/sortable by status, type, priority, labels, assignee
- **Issue Detail**: Full description, dependencies (blocks/blocked-by), graph position
- **Dependency Graph**: Interactive Mermaid.js visualization
- **Insights View**: PageRank leaders, bottlenecks, critical path, recommendations
- **Full-Text Search**: FTS5-powered search with ranking and snippets

## Technical Approach (Matching mcp_agent_mail)
| mcp_agent_mail Component | bv Equivalent |
|--------------------------|---------------|
| SQLite snapshot | SQLite export (beads.sqlite3) |
| sql.js WASM | sql.js WASM (client-side queries) |
| FTS5 full-text search | FTS5 full-text search |
| Materialized views | Materialized views (issue_overview_mv) |
| OPFS caching | OPFS caching (offline support) |
| Database chunking | Database chunking (>5MB) |
| Message list | Issues list with SQL filtering |
| Thread detail | Issue detail + dependency subgraph |
| Alpine.js + Tailwind | Same stack (proven, lightweight) |
| DOMPurify + Trusted Types | Same security approach |
| gh CLI deployment | Same approach |
| Cloudflare wrangler | Same approach |
| coi-serviceworker.js | Same (COOP/COEP for OPFS) |

## Bundle Structure
```
bv-pages/
  index.html              # SPA entry with CSP, COOP/COEP via service worker
  viewer.js               # Main app with sql.js integration
  styles.css              # Tailwind + custom styles
  beads.sqlite3           # Optimized SQLite database
  beads.sqlite3.config.json  # Chunk config (if chunked)
  chunks/                 # Database chunks (if >5MB)
  data/
    triage.json           # Full --robot-triage output
    insights.json         # Full --robot-insights output
    plan.json             # Full --robot-plan output
    meta.json             # Export metadata
  vendor/
    sql-wasm.js           # sql.js library
    sql-wasm.wasm         # SQLite WASM binary
    alpine.min.js         # Reactive UI framework
    marked.min.js         # Markdown rendering
    dompurify.min.js      # HTML sanitization
  coi-serviceworker.js    # COOP/COEP for GitHub Pages
  assets/
    favicon.svg
```

## Deployment Wizard Flow (Interactive)
1. Export options: select issues (all/filtered), include closed (yes/no)
2. Analysis: regenerate or use cached robot outputs
3. Target selection: GitHub Pages / Cloudflare Pages / Local only
4. Prerequisites check: gh/wrangler auth, git config, confirm identity
5. Preview: local server with hot reload
6. Deploy: create/update repo (with safety checks), push, enable Pages

## CLI Commands
```bash
# Full wizard (interactive)
bv --pages

# Quick export to directory
bv --export-pages ./bv-pages

# Export with options
bv --export-pages ./bv-pages --include-closed --title "My Project"

# Preview existing bundle
bv --preview-pages ./bv-pages

# Update existing deployment
bv --update-pages ./bv-pages
```

## Security Considerations
- No secrets in exports (bv data is typically non-sensitive)
- CSP headers for XSS prevention
- DOMPurify + Trusted Types for markdown rendering
- COOP/COEP via service worker for OPFS support
- Safety checks before any destructive git operations
- Permission prompts before software installation

## Future Enhancements (Out of Scope for MVP)
- Historical trend charts (requires multiple snapshots)
- Embedded search highlighting
- PDF export option
- Custom themes/branding

## Success Criteria
1. User can run `bv --pages` and have a live GitHub Pages site in <5 minutes
2. Site shows all data from bv TUI in readable format
3. Site works offline after initial load (OPFS caching)
4. Works on mobile browsers
5. Dark mode support
6. Full-text search works with ranking
7. Dependency graph is interactive and useful

## Architecture Rationale (Why SQLite, not JSON)
1. **Real Search**: FTS5 provides full-text search with ranking (bm25), snippets, prefix matching
2. **Complex Queries**: SQL handles dependency traversal, filtering, sorting without JavaScript complexity
3. **Scale**: Same approach works for 50 or 50,000 issues
4. **Performance**: Client queries only what it needs, pagination via LIMIT/OFFSET
5. **Caching**: OPFS support for offline use with cache invalidation
6. **Proven**: mcp_agent_mail demonstrates this architecture works exceptionally well

## References
- mcp_agent_mail share.py (2207 lines): Data export pipeline
- mcp_agent_mail share_to_github_pages.py (1353 lines): Deployment wizard
- mcp_agent_mail viewer_assets/: SPA implementation with sql.js
close_reason: All 19 component tasks completed - static site export feature fully implemented

title: Implement --robot-next command
description:
# --robot-next: The One-Answer Command

**For when you just need ONE thing to work on. No context, no analysis, just an answer.**

## Usage
```bash
bv --robot-next                    # What should I do next?
bv --robot-next --skip=bt-42       # Skip specific bead
bv --robot-next --agent=claude-1   # Respect agent claims
```

## Output (Minimal by Design)

```json
{
  "next": {
    "bead_id": "bt-43",
    "title": "Implement auth middleware",
    "score": 94.2,
    "why": "Unblocks 5 items, label needs attention",
    "claim": "bv --robot-claim bt-43"
  },
  "alerts": 2,
  "your_claims": 1
}
```

That's it. ~10 lines of JSON.

## When to Use

| Scenario | Use |
|----------|-----|
| Starting fresh session | `--robot-triage` (full context) |
| Mid-session, need quick direction | `--robot-next` |
| Polling frequently | `--robot-next` |
| First time on project | `--robot-triage` |
| Debugging/investigating | `--robot-triage` |

## Behavior

1. Computes full triage internally (same as --robot-triage)
2. Returns ONLY the top_pick + minimal metadata
3. Respects agent claims (excludes items claimed by others)
4. Excludes your own claims (you already have work)
5. Applies --skip filters

## Edge Cases

**No work available:**
```json
{
  "next": null,
  "reason": "all_claimed",
  "suggestion": "Wait for claims to release or help with bt-42 (claimed by cursor-agent)"
}
```

**Critical alerts pending:**
```json
{
  "next": {
    "bead_id": "bt-43",
    "title": "Implement auth middleware",
    "claim": "bv --robot-claim bt-43"
  },
  "alerts": 1,
  "alert_preview": "CRITICAL: Dependency cycle detected",
  "suggestion": "Run bv --robot-triage to see full alert details"
}
```

## Implementation

Internally calls the same triage computation as --robot-triage, then:
```go
func RobotNext(agentName string, skip []string) NextResult {
    full := ComputeTriage(agentName)
    
    if len(full.Alerts.Critical) > 0 {
        return NextResult{
            Next: full.TopPick,
            Alerts: len(full.Alerts.Critical) + len(full.Alerts.Warning),
            AlertPreview: full.Alerts.Critical[0].Message,
            Suggestion: "Run bv --robot-triage to see full alert details",
        }
    }
    
    return NextResult{
        Next: full.TopPick,
        Alerts: len(full.Alerts.Warning),
        YourClaims: len(full.YourSession.Claims),
    }
}
```

## Relationship to --robot-triage

```
--robot-triage  = Full meal (everything you need)
--robot-next    = Snack (just the answer)
```

Both compute the same underlying triage. --robot-next just returns less.

title: Implement --robot-triage command
description:
# --robot-triage: The Mega Command (Incremental Implementation)

**THE primary entry point for AI agents. One call = everything available.**

## Key Design Principle: Graceful Degradation

The mega-command works with WHATEVER features are available. Sections are included only when their underlying systems are ready:

| Section | Requires | Phase |
|---------|----------|-------|
| meta, quick_ref | Nothing | 1 (MVP) |
| recommendations | ComputeImpactScores() | 1 (MVP) |
| project_health.counts | Basic stats | 1 (MVP) |
| project_health.graph | Analyzer | 1 (MVP) |
| alerts | Proactive Alerts (bt-135) | 2 |
| your_session, team | Agent Swarm (bt-132) | 2 |
| project_health.labels | Labels View (bt-99) | 2 |
| history_hints | History (bt-62) | 3 |
| suggestions | Smart Suggestions (bt-137) | 3 |

**Missing sections return `null` or are omitted entirely.**

## Phase 1 MVP Output (Works Today)

```json
{
  "meta": {"command": "--robot-triage", "generated_at": "...", "data_hash": "..."},
  "quick_ref": {
    "top_pick": "bt-43: Implement auth middleware",
    "actionable_items": 33,
    "status": "ready_to_work"
  },
  "alerts": null,
  "top_pick": {
    "bead_id": "bt-43",
    "title": "Implement auth middleware",
    "score": 94.2,
    "why": ["Highest impact score", "Unblocks 5 items"],
    "claim_command": "bv --robot-claim bt-43"
  },
  "your_session": null,
  "team": null,
  "recommendations": [...],
  "quick_wins": [...],
  "blockers_to_clear": [...],
  "project_health": {
    "counts": {"total": 169, "open": 131, "blocked": 12},
    "graph": {"cycles": 0, "density": 0.15, "health": "good"},
    "labels_attention": null,
    "drift": null
  },
  "suggestions": null,
  "commands": {"claim_top_pick": "bv --robot-claim bt-43", "see_full_plan": "bv --robot-plan"}
}
```

## Full Output (All Features Enabled)

[Previous full specification remains valid - all 12 sections populated]

## Implementation Approach

```go
func ComputeTriage(agentName string) TriageResult {
    result := TriageResult{
        Meta: buildMeta(),
    }
    
    // Phase 1: Always available
    result.Recommendations = computeRecommendations()
    result.TopPick = result.Recommendations[0]
    result.ProjectHealth.Counts = computeCounts()
    result.ProjectHealth.Graph = computeGraphHealth()
    result.QuickWins = findQuickWins()
    result.BlockersToClear = findBlockers()
    
    // Phase 2: If agent swarm available
    if agentSwarmEnabled() {
        result.YourSession = getSessionContext(agentName)
        result.Team = getTeamStatus()
    }
    
    // Phase 2: If alerts available  
    if alertsEnabled() {
        result.Alerts = getAlerts()
    }
    
    // Phase 2: If labels view available
    if labelsEnabled() {
        result.ProjectHealth.LabelsAttention = getLabelHealth()
    }
    
    // Phase 3: If history available
    if historyEnabled() {
        result.HistoryHints = getHistoryHints()
    }
    
    // Phase 3: If suggestions available
    if suggestionsEnabled() {
        result.Suggestions = getSuggestions()
    }
    
    result.QuickRef = buildQuickRef(result)
    result.Commands = buildCommands(result)
    
    return result
}
```

## Usage

```bash
bv --robot-triage                    # Full output (whatever is available)
bv --robot-triage --compact          # Minimal: quick_ref + top_pick + alerts
bv --robot-triage --agent=claude-1   # Include agent-specific context
```

## Companion Command

`bv --robot-next` - Returns ONLY top_pick (~10 lines). Same incremental approach.

title: Implement reason generation
description:
INCREMENTAL ENHANCEMENT on existing reason generation

## What Already Exists (DO NOT DUPLICATE)
- `pkg/analysis/priority.go:generateRecommendation()` - creates PriorityRecommendation
- `pkg/analysis/priority.go:Reasoning[]` field - list of human-readable explanations
- Existing reasons: PageRank commentary, betweenness commentary, staleness notes, unblocks count

## What This Adds (NEW VALUE)
TRIAGE-specific reasons that supplement priority reasons:
1. Label attention context: "Label 'backend' needs attention (health: 45/100)"
2. Unblock cascade detail: "Completing this unblocks bt-43, bt-44, bt-51 (3 items)"
3. Agent claim status: "Currently unclaimed - available for work"
4. Staleness alert phrasing: "No activity in 12 days - consider reviewing"
5. Quick-win identification: "Low effort, high impact - good starting point"

## Implementation Approach
1. REUSE: Call `GenerateRecommendations()` to get base reasons
2. ADD: generateTriageReasons() that adds triage-specific context
3. ADD: Merge base reasons + triage reasons into unified list
4. ADD: Priority ordering of reasons (most actionable first)

## Key Principle
TriageReasons = BaseReasons + TriageSpecificReasons
Triage reasons should be ACTIONABLE (tell agent what to DO, not just what IS)

## Output Format Example
```json
{
  "reasons": [
    "рҹҺҜ Completing this unblocks 5 downstream issues (highest impact in backlog)",
    "вҡ пёҸ Label 'database' needs attention (health: 32/100)",
    "рҹ“Ҡ High graph centrality (PageRank: 0.82, betweenness: 0.65)",
    "рҹ•җ No activity in 14 days - may be stuck or forgotten",
    "вң… Currently unclaimed - available for immediate work"
  ],
  "action": "Start work on this issue"
}
```
close_reason: Implemented actionable reason generation with emoji-prefixed explanations. All 48 triage tests passing.

title: Implement unified scoring algorithm
description:
INCREMENTAL ENHANCEMENT on existing scoring

## What Already Exists (DO NOT DUPLICATE)
- `pkg/analysis/priority.go:ComputeImpactScores()` - composite scoring
- Existing weights: PageRank(30%), Betweenness(30%), BlockerRatio(20%), Staleness(10%), PriorityBoost(10%)
- `pkg/analysis/priority.go:ScoreBreakdown` - individual component tracking

## What This Adds (NEW VALUE)
TRIAGE-specific scoring that EXTENDS base scoring with:
1. Label health factor (from bt-99 labels view) - boost items in unhealthy labels
2. Agent claim factor (from bt-132) - reduce score for claimed items
3. Unblock impact factor - boost items that unblock many others
4. Quick-win factor - boost small items with high impact ratio

## Incremental Implementation (Graceful Degradation)

**Phase 1 (MVP):** Works with existing scoring only
```go
TriageScore = ComputeImpactScores() // 100% weight
```

**Phase 2 (Labels ready):** Add label health
```go
TriageScore = BaseScore*0.7 + LabelHealthBoost*0.15 + UnblockBoost*0.15
```

**Phase 3 (Agent Swarm ready):** Add claim penalty
```go
if claimedByOther { TriageScore *= 0.1 } // Heavily penalize claimed items
```

**Phase 4 (Attention scores ready):** Refine label factor
```go
// Replace simple label health with attention-weighted health
LabelHealthBoost = labelAttentionScore * 0.15
```

## Key Principle
Start simple, enhance incrementally. Never block triage on optional features.

## Output Format
```json
{
  "triage_scores": [{
    "issue_id": "bt-42",
    "base_score": 0.72,
    "triage_score": 0.85,
    "factors_applied": ["base", "label_health", "unblock"],
    "factors_pending": ["claim_penalty", "attention_score"]
  }]
}
```
close_reason: Implemented Phase 1 MVP scoring with unblock boost (15%) and quick-win factor (15%). All 29 triage tests passing. Ready for Phase 2 enhancements.

title: Define TriageRecommendation and TriageAlert types
description:
# Triage Types: Complete Data Model

## Core Result Type

```go
type TriageResult struct {
    Meta           TriageMeta           `json:"meta"`
    QuickRef       QuickRef             `json:"quick_ref"`
    Alerts         AlertsByLevel        `json:"alerts"`
    TopPick        *TopPick             `json:"top_pick"`        // nil if nothing to work on
    YourSession    *SessionContext      `json:"your_session"`    // nil if no agent identity
    Team           TeamStatus           `json:"team"`
    Recommendations []Recommendation    `json:"recommendations"`
    QuickWins      []QuickWin           `json:"quick_wins"`
    BlockersToClear []BlockerItem       `json:"blockers_to_clear"`
    ProjectHealth  ProjectHealth        `json:"project_health"`
    Suggestions    []Suggestion         `json:"suggestions"`
    Commands       CommandHelpers       `json:"commands"`
}
```

## Supporting Types

```go
type TriageMeta struct {
    Command       string    `json:"command"`
    GeneratedAt   time.Time `json:"generated_at"`
    DataHash      string    `json:"data_hash"`
    AgentIdentity string    `json:"agent_identity,omitempty"`
}

type QuickRef struct {
    TopPick        string `json:"top_pick"`         // "bt-43: Title"
    CriticalAlerts int    `json:"critical_alerts"`
    Warnings       int    `json:"warnings"`
    YourClaims     int    `json:"your_claims"`
    TeamActive     int    `json:"team_active"`
    ActionableItems int   `json:"actionable_items"`
    Status         string `json:"status"`           // "ready_to_work", "alerts_pending", "all_claimed"
}

type AlertsByLevel struct {
    Critical []TriageAlert `json:"critical"`
    Warning  []TriageAlert `json:"warning"`
    Info     []TriageAlert `json:"info"`
}

type TriageAlert struct {
    Type    string   `json:"type"`              // Uses drift.AlertType + new types
    BeadID  string   `json:"bead_id,omitempty"`
    BeadIDs []string `json:"bead_ids,omitempty"` // For multi-bead alerts
    Message string   `json:"message"`
    Action  string   `json:"action"`            // What to do about it
}

type TopPick struct {
    BeadID         string   `json:"bead_id"`
    Title          string   `json:"title"`
    Type           string   `json:"type"`
    Priority       string   `json:"priority"`
    Score          float64  `json:"score"`
    Why            []string `json:"why"`            // Human-readable reasons with emoji
    Labels         []string `json:"labels"`
    EstimatedImpact string  `json:"estimated_impact"`
    FilesLikely    []string `json:"files_likely,omitempty"`
    ClaimCommand   string   `json:"claim_command"`
}

type SessionContext struct {
    AgentName       string         `json:"agent_name"`
    Claims          []ClaimInfo    `json:"claims"`
    PendingHandoffs []HandoffInfo  `json:"pending_handoffs"`
    RecentActivity  string         `json:"recent_activity"`
}

type ClaimInfo struct {
    BeadID    string    `json:"bead_id"`
    Title     string    `json:"title"`
    ClaimedAt time.Time `json:"claimed_at"`
    Files     []string  `json:"files,omitempty"`
}

type HandoffInfo struct {
    FromAgent  string    `json:"from_agent"`
    BeadID     string    `json:"bead_id"`
    Message    string    `json:"message"`
    ReceivedAt time.Time `json:"received_at"`
}

type TeamStatus struct {
    ActiveAgents     []AgentSummary `json:"active_agents"`
    TotalClaimed     int            `json:"total_claimed"`
    FileConflicts    []FileConflict `json:"file_conflicts"`

type Recommendation struct {

title: Implement conflict detection
description:
**NOTE: This task is about HANDOFF MESSAGING (title "conflict detection" is legacy)**

Agent-to-agent handoff messaging for work transitions

## Commands
```bash
bv --robot-handoff bt-42 "Finished API, needs frontend integration"
bv --robot-messages                 # View handoff messages
```

## Purpose
When an agent finishes part of a task or needs to pass context to another agent:
- Send structured handoff message attached to a bead
- Other agents see pending handoffs when claiming work
- Enables smooth multi-agent collaboration

## Data Model
```go
type HandoffMessage struct {
    ID        string    `json:"id"`
    BeadID    string    `json:"bead_id"`
    FromAgent string    `json:"from_agent"`
    ToAgent   string    `json:"to_agent"`     // specific agent or "*" for any
    Message   string    `json:"message"`
    Timestamp time.Time `json:"timestamp"`
    Read      bool      `json:"read"`
}
```

## Storage
Persisted in `.beads/handoffs.jsonl` (same pattern as issues.jsonl)

## Integration Points
- Triage surfaces unread handoffs in recommendations
- --robot-next prioritizes items with pending handoffs
- Claiming a bead shows any pending handoffs

## Output Format (receiving)
```json
{
  "explanation": {
    "what": "Pending handoff messages",
    "when_to_use": "Before starting work on a claimed bead",
    "action": "Read context from previous agent before proceeding"
  },
  "pending_handoffs": [{
    "bead_id": "bt-42",
    "from_agent": "claude-opus-1",
    "message": "Finished API changes, needs frontend integration",
    "timestamp": "2025-01-15T11:00:00Z"
  }],
  "count": 1
}
```

title: Implement work claiming and releasing
description:
Work claiming and releasing mechanism

## Commands
```bash
bv --robot-claim bt-42              # Claim a bead
bv --robot-claim bt-42 bt-43        # Claim multiple beads
bv --robot-release bt-42            # Release a claim
bv --robot-release --all            # Release all claims for this agent
```

## Claim Semantics
- Soft lock (advisory, not enforced)
- Auto-expires after 30 min inactivity (configurable)
- Renewed by heartbeat or explicit --robot-claim
- Other agents see claim but can override with --force

## Conflict Handling
- If already claimed by another agent: return warning, not error
- Include claim holder info so agents can coordinate
- --force flag allows override (for emergencies)

## Output Format (claim)
```json
{
  "explanation": {
    "what": "Work claim result",
    "when_to_use": "Before starting work on an issue",
    "action": "Proceed with work if granted, coordinate if conflict"
  },
  "granted": ["bt-42"],
  "conflicts": [
    {
      "bead_id": "bt-43",
      "holder": "claude-opus-2",
      "claimed_at": "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z",
      "expires_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"
    }
  ],
  "message": "Claimed 1 of 2 requested beads"
}
```

## Integration with Triage
- Triage scoring (bt-147) applies ClaimPenalty to claimed items
- --robot-triage excludes your own claims from recommendations
- --robot-next never suggests items claimed by others

title: Implement agent registry and heartbeat system
description:
Agent registry management and heartbeat system

## Commands
```bash
bv --robot-agents          # List all agents (active + inactive)
bv --robot-agent-register  # Register this agent (reads from env)
bv --robot-agent-heartbeat # Update LastSeen timestamp
```

## Registration Flow
1. Agent starts session
2. Calls --robot-agent-register with name, model, program
3. System assigns unique name if collision (claude-opus-1, claude-opus-2)
4. Returns agent ID for subsequent operations

## Heartbeat Flow
1. Agent calls --robot-agent-heartbeat periodically (every 5 min recommended)
2. System updates LastSeen
3. Returns current claims and any conflicts

## Environment Variables (for auto-detection)
```
BV_AGENT_NAME=claude-opus-1
BV_AGENT_MODEL=claude-opus-4
BV_AGENT_PROGRAM=claude-code
```

## Output Format
```json
{
  "explanation": {
    "what": "Active agent registry",
    "when_to_use": "Before claiming work, to see who else is active",
    "action": "Coordinate with listed agents to avoid conflicts"
  },
  "agents": [
    {
      "name": "claude-opus-1",
      "model": "claude-opus-4",
      "program": "claude-code",
      "active": true,
      "claims": ["bt-42", "bt-43"],
      "last_seen": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"
    }
  ],
  "total_active": 2,
  "total_claims": 5
}
```

title: Define AgentRegistration and WorkClaim data types
description:
Core data types for Agent Swarm Protocol

## Data Model
```go
// .beads/agents.jsonl storage format
type AgentRegistration struct {
    Name        string    `json:"name"`         // "claude-opus-1"
    Model       string    `json:"model"`        // "claude-opus-4"
    Program     string    `json:"program"`      // "claude-code", "cursor", "devin"
    StartedAt   time.Time `json:"started_at"`
    LastSeen    time.Time `json:"last_seen"`
    ClaimedWork []string  `json:"claimed_work"` // bead IDs
    FileHints   []string  `json:"file_hints"`   // files being touched
}

type WorkClaim struct {
    BeadID    string    `json:"bead_id"`
    Agent     string    `json:"agent"`
    ClaimedAt time.Time `json:"claimed_at"`
    ExpiresAt time.Time `json:"expires_at"`  // auto-release after inactivity
    Reason    string    `json:"reason"`      // "implementing", "reviewing", "testing"
}
```

## Storage
- Store in `.beads/agents.jsonl` (same pattern as issues.jsonl)
- One line per agent registration
- WorkClaims embedded in AgentRegistration.ClaimedWork

## Heartbeat Logic
- Agent updates LastSeen on each operation
- Claims auto-expire after 30 minutes of inactivity (configurable)
- Stale agents (LastSeen > 1 hour) marked inactive

## Integration Points
- Triage considers claims (bt-147 ClaimPenalty)
- Graph viz can show claim status
- Alerts when claims conflict or expire

title: Unified Triage Intelligence
description:
# Unified Triage Intelligence: The Mega Command

**This is THE primary entry point for AI agents using bv.**

## Vision

Instead of agents calling 5-10 different robot commands and mentally merging results, `--robot-triage` provides EVERYTHING an agent needs in ONE call:

- What should I work on? (recommendations)
- Are there fires to put out? (alerts)
- Who else is working? (team awareness)
- Will I conflict with anyone? (file conflicts)
- What's the project health? (stats, graph, labels)
- Any handoffs waiting for me? (collaboration)

## Design Principles

1. **One call = full context** - Agent calls triage once, has everything
2. **Critical first** - Alerts before recommendations
3. **Actionable throughout** - Every section says what to DO
4. **Self-documenting** - Output explains itself
5. **Team-aware** - Multi-agent coordination built-in
6. **Progressive detail** - Summaries by default, deep-dive commands available

## Output Structure (Priority Order)

```
1. meta           - Command info, timestamp, data hash
2. quick_ref      - One-line summary for fast parsing
3. alerts         - Critical issues FIRST (fires before features)
4. top_pick       - THE #1 recommendation with full reasoning
5. your_session   - Your claims, handoffs, context
6. team           - Other agents, conflicts, available tracks
7. recommendations- Next 5 items after top_pick
8. quick_wins     - Low-effort high-impact items
9. blockers       - Items that unblock the most work
10. project_health- Stats, graph health, label health, drift
11. suggestions   - Hygiene hints (missing deps, stale claims)
12. commands      - Exact commands for next steps
```

## Relationship to Other Commands

`--robot-triage` INCLUDES summaries from:
- Agent Swarm (team, your_session, conflicts)
- Proactive Alerts (alerts section)
- Priority scoring (recommendations, top_pick)
- Labels View (project_health.labels)
- Execution Plan (available tracks)
- History (your_session context)
- Drift (project_health.drift)
- Suggestions (suggestions section)

Individual commands remain for DEEP DIVES:
- `--robot-plan` for full execution tracks
- `--robot-insights` for complete graph metrics
- `--robot-agents` for detailed agent activity
- `--robot-alerts` for full alert details
- `--robot-history` for commit correlation

## Companion: --robot-next

For agents that just want ONE answer:
```bash
bv --robot-next  # Returns ONLY top_pick, nothing else
```

## Success Criteria

An AI agent should be able to:
1. Call `--robot-triage` at session start
2. Immediately understand what to work on
3. Know if there are any critical issues
4. See team context without additional calls
5. Get exact commands for next actions
close_reason: Core triage intelligence complete: types defined, unified scoring, reason generation, --robot-triage and --robot-next commands implemented. TUI integration (bv-151) remaining as enhancement

title: Agent Swarm Protocol: Multi-Agent Coordination
description:
Enable multiple AI agents to work on the same project without conflicts.

## INTEGRATION PRINCIPLES FOR AI AGENTS

### Problem This Solves
Without coordination, multiple agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex) will:
- Work on the same issues simultaneously
- Edit the same files causing merge conflicts
- Duplicate effort or produce conflicting changes

### How It Integrates With Existing Commands
This feature AUGMENTS existing commands, doesn't replace them:

1. **--robot-plan** will include a new field:
   ```json
   {
     "tracks": [...],
     "agent_claims": {
       "bt-42": {"agent": "claude-opus-1", "claimed_at": "...", "expires_at": "..."}
     },
     "available_for_claim": ["bt-43", "bt-44"]  // Excludes claimed work
   }
   ```

2. **--robot-priority** will filter out claimed items by default:
   ```json
   {
     "recommendations": [...],  // Only unclaimed items
     "claimed_by_others": 3     // How many filtered out
   }
   ```

### Self-Documenting Output Pattern
Every agent-related robot command MUST include an 'explanation' field:
```json
{
  "command": "--robot-agents",
  "result": {...},
  "explanation": {
    "what": "Shows all agents currently registered on this project",
    "when_to_use": "Before starting work to see who else is active",
    "action": "If you see other agents, use --robot-claim to reserve your work"
  }
}
```

### Claim Expiration
Claims auto-expire after 30 minutes of no heartbeat. This prevents orphaned claims from blocking work.

### File Hints Are Advisory
File reservation hints are NOT locks - they're signals to other agents. An agent seeing a conflict should:
1. Check if the other agent is still active (recent heartbeat)
2. If inactive > 30min, proceed with work
3. If active, choose different work or coordinate

title: Labels View & Dashboard System
description:
# Labels View & Dashboard System

## Overview
Implement a comprehensive Labels View that turns labels into a first-class organizational mechanism. This enables long-horizon task coordination by providing dashboards for feature areas (feat:auth), work types (fixes, ui), and epic progress tracking.

## Inspiration
Based on @kraitsura's Labels Viewer concept showing:
- Label selector with fuzzy search and progress bars
- Dashboard view grouping by EPICS and LABELS  
- Completion tracking (X/Y format with visual progress)
- Dependency depth toggle

## User Stories
- As a project lead, I want to see progress across feature areas at a glance
- As a developer, I want to quickly filter to issues with a specific label
- As a team, we want to track epic completion with roll-up statistics
- As anyone, I want fuzzy search to find labels quickly

## Key Features

### 1. Label Selector (Overlay)
- Fuzzy searchable picker accessible via \`l\` key from any view
- Shows all labels and epics with progress bars
- X/Y format showing completed vs total
- Checkmarks for 100% complete labels

### 2. Label Dashboard View  
- Dedicated view showing label statistics
- Two sections: EPICS (issues with type=epic) and LABELS
- Progress bars with completion percentages
- Expandable to drill into issues per label
- Section cycling: Ready/Blocked/InProgress/Closed

### 3. Dependency Depth Toggle
- \`d\` key cycles through 1/2/3/All depth levels
- Controls how deeply to show dependency chains
- Current depth shown in status bar

### 4. Visual Enhancements
- Distinction between labeled and unlabeled issues
- Epic progress roll-up from child issues
- Quick jump from dashboard to filtered list

## Keybindings
| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| l | Open label selector (global) |
| Enter | Select label / Confirm |
| Esc | Close selector / Back |
| j/k | Navigate |
| Tab | Cycle sections |
| d | Cycle dependency depth |

## Success Criteria
- [ ] \`l\` key opens label selector from any view
- [ ] Dashboard shows accurate progress for all labels
- [ ] Fuzzy search finds labels quickly
- [ ] Epic completion rolls up from children
- [ ] Dependency depth toggle works smoothly

## Implementation Phases
1. **Phase 1**: Label statistics extraction and data model
2. **Phase 2**: Label selector UI with fuzzy search
3. **Phase 3**: Label dashboard view
4. **Phase 4**: Integration, depth toggle, polish

title: Add --robot-history CLI flag (MVP)
description:
# Robot History CLI Flag (MVP)

## Context
This completes the MVP. With this flag, AI agents can get structured JSON output of bead-to-commit correlations.

\`\`\`go

    "Output bead-to-commit correlations as JSON")

    "Show history for specific bead ID")

\`\`\`

## Usage Examples
\`\`\`bash
# All bead correlations
bv --robot-history

# Specific bead

# Last 30 days

  "generated_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z",

    {

title: Implement co-committed file extraction
description:
# Co-Committed File Extraction

## Context
This is the highest-confidence correlation method (95-99%). When a bead status changes and code files change in THE SAME COMMIT, that's a direct causal link.

## Why This Works
Developer workflow:
1. Write code to implement feature
2. Mark bead as closed
3. Commit both changes together

The code files in that commit ARE the implementation of the bead.

## Git Command
\`\`\`bash
git show --name-status --format="" <sha>
\`\`\`

Output:
\`\`\`
M       pkg/auth/login.go
M       pkg/auth/session.go
M       .beads/beads.jsonl
\`\`\`

## Algorithm
1. For each BeadEvent with status change (claimed/closed)
2. Run \`git show --name-status\` for that commit
3. Filter OUT bead files (.beads/*)
4. Return code files only as CorrelatedCommit records

## What Counts as "Code Files"?
Include: .go, .py, .js, .ts, .rs, .java, .yaml, .json, .md
Exclude: .beads/*, .bv/*, .git/*, node_modules/, vendor/

## Getting Line Stats
\`\`\`bash
git show --numstat --format="" <sha>
\`\`\`

## Confidence Scoring
Base: 0.95

Adjustments:
- Commit message mentions bead ID: +0.04 вҶ’ 0.99
- >20 files changed (shotgun commit): -0.10 вҶ’ 0.85
- Only test files changed: -0.05 вҶ’ 0.90

## File Location
\`pkg/correlation/cocommit.go\`

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Correctly extracts co-committed files for any commit
2. Filters out bead files and non-code files
3. Includes insertion/deletion stats
4. Applies confidence adjustments correctly
5. Handles commits with no code files gracefully
close_reason: Implemented co-committed file extraction in pkg/correlation/cocommit.go with confidence scoring and tests

title: Implement git history parser for beads.jsonl
description:
# Git History Parser for beads.jsonl

## Context
This is the core extraction logic. We parse \`git log -p -- .beads/beads.jsonl\` to extract bead lifecycle events.

## Git Command
\`\`\`bash
git log -p --follow --format="%H|%aI|%an|%ae|%s" -- .beads/beads.jsonl
\`\`\`

## Parsing Algorithm
1. Run git log command
2. Parse output commit by commit
3. For each commit:
   a. Parse commit metadata (SHA, timestamp, author, message)
   b. Parse the diff section
   c. For each changed line in diff:
      - Parse old JSON (lines starting with -)
      - Parse new JSON (lines starting with +)
      - Detect what changed (new bead, status change, other)
      - Create BeadEvent records
4. Return chronologically sorted events

## Diff Parsing Details

### Detecting Bead Creation
\`\`\`diff
+{"id":"AUTH-123","title":"Fix login","status":"open",...}
\`\`\`
A line starting with \`+\` containing valid JSON = EventCreated

### Detecting Status Changes
\`\`\`diff
-{"id":"AUTH-123",...,"status":"open",...}
+{"id":"AUTH-123",...,"status":"in_progress",...}
\`\`\`
Same bead ID, different status = appropriate event type

## Edge Cases to Handle
1. Malformed JSON lines: Skip with warning
2. Multiple beads changed in one commit: Generate event for each
3. Empty commits: No events generated
4. Merge commits: Parse like regular commits

## Options Structure
\`\`\`go
type ExtractOptions struct {
    Since     time.Time  // Only commits after this time
    Until     time.Time  // Only commits before this time
    Limit     int        // Max commits to process
    BeadID    string     // Filter to single bead ID
}
\`\`\`

## File Location
\`pkg/correlation/extractor.go\`

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Correctly parses sample git log output
2. Handles all edge cases without panic
3. Returns chronologically sorted events
4. Supports filtering options
5. Unit tests with mock git output

title: Define data types for bead history correlation
description:
# Data Types for Bead History Correlation

## Context
This is the foundational task - all other history features depend on these types being well-designed. Getting this right is critical.

## Types to Define

### BeadEvent
Represents a single lifecycle event for a bead, extracted from git history.

\`\`\`go
type BeadEvent struct {
    BeadID      string    \`json:"bead_id"\`
    EventType   EventType \`json:"event_type"\`
    Timestamp   time.Time \`json:"timestamp"\`
    CommitSHA   string    \`json:"commit_sha"\`
    CommitMsg   string    \`json:"commit_message"\`
    Author      string    \`json:"author"\`       // Git author name
    AuthorEmail string    \`json:"author_email"\` // Git author email
}

type EventType string
const (
    EventCreated  EventType = "created"      // Bead first appears in file
    EventClaimed  EventType = "claimed"      // Status changed to in_progress
    EventClosed   EventType = "closed"       // Status changed to closed
    EventReopened EventType = "reopened"     // Status changed FROM closed
    EventModified EventType = "modified"     // Other significant changes
)
\`\`\`

### CorrelatedCommit
A code commit linked to a bead with confidence metadata.

\`\`\`go
type CorrelatedCommit struct {
    SHA           string            \`json:"sha"\`
    ShortSHA      string            \`json:"short_sha"\`
    Message       string            \`json:"message"\`
    Author        string            \`json:"author"\`
    AuthorEmail   string            \`json:"author_email"\`
    Timestamp     time.Time         \`json:"timestamp"\`
    Files         []FileChange      \`json:"files"\`
    Method        CorrelationMethod \`json:"method"\`
    Confidence    float64           \`json:"confidence"\`
    Reason        string            \`json:"reason"\`
}

type FileChange struct {
    Path       string \`json:"path"\`
    Action     string \`json:"action"\`     // A=added, M=modified, D=deleted
    Insertions int    \`json:"insertions"\`
    Deletions  int    \`json:"deletions"\`
}

type CorrelationMethod string
const (
    MethodCoCommitted    CorrelationMethod = "co_committed"
    MethodExplicitID     CorrelationMethod = "explicit_id"
    MethodTemporalAuthor CorrelationMethod = "temporal_author"
)
\`\`\`

### BeadHistory
The complete correlation record for a single bead.

### HistoryReport
The top-level output structure for --robot-history.

## Design Decisions
- Separate Events from milestones (Created/Claimed/Closed) for quick access
- Include CommitIndex in HistoryReport for O(1) reverse lookup
- CycleTime as pointer since not all beads have been closed

## File Location
\`pkg/correlation/types.go\`

## Acceptance Criteria
1. All types compile without errors
2. JSON tags are consistent and documented
3. String methods implemented for enum types
4. Unit tests for serialization roundtrips
5. Go doc comments on all exported types
close_reason: Implemented all types in pkg/correlation/types.go with full test coverage

title: Bead History & Code Correlation System
description:
# Bead History & Code Correlation System

## Overview
Implement a system to track the full lifecycle of beads (creation, claim, modification, closure) and correlate them with the git commits that implemented the work. This enables traceability from issue to code without requiring LLM inference.

## User Stories
- As a project lead, I want to see what code changes were made for each bead
- As a developer, I want to know which bead drove a particular commit
- As an AI agent, I want structured JSON data about bead-to-commit relationships
- As a code reviewer, I want to understand the context behind changes

## The Core Insight
The git history of beads.jsonl IS the source of truth:
1. **Exact timestamps** - when each bead was created, modified, closed
2. **Git author** - the agent/person who made each change  
3. **Commit SHA** - links to all other files changed in that commit

This creates a direct, mechanical correlation chain with no LLM needed.

## Correlation Methods (by confidence)
| Method | Confidence | Description |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| co_committed | 95-99% | Code changed in same commit as bead status change |
| explicit_id | 90-95% | Commit message contains bead ID |
| temporal_author | 40-70% | Same author, within bead's active window |

## Success Criteria
- [ ] \`bv --robot-history\` outputs structured JSON of all correlations
- [ ] \`bv --bead-history <id>\` shows history for a specific bead
- [ ] TUI shows history in detail pane and dedicated view
- [ ] Co-committed correlations have >95% accuracy
- [ ] Performance: <2s for repos with 1000 commits

## Non-Goals (for initial release)
- Real-time commit watching (future enhancement)
- GitHub/GitLab PR integration (future enhancement)
- Automatic bead creation from commits (different feature)

## Implementation Phases
1. **Phase 1 (MVP)**: Core extraction + robot-history flag
2. **Phase 2**: Additional correlation methods + confidence scoring
3. **Phase 3**: TUI integration
4. **Phase 4**: Performance optimization
5. **Phase 5**: Testing and documentation

See docs/bead-history-feature-plan.md for complete specification.
close_reason: All 15 core children completed: history types, git parser, co-commit extraction, robot-history CLI, explicit/temporal correlation, confidence scoring, reverse lookup, TUI view/detail/keybindings, caching, incremental updates, large repo optimization, test suite. Only docs remaining (bv-78)

title: Reuse analysis/cache across robot commands

notes: Robot outputs now share cached analyzer across insights/plan/priority; status and metrics reuse same run; go test ./... clean.

title: Surface metric status/timeouts in robot outputs
description: Problem: timeouts/approximations are hidden from robot outputs; agents canвҖҷt tell if PageRank/Betweenness/HITS/cycles are partial. Keep payload small (enums).

description: Problem: robot-insights JSON only includes top lists because GraphStats stores metrics in unexported maps; agents canвҖҷt look up per-issue scores. Consider payload size on large graphs and offer opt-in for full maps.

title: Implement priority scoring function
description:
Create composite scoring combining PageRank (30%), Betweenness (30%), Blocker count (20%), Staleness (10%), Explicit priority (10%).

## Algorithm
func (a *Analyzer) ComputeImpactScore(issue *model.Issue) ImpactScore:
  - Normalize all metrics to 0-1 range
  - Apply weights: 0.3*pagerank + 0.3*betweenness + 0.2*blockerRatio + 0.1*staleness + 0.1*priorityBoost
  - Return: IssueID, ImpactScore, ScoreBreakdown map

## Staleness Penalty
- Days since update, normalized: min(daysSinceUpdate / 30, 1.0)
- Older items get slight penalty to surface them

## Priority Boost
- P0=1.0, P1=0.75, P2=0.5, P3=0.25, P4=0.0 (higher priority = higher boost)

## File Changes
- pkg/analysis/priority.go: New scoring logic
- pkg/analysis/priority_test.go: Tests with known values

title: Generate priority recommendations with explanations
description:
Compare impact scores to current priorities and generate actionable recommendations.

## Output Structure
type PriorityRecommendation struct {
    IssueID           string
    CurrentPriority   int
    SuggestedPriority int
    ImpactScore       float64
    Confidence        float64  // 0-1, higher when score difference is large
    Reasoning         []string // Human-readable explanations
}

## Reasoning Templates
- 'Blocks N high-priority items' (when UnblocksCount > 2)
- 'Critical path bottleneck' (when Betweenness > 0.5)
- 'High centrality in graph' (when PageRank > 0.3)
- 'Stale for N days' (when staleness penalty applied)

## Confidence Calculation
- Large score vs priority mismatch = high confidence
- Multiple reasoning factors = higher confidence
- Single weak signal = low confidence

## File Changes
- pkg/analysis/priority.go: Add recommendation generation
- pkg/analysis/priority_test.go: Test recommendations

title: Add --robot-priority CLI flag
description:

  }],

}

## Sorting

title: Implement recipe loader with embedded defaults
description:
Load recipes from embedded defaults and user configuration, with proper merging.

## Loading Order (later overrides earlier)
1. Embedded defaults (compiled into binary)
2. User config at ~/.config/bv/recipes.yaml (global)
3. Project config at .bv/recipes.yaml (local)

## Embedded Defaults
Use Go embed to include default recipes:
//go:embed defaults/recipes.yaml
var defaultRecipes []byte

## Merge Strategy
- User recipes with same name override defaults completely
- User can disable a default recipe: triage: null
- Unknown keys in user config generate warnings

Enables --robot-recipes for AI agent discovery.

title: Add GetExecutionPlan() with parallel track detection
description:
Generate a dependency-respecting execution plan with parallel tracks identified for concurrent work.

## Data Structure
type ExecutionPlan struct {
    Tracks []ExecutionTrack
    Total  int
}

type ExecutionTrack struct {
    TrackID string
    Items   []PlanItem
    Reason  string  // Why these are grouped
}

type PlanItem struct {
    ID          string
    Title       string
    Priority    int
    UnblocksIDs []string  // What becomes actionable when done
}

## Algorithm
1. Get actionable issues from GetActionableIssues()
2. Identify connected components among actionable issues
3. Within each component, sort by priority then topological order
4. For each item, compute what it unblocks

## Considerations
- Parallel Tracks: Independent subgraphs can be worked on concurrently
- Unblocks Info: Helps prioritize - 'doing X unblocks 5 other tasks'
- Priority Integration: Within tracks, respect explicit priority

## File Changes
- pkg/analysis/plan.go: New file with plan generation logic
- pkg/analysis/plan_test.go: Tests

title: Add GetActionableIssues() to analysis package
description:
Identify issues that can be worked on immediately - those with no open blocking dependencies.

## Algorithm
func (a *Analyzer) GetActionableIssues() []model.Issue:
  - Skip closed issues
  - Skip issues with open blockers (only 'blocks' type creates hard deps)
  - Missing blockers don't block (graceful degradation)

## Key Design Decisions
1. Only 'blocks' type creates hard dependencies - 'related' and 'parent-child' are informational
2. Missing blockers don't block - Graceful degradation when referenced issue doesn't exist
3. Closed issues are never actionable - They're done

## File Changes
- pkg/analysis/graph.go: Add methods to Analyzer struct
- pkg/analysis/graph_test.go: Add comprehensive tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Returns only open/in_progress/blocked issues
- [ ] Correctly identifies issues with no open blockers
- [ ] Handles missing dependency references gracefully
- [ ] Handles cycles gracefully
- [ ] Unit tests cover all edge cases

title: Time-Travel Graph Diffing
description:
Compare graph structure across git commits to understand how the project evolved and detect structural regressions.

## Background & Motivation
Since beads are stored in git, we have full history available. Questions like 'What changed since last week?' or 'Did my changes introduce a bottleneck?' are answerable but not currently exposed.

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: Visualize progress, understand what changed during a sprint, postmortem analysis
- For AI Agents: Structural regression detection - validate changes before committing

## Technical Approach
1. Add git history loader (read files at specific commit)
2. Create snapshot comparison logic
3. Generate diff report (new/closed issues, metric changes, new cycles)
4. CLI: --diff-since <commit|date> and --as-of <commit|date>
5. TUI: Highlight changed items

## Key Features
- Load historical state without checking out
- Compare metrics (PageRank, betweenness) between snapshots
- Detect new cycles or bottlenecks
- 'What would change if I close these issues?' simulation

title: AI-Aware Kanban Autoprioritizer
description:
Use graph metrics (PageRank, betweenness, freshness, blocker count) to suggest priority adjustments and surface high-impact items.

## Background & Motivation
The analysis package computes rich metrics but they're only visible in the insights panel. Users and agents want actionable recommendations: 'These 5 items should be prioritized because they unblock the most work.'

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: 'Smart suggestions' that explain why items should be reprioritized
- For AI Agents: --robot-priority provides a ranked work queue with reasoning

## Technical Approach
