title: Multi-Repo Aggregation View
description:
Unified view across multiple .beads/ directories for monorepos and multi-service projects.

## Background & Motivation
Organizations often have:
- Monorepos with multiple services
- Microservices with separate repos
- Frontend/backend splits

Currently, bv only shows one project at a time.

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: Single view of all project issues
- For AI Agents: Cross-repo dependency visibility, scoped queries

## Technical Approach
1. Workspace configuration file
2. Namespaced IDs (repo:issue-id)
3. Aggregate loader merging multiple sources
4. Cross-repo dependency support
5. Repo-scoped filtering

## Key Challenges
- ID collision handling
- Performance with many repos
- Cross-repo dependency semantics
- Clear provenance in UI
close_reason: All subtasks (bv-epf.1-.4) complete: workspace schema + namespacing + aggregate loader + CLI/TUI repo filter.

title: Actionable Exports & Hooks System
description:
Enhance exports to include runnable commands and add a hook system for automation.

## Background & Motivation
Current exports are read-only reports. Users want exports that enable action: 'here's the command to close these resolved issues' or 'here's the PR description for this work'.

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: Exports include copy-paste ready commands
- For AI Agents: Chain exports вҶ’ actions without manual parsing

## Technical Approach
1. Enhance markdown export with bd command snippets
2. Add hook system (pre-export, post-export)
3. Hook configuration in .bv/hooks.yaml
4. Environment variables passed to hooks

## Hook Use Cases
- Post to Slack after export
- Create GitHub issue from bead
- Trigger CI pipeline
- Sync to external tracker

## Security Considerations
- Hooks are opt-in (require explicit config)
- Commands are not auto-executed
- Hook output captured but not interpreted
close_reason: All subtasks (bv-qjc.1-.3) complete: command snippets in exports + hooks config + export pipeline integration.

title: bt robot pair show <suffix>: drill-in subcommand for pair triage
description:
## Why

To judge whether a pair is intentional or suffix-collision, the operator
needs both/all members' title + description + existing deps. Today that's
multiple `bt robot list` dumps + filter passes (see bt-we7z for the ID
filter wish-list item).

A focused subcommand would make pair triage a single command:

```bash
bt robot pair show 153
```

Returns every issue with suffix `153` (via `analysis.SplitID`) вҖ” across
projects if `--global` вҖ” with title, description, status, priority,
source_repo, and existing deps. Pure read; no writes.

## Proposed change

New subcommand `bt robot pair show <suffix>`:

- Takes one arg: the suffix (`153`, `mhwy.2`, `zsy8`).
- Scans issue set (respects `--global`) for members sharing that suffix.
- Emits JSON record per member with fields: `id, title, description,
  status, priority, source_repo, dependencies` (the full dep list, not
  filtered).
- Also emits one envelope-level field: `suggests_pair: true|false` based on
  the pair.v2 rule (dep edge present вҶ’ true, missing вҶ’ false).

Composes naturally with `bt robot pairs --orphaned`: given the orphaned
output, `bt robot pair show <suffix>` is the drill-in for each row.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `robot pair show <suffix>` subcommand under existing `robot pairs`
      OR as a new `robot pair` parent (design call)
- [ ] Unit test covering multi-member suffix, single-member (degenerate),
      unknown suffix (empty result)
- [ ] Integration into `bt robot schema` output
- [ ] Consider: does this replace or complement bt-92ic (enriched
      --orphaned)? Probably complements вҖ” enriched --orphaned is
      batch-triage; `pair show` is per-suffix drill-in.

## Dependencies

Doesn't block anything. Can ship after Phase 2 of bt-gkyn (where pair.v2
semantics are locked) or earlier as a v1-compatible reader.

title: bt robot list --global silently truncates at --limit=100 default
description:
## Bug

`bt robot list --global` defaults `--limit=100`. When the global corpus is
larger than 100 (currently ~2770 issues on the shared server), the output
is silently truncated with no signal to the consumer.

During bt-gkyn Phase 1 triage this caused an initial jq filter to return
3/44 expected matches вҖ” not obvious until I noticed `.issues | length = 100`.

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
bt robot list --global > /tmp/all.json
jq '.issues | length' /tmp/all.json
# 100 вҖ” not 2770
```

## Root Cause

`cmd/bt/cobra_robot.go` registers `--limit` with default `100` for list.
The default is fine for single-project scopes (most projects have <100
issues) but silently undercounts `--global`.

## Proposed fix

Option A (lowest friction): when `len(issues) == limit`, emit a stderr
warning that the result may be truncated.

Option B: under `--global`, default `--limit=0` (unlimited) instead of 100.
Leaves non-global invocations unchanged.

Option A keeps the 100-default contract but adds visibility. Option B
changes the default for `--global` consumers. Either option resolves the
silent-truncation failure mode.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Either a stderr warning on truncated output OR a different default
      under `--global`
- [ ] Test covering the truncation signal
- [ ] Update `bt robot list --help` text to document the chosen behavior

title: bt robot list --ids <csv>: direct ID filter, avoids full-corpus dump for targeted queries
description:
## Why

Pulling N specific issues by ID from `--global` (~2770 issues on the shared
Dolt server today) requires dumping the full list and filtering client-side.
During bt-gkyn Phase 1 triage this meant:

```bash
bt robot list --global --limit=0 --full > /tmp/all.json
jq '.issues[] | select(.id | IN("bt-153","mkt-153",...))' /tmp/all.json
```

N = 44 IDs needed, 2770 scanned. Two-step workflow for what should be a
single query.

## Proposed change

Add `--ids <csv>` flag to `bt robot list`:

```bash
bt robot list --global --ids=bt-153,mkt-153,cnvs-1hk,...
```

- Accepts comma-separated ID list. Whitespace trimmed.
- Respects `--global` (cross-project) and single-project scope.
- Returns only matching IDs. Unknown IDs silently skipped (match existing
  dangling-dep tolerance in pair detection) вҖ” or flagged on stderr with a
  count, implementer's call.
- Output shape unchanged (same envelope, same record shape).
- Downstream implications: `--limit` still applies but is irrelevant when
  `--ids` narrows the set.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `--ids` flag registered on `robotListCmd`
- [ ] Filter applies after source/label/BQL scoping
- [ ] Unit test: known IDs return N matches; unknown IDs don't fail
- [ ] Update `bt robot schema` entry for robot-list
- [ ] Benchmark confirms O(k) not O(n) (hash-set lookup)

title: bt robot pairs --orphaned: enrich members with title/status/source_repo for single-pass triage
description:
## Why

`bt robot pairs --global --schema=v1 --orphaned` emits one JSONL record per
v1-detected pair missing a dep edge:

```json
{"suffix":"153","members":["bt-153","mkt-153"],"suggested_command":"bd dep add bt-153 mkt-153 --type=related"}
```

To judge intentional-vs-collision the operator has to cross-reference each
member against a separate `bt robot list --global --limit=0` dump to read
titles. This is two commands and a join per triage pass.

## Proposed change

Enrich each member with title + status + source_repo so the triage read is
single-pass:

```json
{
  "suffix": "153",
  "members": [
    {"id":"bt-153","title":"K-shortest critical paths","status":"closed","source_repo":"bt"},
    {"id":"mkt-153","title":"Rich description nudge hook","status":"open","source_repo":"mkt"}
  ],
  "suggested_command": "bd dep add bt-153 mkt-153 --type=related"
}
```

Same shape on stdout, still JSONL, no new flags. Implementation: swap
`OrphanedPair.Members []string` for `[]OrphanedPairMember` in
`cmd/bt/robot_pairs.go`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] OrphanedPair emits members as objects with id/title/status/source_repo
- [ ] Unit test for the new shape
- [ ] Update convention doc / `bt robot schema` entry for robot-pairs
- [ ] Verified on real shared-server corpus

title: Auto-export git add silently fails вҖ” .beads/issues.jsonl tracked in history but ignored
description:
## Bug

`.beads/issues.jsonl` is tracked in git history (`git log --oneline -- .beads/issues.jsonl` shows multiple commits) but currently ignored by `.gitignore` вҖ” `git check-ignore -v` returns no match, yet `git add` without `-f` fails with "paths are ignored by one of your .gitignore files." The auto-export hook that runs `bd close`/`bd create` warns `auto-export: git add failed: exit status 1` on every mutation.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run `bd close <id> --reason "..."`
2. Observe the stderr warning:
   ```
   Warning: auto-export: git add failed: exit status 1
   ```
3. Run `git status` вҖ” `.beads/issues.jsonl` shows as modified.
4. Run `git add .beads/issues.jsonl` вҖ” fails with "paths are ignored."
5. Run `git add -f .beads/issues.jsonl` вҖ” succeeds.

## Expected

Either:
- The file is tracked and auto-export's `git add` just works (remove whatever rule ignores it), OR
- The file is not tracked and auto-export stops trying to `git add` it (configure the export to skip git add, or document the file as intentionally untracked).

Current state: worst of both вҖ” the file is committed historically, auto-export wants to commit it, but `git add` silently fails on every run. Every session produces the warning; every session-close commit needs manual `-f`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Session-close flow (bd close вҶ’ git commit) works without `-f` or stderr warnings.
- No `auto-export: git add failed` messages in normal workflow.
- `.beads/issues.jsonl` tracking decision is explicit (either in or out of version control, not ambiguous).

## Relates

- bd-c3r (upstream beads project tracks the "warning is noise" side; this is the bt-side misconfiguration).
- Discovered during bt-mhwy.2 close (2026-04-20).

## Notes

Other nested `.beads-local/` and similar patterns are in `.gitignore`, but nothing in the visible rules explains why `.beads/issues.jsonl` specifically is blocked. Might be an inherited rule from a submodule/worktree, a per-user git config, or a `.git/info/exclude` entry. Investigation starts with `git config --show-origin --get-all core.excludesfile` and scanning all .gitignore layers.

comments.author: sms
comments.text:
Root cause identified (2026-04-20): not `.gitignore` вҖ” the block comes from `.git/info/exclude`:

```
# Beads fork protection (bd init)
.beads/
**/RECOVERY*.md
**/SESSION*.md
```

This is per-repo and NOT in version control. It was placed by `bd init` as fork-protection against accidentally committing Dolt state (`.beads/dolt/`, `.beads/dolt-server.log`, credential files) that live alongside `issues.jsonl`.

`git check-ignore -v .beads/issues.jsonl` returns nothing because `git check-ignore` doesn't consult `.git/info/exclude` the same way вҖ” hence the earlier confusion.

This makes the fix less trivial than the description suggests:
- A `.gitignore` carve-out `!.beads/issues.jsonl` won't work because `.git/info/exclude` takes effect regardless.
- A `.git/info/exclude` carve-out (`!.beads/issues.jsonl` after the `.beads/` line) fixes it per-clone only вҖ” doesn't help fresh checkouts.
- The real fix is in upstream `bd init`: stop writing the broad `.beads/` rule, or write it as `.beads/*` with `!.beads/issues.jsonl` exception. Or have auto-export skip the git-add step when the file is excluded.

Reframing this bead: it's an upstream beads concern, not a bt misconfig. Possibly closes as "upstream" or gets paired with a bd-side follow-up.

title: TUI: shift+S to reverse-cycle sort order (symmetric with other reverse-cycle keybinds)
description:
## Why

`s` in the TUI cycles sort order forward. Other cycle-based keybindings in the TUI support reverse cycling (typically `shift+<key>` or a distinct key). Sort doesn't, which means to go back one sort mode you have to cycle all the way around.

## What

Add reverse sort cycling. Two natural options:
1. `S` (shift+s) cycles backward, `s` still cycles forward вҖ” most consistent with Unix/vim conventions and with other bt reverse patterns
2. `s` forward, a dedicated backward key (e.g., `shift+s` with same mapping as option 1)

Option 1 is the ergonomic ask.

## Scope

- In: reverse sort cycle on the issue list (global + per-project views)
- Out: changing the sort modes themselves
- Out: applying to non-list views unless they already have forward sort (check triage view specifically вҖ” observed during dogfood that modal behaviour differs there)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `s` cycles sort forward (current behaviour, unchanged)
- `S` (shift+s) cycles sort backward through the same modes in reverse
- Works across the views where forward sort currently applies (default list, triage, filtered)
- Documented wherever sort is hinted (footer, help overlay, `?`)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open bt
2. Press `s` N times to cycle sort N modes forward
3. Try to go back one mode вҖ” no way to reverse; must cycle (modes - 1) forward again

## Relates

- `bt-xavk` (help system redesign) вҖ” this keybinding should land in the audit there; ensures the shortcut doesn't stay undocumented like `shift+<`/`shift+>` did
- `bt-gf3d` (keybinding consistency epic) вҖ” parent-level fit
- `bt-tkhq` (TUI keybinding research) вҖ” if that lands first, align with its conventions for reverse-cycle

## Notes

Dogfood discovery 2026-04-20. Small ergonomic; high ratio of value-to-effort.

title: Establish periodic schema-drift detection between bt and upstream beads
description:
## Why

Upstream beads ships ~30 releases / 4 months. bt's Dolt readers query a fixed column list; silent drift is inevitable. One-time audits are necessary (see schema-drift-audit bead) but not sufficient вҖ” drift resumes the moment the audit ships.

Without periodic detection, we will re-live the same discovery every quarter: another missing column, another rush bead, another brainstorm tangent. The user's own CLAUDE.md flags this explicitly: *"we need to find a better way for this project to periodically analyze the beads changelog to make sure we're staying current."*

## What

Establish an automated mechanism that detects schema drift between bt and upstream beads. Design phase picks the best fit for existing infrastructure; candidate approaches:

- **CI test**: Go test that reads upstream migrations, constructs the current schema, compares against bt's `IssuesColumns`. Fails CI if drift detected.
- **Scheduled/cron bead**: background job that diffs and files a drift-alert bead automatically when new columns appear upstream.
- **Upstream release hook**: on upstream beads release, run drift check; open a bead if diff.

Design decision recorded via `bd decision` before implementation.

## Scope

- Out: fixing the drift вҖ” that is the job of bt-mhwy.0-style beads spawned by the detector
- Out: alerting beyond the issues-table family (gates, molecules, wisps вҖ” a separate detector if needed)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Automated mechanism flags new drift within a release cycle of upstream shipping it
- False positives low enough to be useful (no crying wolf on harmless additions)
- Integrates with existing test/CI infrastructure rather than standing up net-new systems

## Relates

- schema-drift-audit (this is the recurring version of the one-time audit)
- bt-mhwy.0 (the implementation pattern this detector would trigger future versions of)

title: Session close: flag formatter-induced diffs outside current task scope
description:
## Bug

When an agent session runs a workspace-wide formatter (`go fmt ./...`, `gofmt -w`, `goimports`, `prettier`, etc.) as part of a verification pass, changes to files outside the current task's scope can silently accumulate in the working tree. They're not intentional, not malicious, and not flagged at session close вҖ” they just sit there uncommitted until a future session picks them up days later.

Confirmed example: session `5f7c624f-6d86-407e-84a3-892b2501c95c.jsonl` (2026-04-16) ran `go fmt` across the entire workspace while fixing bt-ju7o/bt-hqym. The fixes shipped. The ~56 files of incidental gofmt output sat uncommitted until 2026-04-18 when a later session noticed the drift and committed it as a separate chore (`be9a39e5`).

## Why it matters

- Future sessions see a giant "noise" diff that looks unrelated to current work, eating attention on diagnosis before real work starts
- If the formatter drift is tangled with real WIP, surgical committing is harder
- Root cause is invisible: the session that did it already closed, its transcript is in cass but the mechanism (formatter side-effect) isn't indexed as a bash command

## Proposed fix

At session close (wrapup skill or bt session-close protocol):

1. Diff working tree vs HEAD. If there are tracked-file modifications that weren't explicitly touched by the session's Edit/Write actions, flag them to the user before committing/pushing.
2. Specifically for formatter tools (`go fmt`, `gofmt -w`, `prettier --write`, etc.) used during the session: after running, snapshot what files got touched; list them explicitly in the session summary so the user knows there's a choice to make (commit as chore, revert, or bundle).

## Related

- Session that caused it: `5f7c624f-6d86-407e-84a3-892b2501c95c.jsonl`
- Landed chore commit: `be9a39e5 chore: gofmt column alignment across files touched by prior sessions`
- Surface the formatter-command-ran signal in cass (see bt-tjq0 neighbor: agent observability improvements)

## Acceptance

- [ ] Wrapup/session-close surfaces working-tree drift that's outside the session's touched-file set
- [ ] Running a workspace-wide formatter is flagged explicitly in the session summary (not just hidden inside a "verification" subsection)
- [ ] User is asked to decide (commit separately / revert / bundle) before the session closes

title: Apply databasesWithTable resilience to labels and dependencies loaders
description:
## Task

loadAllLabels and loadAllDependencies in global_dolt.go use UNION ALL across all databases without checking table existence first. loadAllComments was fixed in bt-ju7o with a databasesWithTable() helper that pre-filters databases. Apply the same pattern to labels and deps for cross-version database compatibility.

## Context

Today all 15 databases have all tables. But if a new project is initialized with a different beads version, or if a table is dropped, the UNION ALL fails and silently drops ALL labels/deps for ALL issues.

## Acceptance Criteria
- loadAllLabels uses databasesWithTable before building UNION ALL
- loadAllDependencies uses databasesWithTable before building UNION ALL
close_reason: Applied databasesWithTable to loadAllLabels and loadAllDependencies. Same pattern as comments fix.

title: BQL query modal UX polish: syntax hints, autocomplete, visual cleanup
description:
## Task

The BQL query modal (: key) works functionally but the UX is rough:
- No syntax hints or autocomplete for field names
- No inline help for available operators
- Visual presentation needs polish (matches alerts/label picker standards)
- Not discoverable - users don't know what queries are valid

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Modal shows available fields and operators
- [ ] Visual styling consistent with other modals
- [ ] Basic autocomplete or tab-completion for field names
- [ ] Error messages for invalid queries are helpful

title: Standardize modal footer format across all overlays
description:
## Task

Modal footers use inconsistent formats across the TUI. Standardize to the action: key format established in the alerts modal redesign (bt-46p6.5).

## Current State

- Alerts: action: key format with shift hints - DONE
- Repo picker: action: key format - DONE  
- Label picker: action: key format - DONE
- BQL modal: key: action with pipes
- Recipe picker: key: action with dots
- Agent prompt: prose style
- CASS session: [key] Action bracketed style
- Flow matrix: Key: Action capitalized

## Target

All modals use: action: key (modifier) format, bullet separator, no redundant esc hints.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All modal footers use consistent action: key format
- [ ] Shift/modifier hints use parenthetical style
- [ ] No redundant esc/close hints

title: PageRank/centrality: improve exposure and alert-to-insights navigation
description:
## Task

The PageRank/centrality data is computed and available in the Insights view, but the drift alert just says "top 10 shifted" with no way to see or act on the actual rankings. The alert should link to the insights view, and the centrality data needs a clearer exposure path.

## Current State

- Insights view (ViewInsights, key: i) already shows PageRank data
- Drift alert checkPageRankChanges() fires when top-10 items shuffle
- Alert message lists which items entered/left the top 10
- CLI robot-drift outputs rankings in JSON
- No way to navigate from alert вҶ’ insights view

## Design

1. When user presses enter on a pagerank_change alert, navigate to Insights view instead of trying to jump to a specific issue
2. Consider adding a "centrality" section to the detail panel showing the selected issue's graph metrics
3. Evaluate whether the pagerank alert type is useful enough to keep, or whether insights view makes it redundant

## Open Questions
- Is the drift alert for PageRank changes useful, or is the Insights view sufficient?
- Should centrality metrics appear in the issue detail panel alongside graph analysis?
- Would agents benefit from a dedicated --centrality robot subcommand?
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Enter on pagerank alert navigates to insights view (not a specific issue)
- [ ] Alert usefulness evaluated and documented (keep/modify/remove recommendation)
- [ ] Centrality data accessible without entering insights view (detail panel or similar)

title: Notification center: separate informational alerts from actionable ones
description:
## Feature

Design and implement a notification center that separates informational updates from actionable alerts. Some current alert types (actionable_change, node/edge count changes) are notifications, not alerts - they tell you something changed but don't require action.

## Context

The user identified a taxonomy gap: alerts (act on this) vs notifications (be aware of this). Currently everything is lumped into the alerts panel. The status bar already has a bead open about giving updates (cross-session log) which connects to this concept.

## Design Direction

### Option A: Tab within alerts modal
- Same overlay frame, key to flip between "Alerts" and "Activity" tabs
- Alerts: actionable items (stale, cascade, loops, abandoned)
- Activity: informational items (count changes, density shifts, cross-session log)
- Single entry point, separate views

### Option B: Separate notification view
- Dedicated view mode (like insights, board, graph)
- Richer display: timeline, grouping by session/time
- Higher implementation cost

### Option C: Merge into alerts with type filtering
- Keep one list, but the type filter effectively separates them
- "Actionable" types vs "informational" types as a filter preset
- Lowest implementation cost, builds on bt-46p6.5

## Open Questions
- Should notifications include cross-session activity (bead created/closed by other sessions)?
- Should this integrate with the real-time Dolt polling for live updates?
- What's the minimum viable version - Option C (filter presets) vs Option A (tabs)?

## Related
- Status bar updates bead (in-progress elsewhere)
- Cross-session log concept
- bt-46p6.5 (stackable filters) - Option C builds directly on this
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Informational alerts (count changes, density) separated from actionable alerts
- [ ] User can view both categories through a clear UI mechanism
- [ ] Actionable vs informational classification documented per alert type
- [ ] Entry point discoverable from main TUI (not hidden)

title: Rename alert type taxonomy for human readability
description:
## Task

Rename alert type constants to be human-readable and accurate. Current names use internal jargon that doesn't communicate meaning to users.

## Proposed Renames

| Current | Proposed | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| new_cycle | dependency_loop | "cycle" is graph theory jargon; "dependency loop" communicates the problem |
| blocking_cascade | high_leverage | describes what it IS (actionable leverage point) not what it does |
| stale_issue | stale | shorter, clear |
| blocked_increase | blocked_surge | "increase" is vague; "surge" communicates sudden rise |
| actionable_change | actionable_shift | "change" is generic; "shift" implies directional movement |
| density_growth | coupling_growth | "density" is graph theory; "coupling" communicates what it means for users |
| pagerank_change | centrality_shift | "pagerank" is an algorithm name; "centrality" describes the concept |
| node_count_change | graph_resize | more descriptive of what happened |
| edge_count_change | dependency_change | edges ARE dependencies; rename accordingly |

## Impact

- pkg/drift/drift.go - AlertType constants
- cmd/bt/robot_alerts.go - --alert-type filter accepts these strings
- Any drift config files using disabled_alerts with old names
- Need backward-compat in config parsing (accept old names, map to new)

## Open Question

Some of these renames are debatable. The epic owner should approve final names before implementation. The key principle: names should describe what the user cares about, not the algorithm that detected it.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] All alert type constants renamed to human-readable names
- [ ] CLI --alert-type accepts both old and new names (backward compat)
- [ ] Drift config disabled_alerts accepts both old and new names
- [ ] TUI displays new names
- [ ] No breaking changes to existing configs or CLI scripts
notes: CLI backward compat needed: robot_alerts.go --alert-type flag accepts string values. Old names must still work in drift config disabled_alerts. Add a mapping layer, don't break existing configs.

title: Swarm viz (s) needs better error UX when no valid epic selected
description:
## Bug

Pressing `s` in graph view shows "swarm: bd swarm validate: exit status 1" when the selected issue isn't a valid epic or when bd swarm validate fails. The error message is unhelpful - the user doesn't know what to do.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open graph view (`g`)
2. Navigate to any non-epic issue
3. Press `s`
4. Red error bar shows "swarm: bd swarm validate: exit status 1"

## Root Cause

`model_keys.go` handleGraphKeys `s` case calls `loadSwarmData` which runs `bd swarm validate <id> --json`. When the issue isn't an epic, bd returns exit status 1 with a JSON error. bt surfaces the raw exit code instead of the meaningful error.

## Fix

- Parse stderr/JSON error from bd swarm validate to extract the actual message
- Show "select an epic to enable swarm view" when the issue type isn't epic
- Consider disabling `s` hint in graph footer when not on an epic

## Acceptance Criteria

- Clear actionable error message when pressing s on a non-epic
- bd swarm validate stderr surfaced in error message, not just exit code
close_reason: Parse bd swarm validate JSON error response instead of showing raw exit status. Shows friendly message for non-epic issues, actual error text otherwise.

title: Wisp visibility toggle in list view
description:
## Why
Beads supports ephemeral issues (wisps) that are deliberately lightweight and disposable. bt currently has no way to show/hide wisps, and no visual distinction for ephemeral issues. Once the data model is extended with the ephemeral field (bt-c4da), bt should let users toggle wisp visibility.

## What
- Filter toggle in list view to show/hide ephemeral issues
- Visual indicator on ephemeral issues (dimmed, dashed border, or ghost icon)
- Default: hide wisps (matches bd ready behavior)

## Files to change
- pkg/ui/model_filter.go: add ephemeral filter toggle
- pkg/ui/delegate.go: visual distinction for ephemeral issues
- pkg/ui/model_keys.go: keybinding for toggle

## Complexity
LOW once bt-c4da is complete (needs ephemeral field in model).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Keybinding toggles wisp visibility in list view
- [ ] Ephemeral issues have distinct visual treatment when visible
- [ ] Default state hides wisps
- [ ] Toggle state persists within session

## Discovered from
bd-0il feature audit, bt-mol-qvjm research-audit molecule
close_reason: wisp toggle: w key, ephemeral filter in all view paths, dimmed styling, footer badge

title: Improve startup info output: format, usefulness, and clarity
description:
## Why

When bt boots, the startup messages (shared server detection, database discovery, connection status) are raw debug-style output. The information is useful but the format isn't - it's hard to quickly parse what happened.

## What

Replace individual log lines with a brief structured summary showing:
- What server mode was detected
- How many databases found
- Which project is current
- Whether the poll loop is active

## Acceptance Criteria

- Startup output is cleanly formatted and structured
- Key information (server mode, databases, current project, poll status) is clearly surfaced
- Output is actionable - easy to tell at a glance if something is wrong

title: Project registry: surface what each repo is and who owns what for cross-project agents
description:
## Why

When an agent in one project (e.g. beads) needs to assess impact on another project (bt, marketplace), it has no way to discover what those projects ARE without reading their codebases. There's no index, no registry, no machine-readable summary of projects on the shared Dolt server.

Discovered during the bd-0il audit - had to manually describe what bt and mkt are so the beads session could scope its work. This should be agent-discoverable.

## What

Create a project registry that maps each database on the shared server to: name, purpose, ownership domain, what it builds, key dependencies. The registry should be queryable by agents without leaving their project.

### Implementation options (need design decision)
1. `bt robot projects` - bt as orchestration layer surfaces the index via CLI
2. `bd metadata` per database - beads stores project descriptions in Dolt
3. Convention file on the shared server - static, low overhead
4. bt global view showing project cards/summaries - TUI-native

### Ownership split
- beads owns the data (bd metadata per database)
- bt owns the UI (global view project cards)
- marketplace owns the convention for populating it

## Acceptance Criteria

- Agent in any project can discover what other projects exist and what they do
- No filesystem traversal or cass search required - data is in the Dolt server
- Project summaries include: name, one-line purpose, primary language/framework, key labels
notes: Connected to bd-mh6 epic. Discovered while trying to write cross-project audit prompts - had to manually describe what bt and mkt are so the beads session could scope its work. This should be machine-readable and agent-discoverable.

title: Document local-to-shared server migration path for bt users
description:
## Why

bt's global mode requires projects to be on the shared Dolt server. Users starting with local-only projects need a clear, agent-friendly migration path. bt is developing the global model and needs to help users adopt it.

## What

Document the local-to-shared server migration workflow so that:
1. A fresh session with no context can guide a user through migration
2. bt can detect local-only projects and suggest migration
3. The process is documented in bt's help/tutorial system

The actual migration is `bd init --shared-server` on existing projects, but the full workflow needs coverage: backup considerations, what happens to existing data, verifying it worked.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Migration path documented and agent-readable
- Covers backup, execution (`bd init --shared-server`), and verification steps
- Connected to bt-zsy8 (shared server startup) and the broader CRUD story
notes: Connected to bt-zsy8 (shared server startup) and the broader CRUD story. Even though migration executes via bd, bt should surface it in the TUI or at minimum have agent-readable docs for it.
close_reason: Consolidated into bt-ammc (global mode user docs). Migration path documentation is now a section within the broader global mode guide rather than a standalone doc.

title: Status bar messages persist until manual interaction
description: When the status bar at the bottom updates (e.g., after a command completes or a status change), the message persists indefinitely until the user presses escape or performs another action. Status bar messages should auto-dismiss after a timeout (2-3 seconds for info, longer for errors). This is a UX polish issue - stale status messages create confusion about current state.
close_reason: Fixed in e1a27133 - shared server startup, search preservation, status bar auto-dismiss

title: Scroll acceleration / speed ramp on main issues list
description:
## Why

Scrolling through a long issues list one item at a time is tedious. Native UIs use kinetic/inertial scrolling to let users move faster through large lists.

## What

Ramp up scroll speed based on sustained input - start moving one issue at a time, then accelerate to skip multiple rows as the user keeps scrolling in the same direction. Decelerate when scrolling stops or reverses.

Lives in the Bubble Tea update loop where j/k or arrow key inputs are handled - track consecutive same-direction inputs and scale cursor movement accordingly.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Sustained scrolling in the same direction accelerates cursor movement
- Scrolling stops or reverses direction resets to single-step speed
- Acceleration feels natural, not jarring

title: DR: documentation + status indicator
description:
## Why

Dolt has native disaster recovery via cron + dolt pull, but it's undocumented in the bt context. Users need to know it exists and bt should surface replication health.

## What

Two parts:
1. Document the cron + dolt pull pattern for DR replication (it's a 3-line script using native Dolt commands)
2. Show replication/sync status in the Projects scoreboard view - last sync time, replica health

Query Dolt system tables for commit history and remote state. No management commands - bt provides visibility, not ops tooling.

## Acceptance Criteria

- DR replication pattern documented
- Projects scoreboard shows last sync time and replica health
- Status derived from Dolt system tables (commit history, remote state)

title: Cross-project dependency graphs
description:
## Why

Dependencies don't stop at project boundaries. Understanding cross-project bottlenecks requires graph analysis that spans multiple databases on the shared server.

## What

Graph analysis spanning project boundaries:
- Default: per-project graphs with cross-project deps shown as external edges (highlighted differently)
- Toggle: unified supergraph across all projects for org-level bottleneck analysis

Parse bd `external:<project>:<capability>` dep syntax and resolve against shared server databases to build graph edges.

Builds on `pkg/analysis/graph.go` and `pkg/workspace/` ID resolver.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Per-project graphs show cross-project deps as visually distinct external edges
- Unified supergraph toggle available for portfolio-level analysis
- `external:<project>:<capability>` dep syntax parsed and resolved into graph edges

title: Temporal trending: timeline view
description:
## Why

Burndown charts, blocker trends, and velocity curves give a longitudinal view of project health that point-in-time snapshots can't provide. This is the capstone analytics view in the temporal trending sequence.

## What

Dedicated full-screen analytics view with:
- Burndown charts
- Blocker trends
- Velocity curves over configurable time windows

Works both per-project (default) and at portfolio level (toggle).

Benefits from sparkline (bt-ph1z.2) and diff mode (bt-ph1z.3) groundwork - build this last in the trending sequence.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Full-screen analytics view with burndown, blocker trends, and velocity curves
- Configurable time windows
- Per-project and portfolio-level toggle

title: Temporal trending: diff mode
description:
## Why

Knowing what changed since yesterday (or last week) is high-value for daily triage. Like git diff but for project state - which issues are new, which got unblocked, which stalled.

## What

Compare "now" vs "N days ago" for project state. Uses Dolt `AS OF` queries to load historical snapshots and compute deltas. Show:
- New issues since the comparison point
- Issues that got unblocked
- Issues that stalled (no updates)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Diff mode compares current state against a configurable historical point
- New, unblocked, and stalled issues are clearly identified
- Driven by Dolt `AS OF` queries against version history

title: Temporal trending: sparkline snapshots
description:
## Why

Sparklines give at-a-glance trend visibility without needing a dedicated analytics view. Lightweight way to surface whether things are improving or getting worse.

## What

Daily snapshots of key metrics (open count, blocked count, velocity) rendered as sparklines in existing views. Leverage Dolt version history via `SELECT AS OF` queries to compute historical data points.

No new views needed - slots into existing issue list and project views.

Benchmark Dolt `AS OF` performance before committing to snapshot frequency.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Sparklines for key metrics (open, blocked, velocity) visible in existing views
- Historical data derived from Dolt `AS OF` queries
- Performance benchmarked to determine viable snapshot frequency

title: Portfolio health scoreboard
description:
## Why

With multiple projects on the shared Dolt server, there's no single view that shows portfolio-level health. You have to drill into each project individually.

## What

Cross-project scoreboard as the default Projects view. Each project row shows:
- Open/blocked/closed counts
- Velocity trend arrow
- Health color

Drill into any project for full graph analysis (betweenness, critical path, cycles). Progressive disclosure - quick scan at portfolio level, deep analysis per project.

Builds on existing global Dolt mode data pipeline and per-project analytics engine.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Projects view shows a scoreboard with per-project health metrics
- Each row includes open/blocked/closed counts, velocity trend, and health indicator
- Drill-down into any project for detailed graph analysis

title: Workspace stack vision: bt + tpane + cnvs as cohesive developer workspace
description:
## Why

Three tools are being built in parallel that form a natural workspace stack: bt (issue tracking TUI), tpane (terminal layout manager), cnvs (window/canvas manager). Together they could be the spatial/visual organization layer for developer workflows - like a lazygit-level toolkit but for the full workspace. No design doc or epic connects them yet.

## What

Brainstorm and explore:
- Shared conventions across bt, tpane, and cnvs
- Integration points (tpane launching bt, bt surfacing tpane layouts, cnvs arranging them spatially)
- Whether they should share infrastructure (theme system, keybindings, Charm components)

Related Obsidian note: `00.inbox/soliloquy 2026-04-10 cross-project beads and session awareness.md`. Related to bt-z9ei (lazydev vision).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Integration points between bt, tpane, and cnvs identified
- Decision on shared vs independent infrastructure (themes, keybindings, components)
- Design doc or epic connecting the three tools if the vision holds up
notes: Obsidian note captured: 00.inbox/soliloquy 2026-04-10 cross-project beads and session awareness.md. Related to bt-z9ei (lazydev vision).

title: Same-ID cross-prefix bead linking in global view
description:
## Why

When bd-123 exists upstream and bt-123 is created locally as a mirror/correspondent, bt global view should surface this relationship. This enables navigating between canonical upstream issues and local project-specific tracking.

## What

In the global view, detect and link beads that share the same ID suffix but have different prefixes (e.g. bd-123 and bt-123). Enable sorting, grouping, and navigating between linked beads.

Depends on upstream beads same-ID-different-prefix linking feature (bd-k8b).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Global view surfaces same-ID cross-prefix relationships
- Linked beads can be sorted and grouped together
- Navigation between linked beads is supported

comments.author: sms
comments.text:
Backend landed 2026-04-20 via bt-mhwy.2 (`bt robot pairs --global`). TUI consumers can call `view.ComputePairRecords(issues) []view.PairRecord` directly вҖ” no need to shell out to `bd` or the robot CLI. PairRecord carries `{suffix, canonical, mirrors, drift?}`; see `pkg/view/pair_record.go` for the shape.

Caveat before consuming v1 naively: the current identity rule is pure suffix matching, which produces ~5Г— false positives on the real dataset (24 of 29 pairs fire `title` drift because most "pairs" are unrelated work with coincidental suffix collisions). If the TUI surfaces all detected pairs as meaningful relationships, users will see noise. Options for .6cfg implementation:

1. Wait for bt-gkyn (pairs v2: intent-based identity) before building TUI affordances on top.
2. Build on v1 but filter aggressively (e.g. only show pairs where titles are close, or where both sides are open and referenced in each other's dependencies).
3. Show all pairs but visually de-emphasize suffix-only collisions.

Upstream bd-k8b dep noted in the description is still open (as of 2026-04-20) вҖ” that's likely where canonical identity-marker support would land if beads proper adds a `paired_with` field.

title: Display externally-filed beads differently in TUI
description:
## Why

When beads are filed by a session from another project (cross-project bead filing), there's no visual distinction in the TUI. Users can't tell which beads originated locally vs externally.

## What

Display externally-filed beads with visual distinction - different badge, border color, or icon. Depends on upstream beads supporting provenance metadata (source session, source repo). Part of the cross-project awareness vision (bt+tpane+cnvs workspace stack).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Beads filed from external projects render with distinct visual treatment (badge, border, or icon)
- Local beads remain visually unchanged
- Provenance metadata from upstream beads is consumed when available

title: Dolt e2e test infrastructure: test against real Dolt, not just JSONL fixtures
description:
## Why

The e2e test suite uses JSONL fixtures, which covers TUI/CLI behavior but not the production data path. Dolt is now the primary (and for new projects, only) backend - the Dolt integration path has no e2e coverage.

## What

Build e2e test infrastructure that runs against a real ephemeral Dolt instance. Needs: test helpers for Dolt lifecycle (start/stop ephemeral server), SQL fixture loading, port management, and cleanup.

## Scope

This is infrastructure, not individual test cases. The goal is test helpers and a working example test that proves the setup. Individual Dolt-path e2e tests build on top of this.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Test helper can start/stop an ephemeral Dolt server for a test run
- SQL fixtures can be loaded into the ephemeral instance
- Port management avoids conflicts with running Dolt servers
- At least one e2e test exercises the full Dolt data path (load -> display)
- Cleanup is automatic (no leaked Dolt processes after test failures)

title: Document hook security model in README
description:
## Why

pkg/hooks/executor.go executes .bt/hooks.yaml commands via sh -c without sandboxing. This is by-design (same threat model as git hooks) but undocumented - users should know the security implications before trusting .bt/hooks.yaml in unfamiliar repos.

## What

Add a security note to README covering the hook execution model. Reference: security/260409-2034-stride-owasp-full-audit/findings.md Finding 2.

## Acceptance Criteria

- README documents that hooks can execute arbitrary commands
- Explains the threat model (same as git hooks - trust the repo author)
- Advises users to review .bt/hooks.yaml in untrusted repos

title: Theming: improve and document theme system
description:
## Why

The theme system exists and works (pkg/ui/theme_loader.go, defaults/theme.yaml, user ~/.config/bt/theme.yaml, project .bt/theme.yaml) with Tomorrow Night/Day palettes and layered merge. But it's undocumented, has limited presets, and hasn't been polished since it was wired in session 7.

## What

Improve and document the theme system. Potential areas:
1. More theme presets beyond Tomorrow Night/Day
2. Documentation on how to customize themes
3. Theme picker in the TUI
4. Better integration with terminal color schemes
5. Colorblind-friendly options

## Acceptance Criteria

- At least one of the above areas is addressed (scope TBD)
- Theme customization is documented for end users

title: Recipes: underexposed - need better discoverability and onboarding
description:
## Why

Recipes are powerful (11 built-in presets) but the UX undersells them. They're buried behind ' in TUI and -r on CLI - most users won't discover them.

## What

Improve recipe discoverability and onboarding. Known issues:
1. **Discoverability** - buried behind ' and -r, most users won't find them
2. **Active recipe not indicated** - picker shows no visual mark for which recipe is currently active
3. **Full-screen feel** - modal blanks the background instead of overlaying (no live preview as you navigate)
4. **No create/edit** - recipes are customizable via YAML but there's no in-TUI way to create or modify them
5. **No mouse support** - tracked separately in bt-fbx6

## Scope

Related: bt-sytt (recipes as saved BQL), bt-xavk (help system), bt-fbx6 (mouse in picker).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Recipe discovery path is more prominent (exact approach TBD)
- Active recipe is visually indicated in the picker

title: Recipes: no mouse support in picker modal
description:
## Bug

The recipe picker (' key) has no mouse click handlers. Can't click a recipe to select or apply it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Mouse clicks in the recipe picker select and apply the clicked recipe
- Consistent with broader mouse support effort (bt-166w)

title: Shortcuts bar (;): layout broken, overlaps with underlying view
description:
## Bug

The ; shortcuts bar renders a help popup that overlaps with the underlying view panels (seen over insights). Text clips and misaligns with panel borders. The popup doesn't size or position itself relative to the content underneath.

## Root Cause

No proper modal rendering - the overlay lacks background dimming or dedicated space allocation. Needs to render as a true modal or occupy its own reserved region.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Shortcuts bar renders without overlapping or clipping underlying view content
- Text aligns properly within the popup bounds
- Popup either dims background (modal) or reserves dedicated space

Related: bt-xavk (help system redesign).

title: Attention view (]): barebones table, needs visual redesign
description:
## Why

The attention view (`]` key) shows a raw unstyled table (Rank/Label/Attention/Reason) with 10 rows on a full-screen terminal. 90% empty space. The underlying data is valuable - which labels have blocked/stale issues that need action - but the presentation doesn't match the quality of other views.

## What

Redesign the attention view for density and visual quality:
- Use screen space effectively (not 10 rows on a 40-line terminal)
- Visual treatment should match other views (consider bar charts like flow matrix, or card-style layout)
- Surface why each label needs attention (blocked count, stale count, oldest issue age)
- Enable drill-down into a label's issues (or scope as follow-up)

## Scope

This is strictly the attention ranking view (`]` key). The label dashboard (`[` key) scoping bug is tracked separately in bt-rhqs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Attention view fills available screen space proportionally
- Each label row shows actionable detail (not just a score)
- Visual treatment is consistent with flow matrix and insights panel quality

title: Actionable view (a): wasteful layout, needs density rethink
description:
## Bug

Each 'TRACK' in the actionable view takes ~4 lines to display one bead (colored banner + 'Single actionable item' subtitle + bead + gap). On a 27" monitor only ~10 tracks visible. Most tracks contain a single item, so the track grouping adds no value for those cases.

The recommendation header ('Start with bt-ssk7 - Unblocks 3') is useful. The rest needs compression.

Also has the global scoping bug (bt-dcby) - shows 2207 issues when filtered to a single project.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Single-item tracks don't waste 4 lines of vertical space
- Information density is significantly higher (more beads visible without scrolling)
- Recommendation header is preserved

title: Surface high-connectivity beads: recipe or sort for 'most blocking' / 'most deps'
description:
## Why

No easy way to find beads with the richest dependency trees. The insights panel computes graph metrics (PageRank, betweenness, blocker count) but you can't navigate to those beads quickly.

## What

Surface high-connectivity beads through one or more of:
1. Recipe/preset for 'bottlenecks' sorted by blocking count
2. BQL ORDER BY blocks/deps
3. Sort option in list view (s key cycles?)
4. Graph view search to jump to high-connectivity nodes

Note: the triage sort (S) already exists but uses a different scoring model.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Users can discover which beads are most-blocking or have the most dependencies without manually inspecting the graph
- At least one navigation path to high-connectivity beads exists

title: Board view: G goes to bottom but no keybind to return to top
description:
## Bug

In board view, G scrolls to the bottom of the column but there's no keybind to return to the top. The help overlay shows G for bottom but nothing for 'go to top'. Expected vim-style gg or 0 but neither appears to be wired.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A keybind returns to the top of the column in board view (gg or similar vim convention)
- Help overlay documents the go-to-top keybind alongside the existing G (go-to-bottom)

title: Board view: status bar flickers/hides on poll refresh
description: In board view, each Dolt poll cycle appears to overwrite the status bar footer briefly, hiding the key hints. The transient reload status message is clobbering the board footer layout.
notes: VISION CONTEXT (2026-04-09): Same class of bug as bt-y0k7. Both are blockers for bt-spzz (smarter reload status). The flicker needs to be fixed before investing in better reload messages. In global mode this fires more often (polling across 13 databases), making it more disruptive.

title: Flow matrix: keyboard input conflicts with navigation - can't type to search/filter
description:
## Bug

In the dependency flow view (f), trying to type to search/filter labels triggers navigation keybinds (j/k/etc.) instead of entering text input. No way to filter the 318-label list by typing.

The view also operates on the full global dataset when it should respect the activeRepos filter.

## Root Cause

Keyboard input is routed to navigation handlers with no mode switch for text input/search.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Users can type to search/filter labels in the flow view without triggering navigation
- Flow view respects activeRepos filter (or this is tracked separately)

title: History view: 'Showing N/M' denominator uses global count when filtered to project
description:
## Bug

When cd'd into a project (e.g. updoots), the history view shows 'Showing 151/2189 beads' - the 2189 denominator is the global issue count, not the project count. Should show 151/631 (or whatever the actual project issue count is).

## Root Cause

Denominator pulls from the unfiltered global issue set instead of respecting the activeRepos project filter. Part of the broader global scoping bug (bt-dcby).

## Acceptance Criteria

- History view denominator reflects the filtered project count when a project filter is active
- Global mode (no filter) still shows the true global count

title: Board view: poor use of screen space with empty columns
description:
## Why

On wide monitors (27"+), the board shows 4 equal-width columns even when some are empty (e.g. 0 in_progress, 0 blocked). This wastes half the screen on empty space.

## What

Improve board layout to handle empty columns. Options to explore: collapse empty columns, auto-hide them, flex width based on content count, responsive layout.

Depends on bt-lgbz (card expand broken when empty columns visible). See bt-714y for related project picker visual refresh.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Empty columns don't consume equal screen space as populated columns
- Board layout adapts to the actual distribution of issues across statuses

title: History view doesn't work in global mode (no git repo context)
description:
## Why

History view requires a git repo path from cwd. In global mode (especially from ~ or a non-project directory), there's no git context. Even filtering to one project via the picker doesn't help because bt doesn't know the project's filesystem path.

## What

Make history view work in global mode. Needs exploration: could we map project DB names to filesystem paths (via shared server's Dolt data dir)? Or store project paths in beads metadata?

## Acceptance Criteria

- History view is functional when bt is launched outside a git repo
- Or: history view gracefully indicates why it's unavailable and what the user can do

title: Project picker modal needs visual refresh (rounded corners, issue counts)
description:
## Why

The project picker modal (w key in global mode) uses sharp corners while the rest of the TUI uses RoundedBorder. It also shows no metadata - just a plain checklist with [x] markers.

## What

Visual refresh of the project picker modal:
- Border should use RoundedBorder to match panel.go convention
- Show project name + open issue count per project
- Improve visual hierarchy (color coding, better layout)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Project picker uses RoundedBorder consistent with other panels
- Each project entry shows its open issue count
- Visual style is consistent with other modal overlays in bt
close_reason: Subsumed by bt-s4b7 project navigation redesign. Rounded corners, title-in-border, centered layout all shipped (2026-04-14).

title: Investigate beads ecosystem (gastown, wasteland, gascity) for bt integration opportunities
description:
## Why

The beads ecosystem includes adjacent tools (gastown, wasteland, gascity) that may overlap with or complement bt's functionality. Understanding the landscape prevents building things that already exist or conflict with upstream direction.

## What

Audit the beads ecosystem projects and identify integration opportunities for bt:
- **gastown** (gt): multi-agent workspace manager with TUI (gt feed - 3-panel: agent tree, convoy, event stream). Orchestrates via tmux. v1.0.0.
- **wasteland**: federated network of Gas Towns via DoltHub. Wanted boards, task claiming, reputation stamps.
- **gascity**: orchestration-builder SDK. Enterprise extension.

Potential crossover areas:
1. Agent activity in bt - which agent is working on which bead (gastown knows this)
2. Cross-project coordination - wasteland's wanted board parallels global view
3. Orchestration - gascity SDK as future delegation layer (vs shelling out to bd)
4. Federation - wasteland uses DoltHub for cross-town sync, same infra bt uses
5. gt feed TUI patterns - what can bt learn from their TUI design

Refs:
- https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown
- https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity
- https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-the-wasteland-a-thousand-gas-towns-a5eb9bc8dc1f
- gastown WASTELAND.md: https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown/blob/main/docs/WASTELAND.md

## Acceptance Criteria

- Audit of each ecosystem project completed (what it does, what's relevant to bt)
- Clear list of integration opportunities vs things to avoid duplicating
- Decision on which (if any) integrations to pursue
notes:
ECOSYSTEM OVERVIEW (2026-04-08):
- beads (bd): work tracking backend, Dolt-backed. bt's data source.
- gastown (gt): multi-agent workspace manager. TUI via 'gt feed' (3-panel: agent tree, convoy, event stream). Orchestrates via tmux. v1.0.0.
- wasteland: federated network of Gas Towns via DoltHub. Wanted boards, task claiming, reputation stamps.
- gascity: orchestration-builder SDK. Enterprise extension.

POTENTIAL CROSSOVER:
1. Agent activity in bt: which agent is working on which bead (gastown knows this)
2. Cross-project coordination: wasteland's wanted board parallels sms-ncb global view
3. Orchestration: gascity SDK as future delegation layer (vs shelling out to bd)
4. Federation: wasteland uses DoltHub for cross-town sync - same infra bt uses for shared server
5. gt feed TUI patterns: what can bt learn from their TUI design (Charm-based?)

NOT URGENT: This is exploratory. Current bt development doesn't depend on ecosystem integration. But understanding the landscape prevents building things that already exist or conflict with upstream direction.

REFS:
- https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown
- https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity
- https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-the-wasteland-a-thousand-gas-towns-a5eb9bc8dc1f
- gastown WASTELAND.md: https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown/blob/main/docs/WASTELAND.md
close_reason: Duplicate of bt-8qd1 which covers the same Gas Town/Gas City/Wasteland investigation with more detail and higher priority.

title: Investigate events-based polling as optimization over MAX(updated_at)
description:
## Why

bt currently polls MAX(updated_at) across all databases every 3 seconds to detect changes. This is binary (something changed vs nothing changed) with no granularity on what changed, triggering a full data reload every time.

The beads codebase exposes GetAllEventsSince(ctx, time.Time) in dolt/events.go which returns all events since a timestamp across events + wisp_events tables. This could enable partial reloads instead of full data reload on every change.

## What

Investigate whether events-based polling is a viable optimization:
- Current: globalDoltPollOnce() at pkg/ui/background_worker.go:2086-2115, polls every 3s (BT_DOLT_POLL_INTERVAL_S), compares MAX(updated_at) across all databases, triggers full reload on change
- Potential: use events table to know WHAT changed, reload only affected issues/databases

Assumptions to verify:
1. Does GetAllEventsSince work across databases or is it single-database?
2. What's in the events table schema? What event types exist?
3. Is the events table on the shared server or per-project?
4. Performance: is querying events table faster than MAX(updated_at) UNION ALL at scale?
5. Could bt do partial reloads (only reload changed issues)?
6. Does the wisp_events table exist on all databases or just some?

## Acceptance Criteria

- Investigation session completed with findings documented
- Clear recommendation on whether to adopt events-based polling
- If yes: implementation plan with migration path from current polling
notes:
CONTEXT FOR INVESTIGATING SESSION:

CURRENT STATE (bt side):
- globalDoltPollOnce() at pkg/ui/background_worker.go:2086-2115
- Polls every 3s (BT_DOLT_POLL_INTERVAL_S), compares MAX(updated_at) across all databases
- On change: triggers full data reload via TriggerRefresh() -> LoadFromSource()
- Single timestamp comparison - binary yes/no, no granularity on what changed
- Works fine at current scale (13 databases, ~2k total issues)

BEADS SIDE (from beads codebase audit):
- GetAllEventsSince(ctx, time.Time) in dolt/events.go
- Returns events from events + wisp_events tables ordered by creation time
- Events table tracks individual mutations with timestamps
- updated_at is indexed on issues table (DATETIME NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)
- Metadata table stores sync state (used by federation for last_sync_<peer> timestamps) - bt could use same pattern for last_polled_at per database

ASSUMPTIONS TO VERIFY:
1. Does GetAllEventsSince work across databases? Or is it single-database? (Likely single-db - would need per-db calls)
2. What's in the events table schema? What event types exist?
3. Is the events table on the shared server or per-project?
4. Performance: is querying events table faster than MAX(updated_at) UNION ALL at scale?
5. Could bt do partial reloads (only reload changed issues) instead of full reload?
6. Does the wisp_events table exist on all databases or just some?

OPTIMIZATION OPPORTUNITIES:
- Partial reload: only fetch changed issues instead of full UNION ALL
- Per-database tracking: know which database changed, only reload that one
- Event-driven: subscribe to changes instead of polling (if Dolt supports it)
- Reduce poll frequency if using events (events give exact changes, don't need frequent checks)

NOT URGENT: Current polling works. This is a scale/efficiency optimization for when issue counts grow or poll overhead becomes noticeable.

title: Research: inter-session messaging for Claude Code
description:
## Why

Sessions need to communicate directly - not just async search (CASS) but real-time coordination. Example: "hey beads session, I just changed the schema." CASS already knows which sessions are active. This is a prerequisite for the bt Surface layer vision (see soliloquy 2026-04-03 bt surface layer.md).

## What

Research existing implementations and decide build vs adapt for inter-session messaging. Key constraints: must work on Windows, compose with CASS, no extra server if possible.

Approaches to evaluate:
- File-based mailbox
- MCP broker
- Named pipes
- Other patterns others have built

## Acceptance Criteria

- Survey of viable approaches documented with tradeoffs
- Recommendation on build vs adapt with rationale
- Windows + CASS compatibility confirmed for recommended approach
notes:
Research from .obs session 2026-04-03: Three agent communication repos in s07 GitHub stars: (1) Dicklesworthstone/mcp_agent_mail - async coordination via FastMCP + Git + SQLite, inboxes and threads, purpose-built. (2) ShawnPana/smux - tmux-based agent-to-agent comm, Linux only, won't work on Windows Terminal. (3) parcadei/Continuous-Claude-v3 - hooks/ledgers/handoffs for context management, more about context than messaging. Plus gastownhall/gastown (own project) and CASS (search/read but not messaging). For Windows + WT + tpane setup, solution needs to be file-based or MCP-based, compose with CASS. mcp_agent_mail or building on CASS are most viable paths.
Gastown communication (from March 27 research): gt has built-in mailboxes, NOT mcp_agent_mail. Three mechanisms: (1) gt seance - session discovery, query predecessors for context/decisions. (2) Built-in mailboxes - agent identities with persistent inboxes and handoffs, in the gt binary. (3) Convoys - work bundles assigned to agents with progress tracking. Open question: can gt mailbox + seance be used standalone without full Mayor/Town/Rig architecture? It's a Go binary, Go is already installed. User tried mcp_agent_mail separately and found it clunky.

title: Global beads: cross-project BQL with SQL executor
description: bt's BQL currently uses MemoryExecutor (in-memory filtering). For global beads (205+ issues across 9+ projects), need a DoltExecutor that pushes BQL-generated WHERE clauses to embedded Dolt instances. Implements the Executor interface already defined in pkg/bql/executor.go. Depends on: auto-discovery of .beads/ projects (no upstream mechanism - bt owns this). See brainstorm future integration section.
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: GlobalDoltReader, source type integration, poll loop, --global flag. All 27 test packages pass.

title: Evolve recipes into saved BQL queries
description:
## Why

Currently recipes are YAML filter presets (status/priority/tags/actionable + sort). Migrating to BQL expressions would unify the filtering model and make recipes more powerful.

## What

Migrate recipe format so the filter is a BQL expression string. Recipe picker would show saved BQL queries. Requires rethinking recipe loading in pkg/recipe/.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Recipe format stores a BQL expression string instead of YAML filter fields
- Recipe picker displays saved BQL queries
- Existing recipe loading in pkg/recipe/ updated to handle new format

title: BQL syntax highlighting in query modal
description:
## Why

The `:` query modal input is plain text. Syntax highlighting would make BQL queries easier to read and compose.

## What

Adapt perles styles.go and syntax_adapter.go for bt's theme system. Highlight field names, operators, keywords, and values with distinct colors in the `:` modal input.

## Scope

Low priority - cosmetic improvement.

## Acceptance Criteria

- BQL field names, operators, keywords, and values render in distinct colors in the query modal

title: Prompt management panel in bt
description:
## Why

Agent actions triggered from the TUI would reduce context-switching. User-defined prompts bound to keys or a command palette - like lazygit custom commands but for agent actions - with project context auto-injected.

## What

Add a prompt library/runner to bt. Data model TBD: per-project in .bt/prompts/, global in ~/.config/bt/, or both.

## Acceptance Criteria

- User can define and run prompts from within bt
- Prompts support key bindings or command palette access
- Project context is auto-injected into prompts

title: Investigate x/export keybind - unclear UX, dumps all beads to project root
description:
## Why

Dogfooding finding: pressing 'x' exports the entire beads catalog (312 issues) to a markdown file at the project root (beads_report_portfolio_2026-03-25.md). The behavior is surprising and the use case is unclear.

## What

Investigate the export keybind and decide whether to redesign or remove it. Current issues:

1. **No confirmation dialog** - fires immediately, status bar shows result but it's easy to miss
2. **Exports everything** - dumps all 312 issues, not just the selected one or filtered set. Selecting a bead and pressing 'x' should logically export that bead, not the whole catalog
3. **Dumps to project root** - pollutes the repo with a generated file. Should go to a configurable location, or at least not the repo root
4. **Unclear use case** - who is this for? If it's for sharing/reporting, it should be more intentional (confirmation, scope selection, output path). If it's for LLM context, there might be better formats

## Acceptance Criteria

- Original intent documented
- Decision made: redesign (with specific UX improvements) or remove
- If redesigned: confirmation dialog, scope selection, configurable output path

title: Switch default search mode to semantic
description:
## Why

Dogfooding finding: fuzzy search is the current default, semantic requires Ctrl+S toggle. In practice, when searching in the TUI you're usually looking for a specific bead ID or a known concept - semantic handles both better. Fuzzy search is more useful for LLM sessions (which do their own fuzzy matching).

## What

Make semantic search the default mode, fuzzy the toggle. Example: searching 'iaod' in fuzzy mode doesn't surface portfolio-iaod near the top, but semantic mode puts it first.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Semantic search is the default search mode
- Fuzzy search available via Ctrl+S toggle (or equivalent)

title: Document what repo picker (w) does and whether it's useful
description:
## Why

Dogfooding finding: user pressed w (repo picker) and had no idea what it does or why it exists. The keybinding needs either better discoverability/explanation, or evaluation of whether it earns its keybinding.

## What

Document what the repo picker (w) does, investigate its purpose, and decide whether it should be kept, redesigned, or removed. Part of bt-ty44 view exploration.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Repo picker purpose and behavior documented
- Decision made: keep (with UX improvements), redesign, or remove
close_reason: Subsumed by bt-s4b7 project navigation redesign. Picker was redesigned with rounded borders, overlay compositing, toggle-all, and enter-on-none behavior (2026-04-14).

title: Rethink 'Issues' panel title border - could show repo name or column headers
description:
## Why

The lazygit-style border with 'Issues' inlined in the top border takes prime real estate. The word 'Issues' is obvious from context and the space could communicate more useful information.

## What

Explore alternatives for the panel title border. Questions:

1. Could that space show the repo/project name instead? Important for side-pane use where you might forget which project you're looking at
2. Could column headers (TYPE PRI STATUS ID TITLE) live there instead of taking a full row?
3. Would any of these look bad with the border line running through them?

Related to bt-46fa (column header redesign) but this is about the panel border itself.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Alternatives evaluated with visual mockups or prototypes
- Decision on what to show in the panel border (repo name, column headers, or keep 'Issues')

title: Labels: better visual display + explore as navigation/filter mechanism
description:
## Why

Two related observations from dogfooding: (1) Labels in the details pane are plain text ('Labels: area:tui, ux') - could be styled as colored tags/pills for visual impact. (2) Labels could serve as a navigation mechanism - select a label to filter the issue list, making labels a way to explore and organize. Currently labels are underutilized partly due to beads config (not setting them consistently) but also because the TUI doesn't make them visually prominent or interactive.

## What

Better visual display for labels plus exploration of labels as a navigation/filter mechanism:

- Style labels as colored tags/pills in the details pane
- Make labels selectable in the details pane to trigger label filtering
- Polish the existing 'l' keybind label filter dialog (rounded borders, inline header - same lazygit-style treatment as other overlays)

The 'l' keybind already opens a 'filter by labels' dialog - this is the foundation for the labels-as-navigation feature.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Labels render as styled colored pills in the details pane
- Selecting a label in the details pane filters the issue list by that label
- Label filter dialog has consistent visual polish with other overlays
notes: Dogfooding update (2026-03-25): The 'l' keybind already opens a 'filter by labels' dialog - this is a great starting point for the labels-as-navigation feature. The dialog itself needs visual polish (rounded borders, inline header - same lazygit-style treatment as other overlays). But functionally, the filter-by-label mechanism is already there. This should be the foundation for making labels more prominent: style the dialog, make labels clickable/selectable in the details pane to trigger the same filter, and give labels colored pill rendering.

title: Revisit TYPE column icons - unclear what they mean
description:
## Why

Dogfooding finding: the TYPE icons in the issue list (rockets, stars, etc.) aren't self-explanatory. User couldn't identify what rockets mean (epics?). This matters more as the narrow view is compressed - icons need to communicate type without text labels.

## What

Revisit the TYPE column icon set. Options:

1. Make icons more intuitive (choose universally recognizable symbols)
2. Add a legend somewhere (help overlay, status bar, or tooltip)
3. Rethink the icon set entirely

## Acceptance Criteria

- TYPE icons are identifiable without prior knowledge, or a discoverable legend exists

title: Show Dolt server shutdown message on bt exit
description: When bt auto-starts a Dolt server on launch (shows 'Starting Dolt server...'), quitting bt should print a corresponding message on exit. Two cases: (1) bt owns the server and stops it: 'Stopped Dolt server.' (2) bt didn't stop it because another process is keeping it open: 'Dolt server still running (started by another process)' or similar. User should always know the state of background processes on exit.
close_reason: Closed

title: Normalize golden file comparison for line endings
description: 2 tests in pkg/export fail due to line ending differences (CRLF vs LF). Fix: strip carriage returns before comparing golden files, or use platform-aware comparison. Affects TestGraphExport_GoldenMermaid and TestGraphRender_GoldenSVG.
